## Welcome To the SB TAX PLANNING and FILING GUIDE

Welcome to a guide that we know will save you LOTS of money and headaches! Because tax planning truly is two different types of actions (one largely strategic and the other tactical) , this guide is also divided into two halves: Tax Planning (the strategic side) , and Tax Prep & Filing (the tactical side) . At the end of the guide , there is also a small checklist for year end tax moves , with explanations . While this is a HUGE time and money saving checklist , and a big part of your planning process , this is a small subsection of our overall guide .

So , welcome to our tax guide . For those of you who don ' t know us , I ' m Joe Saul -Sehy . He ' s Kevin Bailey . (Kevin says " Hello! " ) . First , neither of us are tax professionals . We are both guys who ' ve worked in the financial industry for collectively over 50 years . We ' ve also received help on this guide from my podcast partner OG , who is a working CFP. P. Additionally, y, before we sent this out , we ' ve sent it to influencers in the tax space , many of whom are enrolled agents , CPAs , and tax attorneys . You ' ve heard many of them on our podcast and our YouTube channel .

That said , we need to begin with an important disclaimer: this guide will NOT provide specific tax advice . You should consult professional help for your individual tax questions . In fact , we show you how to do that in this section (link) of the guide .

Let 's 's begin!

## COMPLETE THESE STEPS FIRST!

Ready to stack some quick Benjamins? Let ' s grab you the best return on investment right away on this guide , shall we? Want to learn tax strategies?

Sure , we ' ll cover LOADS of them in these pages . But , right upfront , let ' s cut to saving you money quickly and giving you better tax options .

Disclaimer: Before locking in any tax moves, we recommend working with a tax professional . This guide is going to help you educate yourself on WHAT to ask and HOW to find opportunities…but we always recommend deferring to the experts in this area , especially if you 'r 're just starting out.

section_heading: ## Quick Tax To-Do List: Maximize Your Refund, Minimize Your Stress
text:
## Quick Tax To-Do List: Maximize Your Refund, Minimize Your Stress

Stackers , here ' s your shortcut to making the most of tax season . These 20 quick actions will help you save money, y, streamline your process , and ensure you make back what you paid for this guide — and then some!

## Tax Planning To-Do ' s

- Timeline Your Financial Goals: Match your tax-advantaged accounts (401(k) , Roth IRA , HSA , 529) to your short- , medium - , and long-term goals .

- Max Out Tax-Advantaged Accounts: Contribute to your 401(k) , IRA , or HSA before deadlines to lower your taxable income .

- Harvest Investment Losses: Have any losses in your non-IRA portfolio? Sell losing investments to offset gains and reduce your taxable income (if this “ loser ” no longer fits your investment strategy) .

- Check Withholding: Use the IRS Withholding Estimator to ensure you ' re not over- or underpaying taxes throughout the year .

- Claim All Deductions and Credits: Use tax software (see Section 5 of Tax Prep and Filing for our comprehensive list of options , including strengths and weaknesses) or a professional to identify every deduction and credit you qualify for, r, like the Child Tax Credit or education credits .

- Look for HSA Opportunities: Are you eligible for an HSA? Explore how signing up for and using an HSA might help you avoid taxes on retirement saving.

- Contribute to a 529 Plan: Reduce your state taxes (if applicable) while saving for education .

- Adjust Business Expenses: If self-employed , ensure you ’ re deducting home office , mileage , and other eligible expenses .

- Protect Against AMT: Check if your income level triggers the Alternative Minimum Tax and plan accordingly .

- Track Major Life Events: Were you married this year? Divorced? Have a child? Buy a home? All of these can unlock new tax benefits .

## Tax Preparation To-Do ' s

- Gather All Forms and Records: Collect W - 2s , 1099s , 1098s , K- K- 1s , receipts for deductions , and prior-year returns .

- Track Charitable Donations: Have receipts for all cash and non-cash donations to maximize your deductions .

- Organize Investment Details: Gather records for all trades , dividends , and interest to avoid IRS scrutiny .

- Know Your Deadlines: Mark the calendar for estimated payment dates (if applicable) , April 15 for filing, and October 15 if you file an extension .

## Tax Filing To-Do ' s

- File Electronically: Use trusted tax software or IRS Free File (if you ’ re eligible and also not needing help on state or local tax forms) for faster processing and fewer errors .

- Double - Check Before Filing: Review Social Security numbers , bank details , and forms to avoid typos or missed deductions .

- Set Up Direct Deposit: Get your refund faster by providing your bank account details .

- Respond Quickly to IRS Notices: If you receive correspondence , read it carefully and follow instructions promptly .

- Running late? Grab an Extension: File Form 4868 for more time to prepare (but still pay any owed taxes by April 15) .

- Avoid Scams: Only work with reputable tax professionals , and never give sensitive info over email or text .

## The Big Takeaway

Tax season doesn ' t have to be a headache—or a hit to your wallet . With this quick list , you ' ll save money, y, stress less , and keep stacking those Benjamins like the pro you are!

section_heading: ## BAM! You Did It . Let ' s Start Planning!
text:
## BAM! You Did It . Let ' s Start Planning!

Now, w, let 's 's get prepped to dive in and truly win by understanding ALL of your tax options . Here 's 's what you ' ll need to start , if you don ' t already have these things prepped from the checklist above:

1. Grab pens , paper, r, a to-do list or a calendar to schedule out all of the things you ' ll need to investigate .
2. Print out last year ' s tax return AND any important tax documents you wish you knew better . We ' ll cover them all in this guide .
3. Print out your year -end paycheck from last year . Not only will this show you the tax that you withheld , but also will show you LOTS of payroll deductions . While you ' re at it , you may wish to ask about those as well .
4. If you ' re married or in a committed relationship, grab your spouse/partner ' s tax returns , last paycheck of the year, r, and any other tax-related stuff they have questions about . The more you gather now, w, the less you ' ll need to hunt down later!

section_heading: ## Why Both Tax Prep AND Tax Planning Matter
text:
## Why Both Tax Prep AND Tax Planning Matter

These two tasks are inherently different when it comes to winning the tax game , and each has a different goal .

Tax planning allows you to relax , knowing that you have set yourself up for the best future outcome , tax-wise . This is easily the most important part of tax planning, and the most frequently overlooked . An offshoot of this is year-end tax planning, which is a series of tactical moves that will help you maximize whatever time-sensitive decisions you need to make either before the end of the calendar year, r, or the tax year (often April 15 … but for some time -sensitive strategies , other dates like March 15 .

Tax Prep and Filing is important in the moment . How do we present to the IRS our best foot forward , given the pieces of the puzzle that we ' ve completed over the prior year . Have we claimed all of the tax breaks we ' re eligible to receive? Am I using the right software? Am I filing all of the correct paperwork? This is important TODAY, Y, but is far more effective if you ' ve done some tax planning ahead of time .

section_heading: ## How we think about the two halves of this guide … and why tax planning is so important .
text:
## How we think about the two halves of this guide … and why tax planning is so important .

Imagine taxes like it ' s a jigsaw puzzle . Tax preparation and filing assumes you already have all of the pieces on the table and you ' re placing every piece in the optimal spot . Tax planning helps you decide which jigsaw puzzle is best for this moment ahead of time . That way , even before the pieces are on the table , the job of tax preparation and filing is both easier and more congruent with your financial goals .

## Job One: Tax Planning

## SECTION 1: The Basics

The big picture? What 99 . 9% of us want is tax flexibility .

## How does your tax flexibility look now?

Let ' s make this clear: too many people focus on TAX OPTIMIZATION instead of TAX FLEXIBILITY. Y. What ' s the difference?

Tax optimization is the act of figuring out what the best move to lower your taxes today . It assumes that everything tomorrow is going to look the same as it does today, y, which is simply not true . Tax optimization fails because it locks you into today ' s tax " game " and also doesn ' t help you contain your tax bill tomorrow .

## Here ' s what ' s coming:

- Tax rates will change
- You will change tax brackets
- You may change your number of dependents
- You may own a business in the future (or sell one)

## … or other countless changes

This is why our bias in this guide is NOT tax optimization . Our goal is tax flexibility . The idea behind tax flexibility is that we can lower our taxes today AND leave ourselves room to lower our taxes tomorrow if the BEST happens and we end up super wealthy!

section_heading: ## SECTION 2: Understanding and Maximizing Marginal Tax Brackets
text:
## SECTION 2: Understanding and Maximizing Marginal Tax Brackets

If you 'r 're going to understand anything in this guide, e, you need to grasp this one fundamental concept.

The most common misconception about income tax is that by earning more and passing into a higher tax bracket , all of your income is bumped up to a higher tax bracket . As mom would always tell me when I told her that I was going out for the weekend with my buddies , "I "I got some news for you! "

section_heading: ## Say hello to marginal tax brackets (a pizza party metaphor)
text:
## Say hello to marginal tax brackets (a pizza party metaphor)

Okay, y, imagine you ' re at a pizza party . There ' s a rule that the more slices you eat , the more you have to pay for each extra slice . Here ' s how it works:

Your first slice costs just $1 . (Super cheap!) If you want a second slice , it costs $2 . A third slice? That one is $3 .

And so on—the price for each slice gets a little higher as you eat more .

Now, w, let ' s say you eat three slices of pizza . You don ' t pay $3 for every slice . Instead , you pay: $1 for the first slice; $2 for the second slice; $3 for the third slice

So , your total cost is $1 + $2 + $3 = $6.

Graduated tax brackets work the same way! Instead of pizza slices , it ' s about how much money you make . Your first chunk of money (let ' s say $10 , 000) is taxed at a low rate . The next chunk (maybe $10 , 001 to $20 , 000) is taxed at a slightly higher rate . Only the money in each chunk gets taxed at its own rate — just like each slice has its own price .

## Different People/Different Brackets

Single people are subject to a similar but different tax rate structure than married people . Generally, y, there is a " marriage penalty, y, " and married couples tend to pay higher tax rates . Also , married people can decide whether they want to file jointly or if they ' d like to file separately . In the vast majority of cases , filing jointly is the correct answer, r, because in the late 1990s the federal government , in an effort to simplify the tax code , made filing separately less advantageous (and even more disadvantaged) .

section_heading: ## There are generally four ways to file taxes as a person (not an entity):
text:
## There are generally four ways to file taxes as a person (not an entity):

Single - If you are unmarried or considered unmarried and do not qualify as " head of household , " which would give you significant tax advantages over filing single .

Married filing jointly - a married couple filing one tax return . This makes the most sense for 99% of all married couples .

Married filing separately - people who are married but decide for various reasons to file two separate tax returns . The government in the late 1990s cut most of the benefits of this filing structure , but it may make sense for some couples .

section_heading: ## Reasons a couple may decide to file separately:
text:
## Reasons a couple may decide to file separately:

- Independent liability each spouse is responsible for their own tax obligations . This can be beneficial for couples who don ' t share their money and one spouse has significant tax liabilities .
- Financial independence -similar to above , but in this case one spouse has substantial deductions or liabilities .
- Disparity in incomes - The lower paid spouse may benefit from itemizable deductions that could be lost if filing jointly .
- Specific situations - While there are penalties for failing to file jointly , there are many liability and other specific situations where a couple may choose to still file separately .

Head of household -If you are unmarried or considered unmarried , pay more than half of the household expenses , and have a qualifying dependent living with you for more than half the year , you may qualify . This could include a child , parent , or other relative .

If you own a company, y, there are also two ways to think about your taxes: as a " flow -through " to your own tax forms or as a separate entity . We ' ll dive into this more thoroughly later, r, but for most small businesses , a flow -through company is going to keep your tax filing simple and will also help you avoid double taxation . (See section 6 of this guide!)

## The BIG Lesson

Understanding tax brackets isn ' t just a small " wow, w, that ' s neat . " This is the basis of all planning you ' ll do throughout your lifetime . By understanding how to save for greater flexibility, y, and how to maximize deductions and credits to lower your taxable income (especially if you ' re nearing a bracket threshold) , you ' ll find significant tax savings that can make financial independence easier .

## Maximizing Tax Brackets

There are many ways to lower your tax bracket , but we can drive them into three basic ways to pay fewer taxes . You ' ll need to understand:

- What can be deductible if you itemize .
- How to position investments so that you can withdraw money from the right accounts in the future .
- How credits may help you save even more money than deductions .

For each of these areas , we ' ll also need to teach you what ' s available .

For a expanded list of common deductions , see section 9 .

For a list of investment opportunities , begin with section 3 , below .

## TAX BRACKETS: 2025

We 'r 're focusing on 2025 for your planning purposes. You 'l 'll complete these taxes in early 2026 . 2024, 4, which you filed this year, r, reflects 2024 tax rates. Many of these rates previously had "s "sunset " dates . Those have been removed and these brackets are "p "permanent " with the OBBB changes . (We use quotes around "p "permanent" because the idea that taxes rates are ever permanent is laughable . )

Marginal rates . For tax year 2025 , the top tax rate remained 37% for individual single taxpayers with incomes greater than $626 , 350 ($751 , 600 for married couples filing jointly) . The other rates are:

- 35% for incomes over $250 , 525 ($501 , 050 for married couples filing jointly) .
- 32% for incomes over $197 , 300 ($394 , 600 for married couples filing jointly) .
- 24% for incomes over $103 , 350 ($206 , 700 for married couples filing jointly) .
- 22% for incomes over $48 , 475 ($96 , 950 for married couples filing jointly) .
- 12% for incomes over $11 , 925 ($23 , 850 for married couples filing jointly) .
- 10% for incomes $11 , 925 or less ($23 , 850 or less for married couples filing jointly) .

section_heading: ## Changed for 2026: lower brackets previously didn 't 't have inflation indexing . Beginning in 2026 these brackets will index for inflation .
text:
## Changed for 2026: lower brackets previously didn 't 't have inflation indexing . Beginning in 2026 these brackets will index for inflation .

While we ' re at it , there is also a change to the standard deduction:

## Standard deduction in 2025

- For single taxpayers and married individuals filing separately: standard deduction rises to $15 , 000 for 2025 , an increase of $400 from 2024 .
- For married couples filing jointly: the standard deduction rises to $30 , 000 , an increase of $800 from tax year 2024 .
- For heads of households: the standard deduction will be $22 , 500 for tax year 2025 , an increase of $600 from the amount for tax year 2024 .


Now that you know tax brackets and rates , let 's 's dive into how to lower these today and in the future! Next step? Let 's 's take advantage of tax sheltered accounts .

section_heading: ## SECTION 3: Maximizing Your Tax Sheltered Accounts
text:
## SECTION 3: Maximizing Your Tax Sheltered Accounts

## STEP 1 Timeline Your Goals to Find the Best Tax Treatment

The best way to deal with taxes? Avoid them entirely! (And if you ' ve cracked that code , please call us immediately . ) While that ' s not possible for most of us mere mortals , you can choose to minimize taxes by picking when you pay them: either now or later . You can defer taxes on the way in , or you can pay taxes up front and enjoy tax-free withdrawals later .

As you ' ve learned in STACKED (and on the podcast) , good tax planning starts with one critical tool: a timeline for your goals . Knowing when you ' ll need your money — and in some cases , what you ' ll use it for—helps you pick the right tax strategy for each goal .

Let ' s walk through an example .

Imagine you ' ve got the following goals:

- 1 . Your child ' s education (they ' re 12 now, w, so college is around the corner) .
- 2 . Early retirement in 18 years for spouse (you ' ll be 53) .
- 3 . A second home (no clear timeline) .
- 4 . Normal retirement for you (someday in the future) .


ROTH

Here ' s how you ' d structure your tax-advantaged investments:

section_heading: ## SECTION 3: Maximizing Your Tax Sheltered Accounts
text:
- Your Child 's 's Education: Open a 529 plan . Contributions grow tax-free , and withdrawals for qualified expenses (like tuition , books , and even some room and board) are tax-free . Bonus: You might even score a state tax deduction .
- Early Retirement: Since Roth IRA contributions can be withdrawn penalty-free , you might start there , but Roth accounts have annual contribution limits and are better optimized for later tax savings . You could also add to your workplace retirement plan , but be aware of IRS Rule 72(t) — not exactly your best friend . To keep things flexible , you could save in a non-qualified brokerage account , keeping it separate from your other goals .
- Normal Retirement: Here ' s where you use a mix of pre-tax and Roth accounts . This allows you to manage your tax brackets in retirement by strategically choosing where your withdrawals come from each year . (See how this strategy works in the next section!)
- Second Home: For simplicity, y, you can save for this in a separate non-qualified brokerage account . Keeping it separate ensures you don ' t accidentally dip into these funds for another goal . See how that works? This approach optimizes tax savings while keeping your plan flexible enough to adapt as life throws its curveballs .

## STEP 2 Understand the Tax Triangle


After you ' ve laid out your timeline and goals , it ' s time to figure out which tax-advantaged levers to pull . A simple way to think about this? Picture a triangle . Each corner represents a different type of tax treatment for investments:

- 1 . Pre-Tax (Tax-Deferred Accounts)
- 2 . Tax-Free Accounts
- 3 . Normal Tax Treatment (Taxable Accounts)

Let ' s break down each corner of this triangle — the strengths , weaknesses , and the kinds of investments you ' ll typically find inside .

## Pre-Tax: The Tax Procrastinator 's 's Dream

## Examples:

- 401(k)/403(b)
- Traditional IRA
- HSA (when used for medical expenses)
- Pensions

## Strengths:

- Immediate tax savings: Contributions reduce your taxable income today .
- Tax -deferred growth: No annual taxes on gains , dividends , or interest .
- Ideal for those who expect to be in a lower tax bracket in retirement .

## Weaknesses:

- Withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income , and early withdrawals come with penalties (unless you qualify for an exception) .
- RMDs start at age 73 (as of 2023-present) , forcing you to take taxable withdrawals whether you need the money or not .

section_heading: ## Tax-Free: The Unicorn of Tax Strategies
text:
## Tax-Free: The Unicorn of Tax Strategies

## Examples:

- Roth IRA and Roth 401(k)
- 529 Plans (for qualified education expenses)
- HSA (for medical expenses in retirement)
- Cash Value Life Insurance

## Strengths:

- Withdrawals of contributions and earnings are tax-free if you follow the rules .
- Roth IRAs have no RMDs , giving you more control over your money in retirement .
- Fantastic for long-term growth , estate planning, and financial flexibility .

## Weaknesses:

- Contribution limits are tight (Roth IRA: $7 , 000/year for 2025; $8 , 000 if 50+) .
- Income restrictions apply for Roth IRA contributions (check those phase-outs) .
- With life insurance , you ' re also paying for the insurance component , so costs can be high .

section_heading: ## Deep Dive: Effectively Using Your Health Savings Account (HSA)
text:
## Deep Dive: Effectively Using Your Health Savings Account (HSA)

An HSA can dramatically change your tax savings picture if you use it correctly .

- HSAs contributions are pre-tax , making this saving drop the tax you ' ll owe this current year .
- If you keep receipts , you may withdraw funds tax-free from an HSA , making your contributions fully non-taxable .
- Without keeping receipts , you may withdraw funds tax-deffered from your HSA after age 65 , making your experience similar to a traditional IRA or 401k plan .

## Potential Pitfalls to an HSA:

- If you wish to use this money before age 65 for anything other than health-related expenses , you ' ll pay penalties . Opt for flexibility if you think this may be the case .
- Money in an HSA won ' t grow effectively unless you invest it appropriately . Choose investments based on your timeframe and risk tolerance .
- HSAs are part of your comprehensive health plan , so they must be paired with an eligible high deductible health care option . These plans may significantly increase the out -offpocket expense you have for health care .

HSA Best -Use Flow Chart

section_heading: ## Normal Tax Treatment: Flexibility Over Tax Perks
text:
## Normal Tax Treatment: Flexibility Over Tax Perks

## Examples:

- Brokerage Accounts
- Savings Accounts
- Non -retirement Real Estate

## Strengths:

- No contribution limits—invest as much as you want .
- Total liquidity: Withdraw money anytime without penalties .
- Tax -loss harvesting opportunities: Use losses to offset gains .

## Weaknesses:

- Annual taxes on dividends , interest , and realized capital gains eat into your returns .
- Short -term capital gains are taxed as ordinary income , which can get expensive fast .

## Why the Tax Triangle Matters

Each corner of the Tax Triangle (see graphic on next page) offers different benefits , and the right mix for you depends on your goals and timeline . Whether you ' re saving for retirement , college , or that dream vacation home , knowing how to structure your investments across the Tax Triangle can save you money now and later .

So , Stackers , as you map out your goals , remember: taxes may be inevitable , but with the right strategy, y, they don ' t have to steal the spotlight . Let ' s make Uncle Sam take a backseat while you drive your financial plan forward .

section_heading: ## STEP 3 Master Your Workplace Retirement Accounts
text:
## STEP 3 Master Your Workplace Retirement Accounts

Once you ' ve got your goals timelined and understand the Tax Triangle , it ' s time to focus on one of the most powerful tools in your financial arsenal: workplace retirement accounts . These accounts are like Mom ' s lasagna — layered with goodness , but only if you know how to put them together properly .

From 401(k)s to SEP IRAs , each account type has unique features , rules , and quirks that can help you build a rock-solid financial future . Let ' s dive into the details so you can squeeze every last penny of advantage out of these tools .

section_heading: ## The Classic 401(k): The Swiss Army Knife of Workplace Retirement
text:
## The Classic 401(k): The Swiss Army Knife of Workplace Retirement

## What It Is:

The 401(k) is a retirement savings plan offered by many employers . Contributions are made pre-tax (or after-tax in a Roth 401(k)) , and investments grow tax-deferred (or tax-free in the Roth version) .

## Key Benefits:

- Employer Match: Free money alert! If your employer matches contributions , it ' s like doubling your savings rate . Don ' t leave this cash on the table—it ' s yours for the taking.
- High Contribution Limits: For 2025 , you can contribute up to $23 , 500 ($31 , 000 if you ' re 50+) .
- Automatic Saving: Contributions come right out of your paycheck , so you don ' t have to think about it .

## Watch Out For:

- High Fees: Some 401(k)s have limited investment options with higher fees . Check your plan ' s expense ratios and choose low-cost index funds if available .
- RMDs for Traditional 401(k)s: You ' ll be required to start taking distributions at age 73 (unless you ' re still working for the employer sponsoring the plan) .
- Loan Temptation: Borrowing from your 401(k) can derail your progress — and Uncle Sam might not be thrilled if you can ' t pay it back on time .

Pro Tip: Max out your contributions after claiming the employer match, h, then move on to other taxxadvantaged accounts like a Roth IRA .

section_heading: ## Roth 401(k): Same Plan, Better Tax Treatment
text:
## Roth 401(k): Same Plan, Better Tax Treatment

## What It Is:

Think of the Roth 401(k) as your 401(k) ' s hip cousin who pays taxes now so they can laugh all the way to the bank later . Contributions are made with after -tax dollars , but withdrawals (including earnings) are tax-free in retirement .

## Key Benefits:

- Higher Contribution Limits Than Roth IRAs: The $22 , 500 annual limit ($30 , 000 if you ' re 50+) applies here too , making this a great option for higher earners .
- Employer Match Still Applies: The match goes into a traditional 401(k) bucket , but hey, y, free money is free money .
- No Income Limits: Unlike Roth IRAs , there ' s no income phase-out for contributions .

## Watch Out For:

- Taxing the Match: While your contributions grow tax-free , your employer match is pre -tax and will be taxed when you withdraw it .
- RMDs Still Apply: Roth 401(k)s are subject to RMDs unless you roll them into a Roth IRA .

section_heading: ## The Traditional IRA: Retirement for Everyone
text:
## The Traditional IRA: Retirement for Everyone

## What It Is:

A Traditional IRA (Individual Retirement Account) is a tax-deferred retirement account you can open on your own — no employer required . Contributions may be tax-deductible , depending on your income and access to a workplace retirement plan .

## Key Benefits:

- Flexibility: You can open this account even if you ' re self -employed or don ' t have access to a 401(k) .
- Tax-Deferred Growth: Investments grow without annual taxes until withdrawal .
- Catch -Up Contributions: If you ' re 50+ , you can contribute an extra $1 , 000 annually (limit for 2025 is $7 , 000 , $8 , 000 with the catch -u p) .

## Watch Out For:

- Income Phase -Outs for Deductions: If you ' re covered by a workplace retirement plan , your ability to deduct contributions phases out at certain income levels .
- Early Withdrawal Penalties: Withdraw funds before 59½ , and you ' ll face a 10% penalty plus taxes , unless an exception applies .

section_heading: ## The Roth IRA: Tax-Free Growth for the Win
text:
## The Roth IRA: Tax-Free Growth for the Win

## What It Is:

The Roth IRA lets you contribute after-tax dollars , with tax -free growth and withdrawals if you follow the rules .

## Key Benefits:

- Tax-Free Withdrawals: Qualified withdrawals in retirement are tax-free—earnings and all .
- No RMDs: Roth IRAs let your money grow tax-free for as long as you want .
- Flexibility: You can withdraw contributions (but not earnings) at any time without penalties .

## Watch Out For:

- Income Limits: Contributions phase out for higher earners (for2025 , phase-outs begin at $150 , 000 for single filers and $236 , 000 for married filing jointly) .
- Low Contribution Limits: You can only contribute $7 , 000 annually ($8 , 000 if you ' re 50+) .

Pro Tip: If you earn too much to contribute directly, y, use the backdoor Roth strategy (details HERE) .

## SEP IRA: The Self-Employed Superstar

## What It Is:

The Simplified Employee Pension (SEP) IRA is a retirement plan for self-employed individuals and small business owners . Contributions are tax -deferred .

## Key Benefits:

- High Contribution Limits: Up to 25% of your compensation or $70 , 000 for 2025 , whichever is less .
- Tax Deduction: Contributions are deductible as a business expense .
- Simple Setup: Minimal paperwork and low administrative costs .

## Watch Out For:

- Employer-Only Contributions: If you have employees , you must contribute the same percentage of their compensation as you do for yourself .
- RMDs Apply: You ' ll need to start taking distributions at age 73 .

## Bringing It All Together

Maximizing your workplace retirement accounts isn ' t just about throwing money into an account and hoping for the best . It ' s about understanding the strengths and limitations of each option , strategically layering your contributions , and taking full advantage of tax benefits .

Whether you ' re scooping up free money with an employer match , harnessing tax-free growth in a Roth account , or using a SEP IRA to supercharge your self-employed savings , each of these tools plays a critical role in your financial success .

Stackers , this is your invitation to take action . Start with your 401(k) , layer in your IRA , and don ' t be afraid to get creative (responsibly) with your retirement plan . Remember: future you is counting on present you to make smart moves . And hey, y, if you can do that while sitting in your pajamas listening to the podcast , even better .

section_heading: ## Wait … how do I maximize my Roth IRA savings if I ' m not eligible?
text:
## Wait … how do I maximize my Roth IRA savings if I ' m not eligible?

Great question , Stacker I made up for this section!

If you ' re a taxpayer in the USA who ' s absolutely crushing it income-wise (high five!) , you might find yourself too successful to stroll through the " front door " of Roth IRA contributions .

Each year, r, the IRS sets income limits for direct Roth IRA contributions — and if your earnings blast past those numbers (you show-off , you) , you ' re locked out . (See the current limits above , and of course , check Mom ' s handy lists at the back of this guide for the latest numbers . )

But fear not . If the front door is slammed shut , you can still sneak in through the " back door " — legally, y, proudly, y, and with no need for a ski mask .

Welcome to the world of the Back Door Roth IRA .

section_heading: ## How Does the "Back Door" Roth IRA Work?
text:
## How Does the "Back Door" Roth IRA Work?

Simply put:

If you ' re a high-income earner, r, you can still contribute to a traditional IRA — specifically, y, a non-deductible one (meaning you don ' t get a tax break now) .

Once the cash is in that non -deductible IRA , you can convert it into a Roth IRA .

The IRS says , " Cool with us , " as long as:

- You follow the rules (no sneaky shortcuts) .
- You pay any tax due (and this year, r, not " someday maybe . " )

If you do this quickly after making your initial contribution (and before the account earns anything) , there may be little or no tax due on the conversion . Think of it like walking through the mud before it rains instead of after .

section_heading: ## What's the Limit on How Much I Can Flip Using the "Back Door"?
text:
## What's the Limit on How Much I Can Flip Using the "Back Door"?

Here ' s where it gets spicy:

- The IRS doesn ' t set a specific limit on conversions .
- However , you can only contribute up to the annual IRA contribution limit in the first place .

(Example: $7 , 000 if you ' re under 50; $8 , 000 if you ' re 50 or older — check Mom ' s latest numbers for updates . )

## Important heads-up:

While flipping small amounts is relatively drama-free , trying to convert huge balances (say, y, from an old pre-tax IRA) could trigger a whopper of a tax bill .

If you don ' t have cash on hand to pay Uncle Sam , it could get real ugly, y, real fast.

section_heading: ## Step-by-Step: How to Do a Back Door Roth IRA (The Smart Way)
text:
## Step-by-Step: How to Do a Back Door Roth IRA (The Smart Way)

- 1 . Open a Traditional IRA (specifically for a non-deductible contribution) .
- 2 . Make your non-deductible contribution (stick to the annual limit) .
- 3 . Open a Roth IRA (if you don ' t already have one — it ' ll just sit there politely waiting) .
- 4 . Quickly convert the funds from the Traditional IRA to the Roth IRA .
- Speed matters: You want to do this before your Traditional IRA earns dividends , interest , or grows — otherwise , you ' ll owe taxes on those gains .
- 5 . Pay any taxes due (ideally minimal , if you moved fast enough) .
- 6 . Invest the money inside your Roth IRA like the future financial legend you are .

section_heading: ## Why Would I Bother With All This?
text:
## Why Would I Bother With All This?

Oh , just a few tiny perks:

- No Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) during your lifetime (unlike Traditional IRAs , which eventually force you to withdraw money whether you need it or not) .
- Tax-free growth and tax-free withdrawals in retirement (future you will want to hug past you) .
- Flexible use of contributions (you can pull out the amount you put in anytime without penalty — though earnings have stricter rules) .
- Estate planning win: Roth IRAs transfer to your heirs income tax-free (cue your future grandkids throwing you a posthumous " Thanks , Grandpa/Grandma! " party) .

section_heading: ## Final Word on Back Door Roth IRAs:
text:
## Final Word on Back Door Roth IRAs:

If you ' re earning too much to walk through the Roth IRA' A' s front door, r, don ' t just shrug and move on .

The Back Door Roth IRA is a legitimate , IRS -approved strategy to sneak those dollars into one of the most powerful retirement accounts available .

Done right , it ' s like planting a money tree in your Roth garden — tax-free shade for decades to come .

Warning: Like all good things involving the IRS , the devil ' s in the details . If you already have pre -tax IRA money elsewhere (rollovers , old deductible contributions) , special rules called the pro -rata rule can complicate things . (More on that in Mom ' s extra-credit section at the end . )

But for many high earners?

This back door is wide open — and your Roth IRA dreams are waiting inside .

section_heading: ## STEP 4 Maximize Your Health Savings and Flexible Spending Accounts
text:
## STEP 4 Maximize Your Health Savings and Flexible Spending Accounts

## Let 's 's talk about Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) and Flexible Spending Accounts

(FSAs) — the unsung heroes of tax-advantaged savings . Sure , they might not sound as exciting as your 401(k) or Roth IRA , but these accounts can pack a serious punch when it comes to saving money and covering your healthcare costs . For some people , we like funding the HSA BEFORE funding your retirement accounts . Why? Read on! (Plus , both the HSA and FSA come with tax perks that even Uncle Sam can ' t argue with) .

Let ' s dive into the details so you can squeeze every bit of value out of these accounts—because why pay full price when you don ' t have to?

section_heading: ## Health Savings Accounts (HSAs): The Triple Tax Threat
text:
## Health Savings Accounts (HSAs): The Triple Tax Threat

## What It Is:

An HSA is a savings account specifically designed for people with high-deductible health plans (HDHPs) . It ' s the rare unicorn of tax -advantaged accounts , offering triple tax benefits:

- 1 . Contributions are tax -deductible (or pre-tax if made through payroll) .
- 2 . Growth is tax -free .
- 3 . Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are also tax-free .

## Key Benefits:

- Triple Tax Advantage: No taxes going in , no taxes while it grows , and no taxes coming out (for qualified expenses) .
- High Contribution Limits: For 2025 , you can contribute up to $4 , 300 for self -only coverage or $8 , 550 for family coverage . If you ' re 55 or older, r, tack on an extra $1 , 000 .
- Rollover Power: Unlike FSAs , your unused funds roll over year after year . No " use it or lose it " pressure here!
- Retirement Bonus: After age 65 , you can withdraw funds for any purpose without penalties (though non-medical withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income — so hang on to those medical -expense receipts from years past!) .

## (continued)

## Watch Out For:

- Eligibility: You need to be enrolled in a high-deductible health plan to contribute . No HDHP? No HSA contributions (though you may continue to use your existing HSA) .
- Non-Qualified Withdrawals: If you ' re under 65 and withdraw funds for non -medical expenses , you ' ll face a 20% penalty and income taxes . Ouch .
- Limited Investment Options: Some HSA providers don ' t offer robust investment choices , so shop around .

Pro Tip: Use your HSA as a stealth retirement account by paying current medical expenses out of pocket (if you can afford to) and letting your HSA balance grow tax-free . Down the road, d, you can reimburse yourself for those expenses—tax-free —y —years after the fact (if you ' ve kept the receipts…) and AFTER age 65 even if you haven 't 't kept receipts.

If you don 't 't have eligible medical expenses to offset the HSA distributions, there is a 20% tax penalty due to the IRS (if you 'r 're under age 65) , plus the distribution will be taxed as ordinary earned income . If you 'r 're age 65+, +, the distribution is still taxed as ordinary earned income (without the 20% penalty)

section_heading: ## Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs): The Use-It-or-Lose-It Workhorse
text:
## Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs): The Use-It-or-Lose-It Workhorse

## What It Is:

An FSA is a tax -advantaged account offered through employers that lets you set aside pre -tax dollars for qualified medical or dependent care expenses .

## Key Benefits:

- Tax Savings: Contributions reduce your taxable income , saving you money on taxes .
- Immediate Access: Your full annual contribution amount is available on day one , even if you haven ' t contributed the full amount yet .
- Dependent Care FSAs: Use these accounts to save on childcare expenses , including daycare , preschool , or after -school care .

## Watch Out For:

- Use It or Lose It: Most FSAs require you to spend your funds by the end of the plan year, r, though some employers offer a grace period or allow a small amount to roll over (e . g. , $660 in 2025) .
- Contribution Limits: For 2025 , the annual limit is $3 , 300for healthcare FSAs and $5 , 000 for dependent care FSAs (or $2 , 500 if married filing separately) .
- No Portability: If you leave your job, you forfeit any unused funds unless you have eligible expenses incurred before your departure .

Pro Tip: Plan your contributions carefully. y. Estimate your annual healthcare or childcare costs to avoid leaving money on the table .

section_heading: ## HSAs vs. FSAs: Which One Is Right for You?
text:
## HSAs vs. FSAs: Which One Is Right for You?

- Eligibility: HSAs require a high-deductible health plan , while FSAs are typically tied to any employer health plan .
- Rollover Rules: HSAs roll over indefinitely; FSAs don ' t (with limited exceptions) .
- Tax Benefits: Both offer tax savings , but the HSA wins with its triple tax advantage .
- Flexibility: HSAs provide more long-term flexibility, y, while FSAs are better for near-term , predictable expenses .

## How to Maximize These Accounts

- 1 . Contribute Enough to Cover Medical Expenses: Start by estimating your annual out-of -pocket healthcare costs and contribute at least that amount to your HSA or FSA .
- 2 . Max Out Your HSA If Possible: If you can afford to max out your HSA , treat it as a secondary retirement account . The tax advantages are unbeatable .
- 3 . Invest Your HSA Funds: Once your HSA balance exceeds the minimum required for investing, consider putting the extra funds into low-cost index funds to grow tax-free .
- 4 . Spend FSA Dollars Wisely: Use your FSA funds early in the plan year for predictable expenses like eyeglasses , dental work , or prescription medications .
- 5 . Save Receipts: For HSAs , keep records of all your qualified medical expenses . You can reimburse yourself later — even years down the road .

## Why HSAs and FSAs Matter

Think of these accounts as your financial Swiss Army knives . Whether you ' re using your HSA to stash extra retirement funds or leveraging your FSA to cover braces for your kid , they ' re powerful tools for cutting down on taxes and keeping more money in your pocket .

So , Stackers , don ' t sleep on these accounts . Whether it ' s a tax-free trip to the doctor or a smarter way to pay for daycare , HSAs and FSAs are here to make your financial life healthier—and a lot less taxing.

section_heading: ## STEP 5 Supercharge Your Education Savings with 529 Plans
text:
## STEP 5 Supercharge Your Education Savings with 529 Plans

Let ' s chat about education savings accounts — the secret weapon for funding college , trade school , or even that backup plan where your kid becomes a world-class pastry chef . The 529 plan is the star of this show, w, and when used strategically, y, it ' s like having a personal tutor for your money, y, ensuring it gets the most out of every dollar .

## What is a 529 Plan?

A 529 plan is a tax-advantaged savings account designed to help you save for qualified education expenses . It ' s the go-to option for parents , grandparents , and anyone who wants to invest in a brighter (and hopefully less expensive) future for a student .

## Key Benefits of a 529 Plan

- Tax-Free Growth: Contributions grow tax-free , and withdrawals are also tax -free when used for qualified education expenses like tuition , books , and room and board .
- State Tax Benefits: Many states offer tax deductions or credits for contributions , so check your state ' s rules for extra savings
- High Contribution Limits: Unlike Roth IRAs or other accounts , 529 plans allow you to contribute up to $19 , 000 per year (2025) per beneficiary without triggering the federal gift tax . You can also front -load up to five years ' worth of contributions at once — $95 , 000 for individuals or $190 , 000 for couples .
- Flexible Use: Funds can be used for more than just college tuition . They cover K-12 tuition (up to $10 , 000 annually) , trade schools , apprenticeships , and even student loan repayments (up to $10 , 000 total per beneficiary) .
- Account Control: As the account owner, r, you maintain control of the funds . If Junior decides to drop out tojoin a band , you can redirect the money to another beneficiary or save it for your own education dreams .

## Potential Downsides of a 529 Plan

- Qualified Expenses Only: Withdrawals for non-qualified expenses are subject to income tax on earnings and a 10% penalty . No , Doug, you can ' t use this to fund a wine -tasting trip to Italy (unless you enroll in a wine studies program!) .
- State-Specific Rules: Each state runs its own plan , and while you can invest in any state ' s 529 , the perks vary widely . Some states don ' t offer tax benefits to out -of -state plans . Review this resource from Saving for College to see each state ' s income tax benefit for 529 contributions .
- Limited Investment Options: Plans usually offer a range of pre-selected investment portfolios , which might not be as customizable as your brokerage account .
- Impact on Financial Aid: Assets in a 529 plan are considered the parent ' s asset for FAFSA purposes , which can slightly reduce financial aid eligibility (though much less than if the funds were in the child ' s name) .

## How to Maximize Your 529 Plan

- 1 . Start Early: The sooner you open a 529 plan , the longer your contributions have to grow tax-free . Even small , consistent contributions can add up over time .
- 2 . Take Advantage of State Tax Breaks: If your state offers tax benefits , consider investing in its plan — just make sure it has reasonable fees and investment options .
- 3 . Automate Contributions: Set up automatic monthly contributions to keep your savings on track without thinking about it .
- 4 . Invest Aggressively Early, y, Then Shift: Most 529 plans offer age-based portfolios that start with higher equity allocations and gradually become more conservative as the beneficiary approaches college age . This can help maximize growth early while reducing risk later .

- 5 . Use Funds Strategically: Withdraw money for qualified expenses first to maximize tax benefits . Consider pairing with other tax-advantaged accounts like Coverdell ESAs or Roth IRAs for flexibility .
- 6 . Coordinate with Family: Grandparents or other relatives can contribute directly to the 529 plan . Bonus: Their contributions might avoid gift taxes , too

## Alternatives to 529 Plans

While 529 plans are the MVP for education savings , there are other players on the field:

- Coverdell Education Savings Accounts (ESAs): Similar to a 529 but with lower contribution limits ($2 , 000 per year) and more flexible investment options .
- Why use this account? If someone in the family is headed to K12 education programs , you may use Coverdell ESAs to cover these costs .
- Roth IRAs: Not just for retirement — Roth contributions can be withdrawn tax -free and penalty-free for qualified education expenses .
- We prefer to save Roth IRA dollars to maximize tax brackets in retirement , so generally we ' ve cautioned people against using these dollars for college .
- UTMA/UGMA Accounts: Custodial accounts that can be used for anything benefiting the child , not just education (but lack the tax perks of 529s) .
- The biggest downside of UTMA/UGMA accounts is that they are " taxed " far more harshly than a 529 plan when it comes to expected family contribution .

## Why Education Savings Plans Matter

A 529 plan isn ' t just an investment in your kid ' s future—it ' s an investment in your own sanity . With college costs climbing faster than Doug's stories about trivia nights , having a tax-advantaged plan in place can save you stress and money . Whether it ' s funding tuition , covering books , or helping your kid achieve their dream of becoming a rocket scientist , these accounts are a no -brainer for education savings .

So , what ' s your next step? Dust off that timeline , figure out how much you ' ll need , and let ' s get saving. Because the only thing better than watching your kid graduate is knowing you didn ' t pay an extra dime in taxes to get there .

## SECTION 4: Tax-Loss Harvesting

Now let ' s show you a way to turn lemons into lemonade — or, r, in this case , investment losses into tax savings . Welcome to the world of tax -loss harvesting, where even your portfolio ' s bad days can work for you . This strategy isn ' t just for fancy Wall Street types; it ' s for anyone who wants to maximize their after-tax returns and keep Uncle Sam ' s hand out of their wallet .

Here ' s how to use your losses wisely and avoid common pitfalls (like the dreaded wash sale rule) while staying in the good graces of the IRS .

## What is Tax-Loss Harvesting?

Tax -loss harvesting is the process of selling investments that have dropped in value to offset capital gains (and , in some cases , ordinary income) . Think of it as turning that underperforming stock into a tax-saving hero .

## How Tax-Loss Harvesting Works

- 1 . Offset Capital Gains: Losses from one investment can be used to offset gains from another . If you sold some investments at a profit this year, r, those losses can neutralize the tax impact .
- 2 . Offset Ordinary Income: If your losses exceed your gains , you can use up to $3 , 000 of the excess to reduce your ordinary income .
- 3 . Carry Forward Losses: Got more than $3 , 000 in unused losses? No problem! You can carry them forward to offset gains or income in future years .

## Why Tax-Loss Harvesting Matters

- Lower Taxes: By offsetting gains and income , you reduce your tax bill — leaving more money to reinvest .
- Improve Portfolio Health: Selling losers can free up capital to reinvest in more promising opportunities .
- Long-Term Gains: Used strategically, y, this approach can help you keep more of your money working for you , year after year .

section_heading: ## Wash Sales: The Rule You Can't Ignore
text:
## Wash Sales: The Rule You Can't Ignore

Of course , the IRS isn ' t going to let you harvest losses without some ground rules . Enter the wash sale rule , which prevents you from claiming a tax loss if you sell a security at a loss and buy a " substantially identical " security within 30 days before or after the sale .

## What Counts as "Substantially Identical"?

- The same stock , bond , or mutual fund .
- Options or contracts to purchase the same stock or bond .

## Why Does This Matter?

Let's say you sell Stock A at a loss, then buy it back two weeks later because you still believe in it long-term . The IRS says, "N "Nice try, y, " and disallows your loss for tax purposes. The loss isn 't 't gone forever — it's added to the cost basis of the new investment—but you won 't 't get the immediate tax benefit.

## How to Avoid Wash Sales

- 1 . Wait 31 Days: The simplest way to avoid a wash sale is to wait at least 31 days before repurchasing the same investment .
- 2 . Buy a Similar, r, Not Identical , Investment: Instead of repurchasing Stock A , consider buying Stock B , which is in the same industry or sector but isn ' t " substantially identical . " For example , sell an S&P 500 ETF from one provider and buy another provider ' s version .
- 3 . Coordinate Accounts: Wash sale rules apply across all your accounts , including IRAs and your spouse ' s accounts . Make sure you ' re not accidentally triggering a wash sale by buying the same security in another account .

## When Tax-Loss Harvesting Makes Sense

- 1 . You Have Gains to Offset: If you ' ve sold other investments at a profit this year, r, harvesting losses can neutralize those gains .
- 2 . You ' re in a High Tax Bracket: The higher your tax rate , the more valuable tax -loss harvesting becomes .
- 3 . Rebalancing Your Portfolio: Harvesting losses while rebalancing lets you kill two birds with one stone—improve your portfolio and save on taxes .

## When to Skip Tax-Loss Harvesting

- 1 . If It Compromises Your Long-Term Plan: Don ' t sell an investment just for the tax benefit if it disrupts your overall strategy .
- 2 . In Tax -Advantaged Accounts: Losses in accounts like 401(k)s , IRAs , or HSAs aren ' t deductible , so tax-loss harvesting doesn ' t apply here .
- 3 . If the Savings Don ' t Outweigh the Costs: Factor in transaction fees , bid/ask spreads , and potential tax consequences before selling.

section_heading: ## Step-by-Step: How to Execute Tax-Loss Harvesting
text:
## Step-by-Step: How to Execute Tax-Loss Harvesting

- 1 . Review Your Portfolio: Identify underperforming investments you ' re ready to part with .
- 2 . Calculate Gains and Losses: Determine your total gains and losses for the year to .
- see how much offsetting you need
- 3 . Sell Strategically: Sell enough losing investments to offset your gains or maximize the $3 , 000 deduction .
- 4 . Reinvest Smartly: Avoid wash sales by choosing a different investment or waiting 31 days to repurchase .
- 5 . Track Everything: Document every trade , gain , and loss to ensure you ' re compliant with IRS rules .

## The BIG Lesson

Tax -loss harvesting isn ' t just a way to save money — it ' s a way to keep your financial plan on track , even when the market decides to throw a tantrum . By understanding the rules (and staying clear of the dreaded wash sale) , you can turn portfolio pain into a long-term gain .

So the next time your investments have a bad day, y, remember: every loss has the potential to help you win the tax game . And as always , make sure you ' re playing by the rules—because Uncle Sam doesn ' t mess around when it comes to wash sales .

## SECTION 5: Charitable Giving Strategies

Giving back isn ' t just good for the soul — it can also be good for your wallet . Whether you ' re looking to lower your tax bill , simplify your giving, or make a bigger impact , there are two key strategies to keep in your toolkit: Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) and Qualified Charitable Distributions (QCDs) .

Let ' s dive into how these powerful tools work and how you can use them to give smarter, r, not just harder .

## What are Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs)?

A donor-advised fund is like your personal giving account , but with tax perks . You contribute to the fund , get an immediate tax deduction , and then recommend grants to your favorite charities over time . Think of it as combining a savings account with a charitable twist—except instead of earning interest , you ' re earning good karma .

## How DAFs Work:

- 1 . Contribute: Donate cash , stocks , or other assets to your donor-advised fund . You ' ll get a tax deduction for the value of your contribution in the year it ' s made .
- 2 . Invest: The money in your DAF can be invested , allowing it to grow tax-free while you decide which charities to support .
- 3 . Grant: Recommend grants to qualified charities whenever you ' re ready .

## Key Benefits of Donor-Advised Funds

- Immediate Tax Benefits: Get a tax deduction in the year you contribute , even if you don ' t distribute the funds to a charity right away .
- Flexible Giving: Support multiple charities over time , even in different years , while only making one contribution .
- Investment Growth: If your DAF funds are invested , they can grow tax-free , increasing the amount you can eventually donate .
- Simplified Record-Keeping: Instead of keeping track of receipts from multiple charities , you ' ll have one statement from your DAF. F.

## Heads-Ups for Donor-Advised Funds

- Irrevocable Contribution: Once you donate to a DAF, F, the funds are no longer yours . You can ' t take them back .
- Fees: Some DAF providers charge administrative and investment fees , which can eat into your balance .
- Charity Eligibility: You can only make grants to qualified 501(c)(3) organizations .

Pro Tip: Donating appreciated assets (like stocks or mutual funds) to a DAF allows you to avoid capital gains taxes while deducting the full market value of the asset. It's a win-win!

## What are Qualified Charitable Distributions (QCDs)?

A Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD) is a direct transfer of funds from your IRA to a qualified charity . If you ' re 70½ or older, r, QCDs are one of the smartest ways to combine your giving goals with your tax strategy .

## How QCDs Work:

- 1 . Eligibility: You must be 70½ or older and own a Traditional IRA .
- 2 . Distribution: Directly transfer up to $100 , 000 annually from your IRA to a qualified charity .
- 3 . Tax-Free: QCDs aren ' t included in your taxable income , which can help lower your adjusted gross income (AGI) .

section_heading: ## Key Benefits of Qualified Charitable Distributions
text:
## Key Benefits of Qualified Charitable Distributions

- Reduce Taxable Income: QCDs don ' t count as taxable income , which means no extra income taxes .
- Satisfy RMDs: If you ' re required to take a required minimum distribution (RMD) , a QCD can count toward that amount .
- Lower AGI Impact: By keeping your AGI lower, r, you may also reduce the taxability of Social Security benefits and avoid income-based Medicare surcharges .

section_heading: ## Watch Outs for Qualified Charitable Distributions
text:
## Watch Outs for Qualified Charitable Distributions

- Account Type Matters: QCDs can only be made from Traditional IRAs , not Roth IRAs or 401(k)s .
- Direct Transfer Required: The funds must go directly to the charity . If you withdraw the money first , it ' s taxable income .
- Qualified Charities Only: Make sure the charity is eligible to receive QCDs . Contributions to donor -advised funds or private foundations don ' t count .

Pro Tip: Use QCDs if you no longer itemize deductions. You 'l 'll still get the tax benefit of giving without needing to itemize .

section_heading: ## When to Use DAFs vs. QCDs
text:
## When to Use DAFs vs. QCDs

- DAFs: Great for multi -year giving strategies , donating appreciated assets , or simplifying your charitable donations .
- QCDs: Ideal for retirees who want to lower taxable income , satisfy RMDs , or give directly from their IRA .

section_heading: ## Making the Most of Your Charitable Giving
text:
## Making the Most of Your Charitable Giving

- Bundle Donations: If you don ' t itemize every year, r, consider " bunching" multiple years ' worth of donations into one year using a DAF. F. This allows you to itemize in that year and take the standard deduction in others .
- Plan Around RMDs: Use QCDs to reduce the tax impact of your required minimum distributions .
- Work with a Pro: Consult a tax advisor to ensure your charitable giving strategy aligns with your overall financial plan .

New for 2026: Corporations may deduct charitable contributions exceeding 1% of taxable income and up to 10% with carry-forward options .

## The BIG Lesson

Giving back doesn ' t have to come at a huge cost to you . Whether you ' re using a donor -advised fund to grow your impact over time or a QCD to lower your tax bill in retirement , smart charitable giving strategies can help you make the world a better place while keeping Uncle Sam at bay .

Because what ' s better than saving money and spreading good vibes? Absolutely nothing.

## SECTION 6: Small Business Tax Planning

Running a small business can be rewarding, but let ' s be real—navigating the tax landscape can feel like trying to assemble IKEA furniture without the instructions . Luckily, y, with a few smart strategies , you can minimize your tax burden and keep more of your hard-earned money working for you .

This section breaks down the key areas small business owners need to focus on , including how to choose the right business entity, y, take advantage of deductions , and use depreciation strategies to your benefit .

## Choosing the Right Business Entity

One of the first decisions you ' ll make as a small business owner is choosing your business entity . Your choice has big implications for how you ' re taxed , how you operate , and how much paperwork you ' ll need to deal with . Let ' s break it down .

## Sole Proprietorship

What It Is: The simplest structure — basically, y, you are the business .

Taxes: Profits are reported on your personal tax return , and you pay self-employment tax (15 . 3%) on earnings .

Pros: Easy setup, minimal paperwork .

Cons: You

’ re personally liable for all business debts and legal issues .

## Limited Liability Company (LLC)

What It Is: A flexible structure offering liability protection for the owner(s) .

Taxes: By default , profits flow through to your personal tax return . You can elect to be taxed as an S - Corp or C-Corp.

Pros: Liability protection , flexible taxation options .

Cons: Annual fees and paperwork vary by state .

## S-Corporation (S-Corp)

What It Is: A special tax status for corporations or LLCs that allows profits and losses to pass through to owners .

Taxes: Profits aren ' t subject to self-employment tax; instead , you pay yourself a " reasonable salary, y, " which is subject to payroll taxes , and take the rest as distributions (not subject to self-employment tax) .

Pros: Big savings on self-employment taxes .

Cons: Strict rules for eligibility, y, more paperwork , and payroll requirements .

## C-Corporation (C-Corp)

What It Is: A corporation taxed separately from its owners

.

Taxes: The business pays corporate taxes (21% federal) , and dividends are taxed again on the owner ’ s personal return (double taxation) .

Pros: Great for scaling, unlimited shareholders , and access to certain tax deductions .

Cons: Double taxation and more regulatory requirements

.

Pro Tip: Most small businesses benefit from starting as an LLC and electing S-Corp status as they grow. w. Consult with a tax pro to determine what’s best for your business.

## Deductions for Small Businesses

One of the best parts of running a business? The tax deductions! Here are the key areas to focus on:

## Home Office Deduction

What It Is: If you work from home , you can deduct a portion of your home expenses .

Requirements: Your home office must be used exclusively and regularly for business .

## How It ’s ’s Calculated:

- 1 . Simplified Method: $5 per square foot , up to 300 square feet .
- 2 . Actual Expenses Method: Deduct a percentage of actual home expenses like rent , utilities , and internet .

## Vehicle Expenses

What It Is: Deduct expenses for a vehicle used for business .

## How It ’s ’s Calculated:

Standard Mileage Rate: Deduct a set rate per mile driven for business (70 cents/mile for 2025) .

Actual Expenses: Deduct costs for gas , maintenance , insurance , and depreciation based on business use percentage .

Pro Tip: Keep meticulous records of your mileage and expenses to substantiate your deduction .

## Business Expenses

What 's 's Deductible: Ordinary and necessary expenses for running your business , such as:

- Office supplies
- Marketing and advertising
- Professional services (e . g. , accountants , lawyers)
- Meals (50% deductible , must be business -related)
- Travel expenses (flights , hotels , rental cars , etc . )

## Depreciation Strategies

Depreciation lets you recover the cost of big-ticket items over time , rather than deducting the full expense in one year . This applies to assets like equipment , vehicles , and even buildings .

## Key Depreciation Methods:

- 1 . Straight-Line Depreciation: Spread the cost evenly over the asset ' s useful life . Simple and predictable .
- 2 . Bonus Depreciation: Deduct up to 40% of the asset ' s cost in the first year (2025) . This applies to new and used property .
- 3 . Section 179 Expensing: Deduct the full cost of qualifying assets (up to $125 million in 2025) in the year they ' re purchased and placed in service .

Pro Tip: Use bonus depreciation or Section 179 to maximize deductions in high-income years, but remember that taking these deductions upfront means fewer deductions in future years.

## The BIG Lesson

Running a small business is hard work , but smart tax planning can help you keep more of your profits . From choosing the right entity to leveraging deductions and depreciation , these strategies can save you money, y, simplify your finances , and set you up for success .

Because Stackers , when it comes to taxes , the goal isn ' t just to file — it ' s to file smarter . So , grab your calculator (or a pro) , and let ' s make sure your business thrives , one smart move at a time .

section_heading: ## SECTION 7: Estate and Gift Tax Planning—Keep Your Benjamins in the Right Hands
text:
## SECTION 7: Estate and Gift Tax Planning—Keep Your Benjamins in the Right Hands

When you hear " estate planning, " you might picture yachts , sprawling mansions , or monocled billionaires . But here ' s the truth: everyone needs an estate plan . Whether you ' re leaving behind a collection of vintage Nintendo games or a multimillion-dollar portfolio , it ' s all about making sure your assets end up where you want them — and minimizing the taxes that might follow .

Because without a plan , your stuff could be divvied up by the government like Doug guessing wildly at trivia: randomly and with questionable results .

## Estate Documents 101: What You Need

The basis of an estate plan is a WILL . This tells probate court how to handle your estate . Some people then have a TRUST to further protect themselves (we ' ll dive into this in the next pages) . Also (and not germaine to tax planning , so we ' ll keep it brief) , people also will often designate a durable power of attorney to handle their financial affairs if they ' re incapacitated , a health care proxy to speak to medical professionals if they ' re unable , and a conservator who takes care of money for any minors in the family .

## How Your Estate Is Taxed

The government (both state and federal) will add up the value of your estate to see how much everything is worth . Your estate will file forms detailing the amounts . Based on how much you have and your state of residence , your estate may owe taxes when you die .

The good news? You receive a Federal estate tax exemption in the USA . You may also gift money to beneficiaries and friends to lower the amount of your estate , but the Federal government also caps those amounts .

section_heading: ## The Big Numbers You Need to Know
text:
## The Big Numbers You Need to Know

Thanks to the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 (TCJA) , we ' ve had some generous estate and gift tax exemptions:

- $15 million for individuals in 2026 .
- $30 million for married couples in 2026 .

That means you can transfer a whole lot of assets—during your lifetime or after you ' re gone — before the IRS gets their cut . On top of that , you can gift up to $19 , 000 annually per person without it even touching that lifetime exemption .

## What This Means for You

If your estate is growing faster than Doug's trivia confidence , now ' s the time to act . Here ' s how to make the most of the current rules:

- 1 . Check Your Estate 's 's Value: If you ' re nearing the $15 million mark , you may need a plan to minimize future tax hits .
- 2 . Gift Strategically: Consider giving assets away now while exemptions are high and annual gift exclusions are in your favor .
- 3 . Work With Pros: A CPA or estate planning attorney can help you understand your options and create a tax-efficient strategy .

section_heading: ## Trusts: Not Just for TV Billionaires
text:
## Trusts: Not Just for TV Billionaires

When you hear " trusts , " you might think of snooty people in monocles . But trusts are a powerful tool for anyone who wants to:

- Transfer Wealth Efficiently: Reduce taxes for your heirs .
- Protect Assets: Safeguard your wealth from creditors or lawsuits .
- Control Distribution: Set rules for when and how your heirs receive their inheritance (because maybe you don ' t want Junior blowing it all on NFTs) .

## Types of Trusts to Consider:

- Revocable Living Trusts: Great for avoiding probate and keeping your estate private . Married couples may use an A/B trust setup to effectively use both people ' s tax exemption .
- Example: It ' s 2026 . Joe & Cheryl have an estate of $32M . They ' re going to owe considerable taxes on this sum . Spouses are allowed to fully inherit their partner ' s assets without a tax becoming due . So when the first between them dies , they set up an " A " trust with this person ' s half of the money so it can pass on to others without being taxed . When the second dies they ' ll form a " B " trust which allows for that spouse ' s credit to be used as well . Joe and Cheryl have effectively eliminated most of the Federal estate tax issue by setting up these trusts ahead of time and using them correctly .
- Irrevocable Trusts: Help minimize estate taxes and protect assets but can ' t be changed once established .
- Specialized Trusts: For specific goals like charitable giving, education funding, or caring for loved ones with special needs .

An estate planning attorney can help you figure out which type of trust makes sense for your situation .

section_heading: ## Gift Tax Planning: Give While You Live
text:
## Gift Tax Planning: Give While You Live

- Annual Gift Exclusion: You can give up to $18,000 per person per year without eating into your lifetime exemption .
- Lifetime Exemption: Use the current $13 . 99 million limit before it drops in 2026 .
- Gifting Assets: Consider gifting appreciated assets like stocks to family members in lower tax brackets—because why let the IRS win?

section_heading: ## Why You Should Care About This Now
text:
## Why You Should Care About This Now

Estate planning isn ' t just about taxes — it ' s about making life easier for the people you care about most . Without a plan , your loved ones might face unnecessary delays , legal headaches , and a bigger tax bill than necessary .

By acting before 2026 , you can lock in today ' s higher exemptions , set up trusts to protect your assets , and ensure that your Benjamins end up where they belong: with the people (or causes) you care about most .

And remember , Stackers: You worked hard to stack those Benjamins . Let ' s make sure they go where you want them to , instead of into Uncle Sam ' s ever-hungry pockets .

## The BIG Lesson

Estate and gift tax planning isn ' t just for the ultra-wealthy — it ' s for anyone who wants to leave a legacy that lasts . Whether you ' re passing down a vintage comic book collection or a family business , the right strategies can make all the difference . So grab your CPA , a hot mug of Mom ' s cocoa , and let ' s get planning. Because keeping your money in the family (or funding a legacy that matters) is always worth it .

section_heading: ## SECTION 8: Managing Income Timing—Make Taxes Work on Your Schedule
text:
## SECTION 8: Managing Income Timing—Make Taxes Work on Your Schedule

When it comes to taxes , timing is everything. Managing when you recognize income or expenses can make a huge difference in how much of your hard-earned money ends up in Uncle Sam ' s pocket — and how much stays with you . This isn ' t about shady loopholes or sneaky tactics; it ' s about being smart with the rules the IRS already has in place .

Let ' s explore how shifting income or expenses , timing bonuses , and deferring capital gains can help you stay in control of your tax situation .

## What is Income Timing?

Income timing is the strategic practice of managing when you receive income or claim deductions . The goal? Reduce your tax liability by keeping your income in lower brackets or taking advantage of credits and deductions at just the right moment .

Think of it as playing financial Tetris — line everything up correctly, y, and you ' ll score big savings .

section_heading: ## Shifting Income or Expenses Between Years
text:
## Shifting Income or Expenses Between Years

Sometimes , a little flexibility in timing can go a long way . Here ' s how to approach it:

## Shifting Income

- Why It Matters: If you expect to be in a lower tax bracket next year, r, consider deferring income until then to reduce your overall tax burden .
- How It Works:
- Freelancers & Small Business Owners: Delay sending invoices until after December 31 to push income into the following year .
- Employees: Defer year-end bonuses (if your employer allows) into January of the next year .
- Retirees: Wait to take distributions from taxable accounts or retirement plans if possible .

## Shifting Expenses

- Why It Matters: If you ' re itemizing deductions and expect higher income or tax rates this year, r, prepaying expenses can increase your deductions when you need them most .
- How It Works:
- Pay January ' s mortgage interest in December .
- Bunch charitable contributions into one year instead of spreading them out .
- Prepay property taxes or medical expenses (as long as they ' re deductible) .

Pro Tip: Be careful not to overdo it. Shifting too much can backfire if it pushes you into a higher bracket in a future year. r. Balance is key!

## Timing Bonuses and Capital Gains

## Timing Bonuses

- Why It Matters: A year-end bonus can bump you into a higher tax bracket if it ' s stacked on top of your regular income .
- How It Works:
- If you ' re close to a bracket threshold , ask your employer to pay your bonus in January instead of December .
- By deferring it to the next calendar year, r, you can spread the tax burden and potentially avoid bracket creep.

Example: You earn $90, 0, 000 annually and receive a $10, 0, 000 bonus in December. r. That extra income pushes you into the 24% tax bracket. But if the bonus is paid in January, y, you stay in the 22% bracket for the current year. r.

## Deferring Capital Gains

- Why It Matters: Selling investments with large gains could push your income into a higher bracket or increase the taxability of Social Security benefits and Medicare premiums .
- How It Works:
- Delay selling appreciated assets until a year when your income is lower .
- Hold assets for more than one year to qualify for long-term capital gains rates , which are typically lower than ordinary income rates .
- Use tax -advantaged accounts like IRAs to defer taxes on gains entirely until withdrawal .

Pro Tip: If you 'r 're charitably inclined, d, consider donating appreciated securities instead of selling them . You 'l 'll avoid capital gains taxes while taking a deduction for the full market value of the gift.

section_heading: ## When Managing Income Timing Makes Sense
text:
## When Managing Income Timing Makes Sense

- Bracket Creep: Avoid jumping into a higher tax bracket by deferring income .
- Income Volatility: If your income varies widely year to year, r, shifting income or expenses can help smooth out your tax liability .
- Anticipated Tax Law Changes: If you expect tax rates to rise (or exemptions to fall) , accelerating income into the current year can be advantageous .

## When to Be Cautious

- 1 . Future Tax Law Uncertainty: If rates are likely to change , you could end up paying more taxes down the road .
- 2 . Impact on Credits and Deductions: Shifting income might phase you out of valuable tax credits or deductions (like education credits or child tax credits) .
- 3 . AMT Risk: If you ' re subject to the Alternative Minimum Tax , timing strategies might not work as expected .

## The BIG Lesson

Managing income timing is like playing chess with your finances . By thinking a few moves ahead , you can stay one step ahead of the tax man . Whether you ' re deferring income , accelerating deductions , or strategically timing bonuses and capital gains , the key is to stay intentional .

Because when it comes to taxes , the best move isn ' t just to file — it ' s to file smarter . So grab your calendar, r, consult a pro , and start making your tax strategy work on your schedule — not Uncle Sam ' s .

section_heading: ## SECTION 9: Maximizing Tax Brackets , Deductions and Credits
text:
## SECTION 9: Maximizing Tax Brackets , Deductions and Credits

It ' s time to chat about the most exciting part of taxes: finding ways to keep more of your hard -earned cash! Okay, y, maybe " exciting" is a stretch , but deductions and credits are like the coupons of the tax world — when you use them right , you get a big discount on your tax bill .

The trick is knowing the difference:

- Deductions reduce your taxable income , which lowers the amount of income tax you owe .
- Credits are even better—they directly reduce the amount of tax you owe , dollar for dollar .

Let ' s dive into the most common deductions and credits you might be leaving on the table and how to use them to your advantage .

section_heading: ## First: Compare Standard Deduction vs Itemized Deductions
text:
## First: Compare Standard Deduction vs Itemized Deductions

Over the last few years , over 90% of individuals filed using the standard deduction . This deduction is defined as:

Standard deduction: a set amount of money the government gives you as a "d "deduction " to simplify the tax planning and filing process .

In 2025 the standard deduction is: $15 , 750 (single) and $23 , 625 (HOH) , $31 , 500 (MFJ)

If you feel that after taking advantage of itemized deductions (listed below) , you will have a larger sum than the standard deduction , you can then show the IRS your list of itemized deductions and qualify for more .

To find out , you ' ll need two things:

- 1 . An understanding of what you could possibly itemize so that comparing the two numbers are worth your time; and
- 2 . The amount this year ' s standard deduction is that you ' ll have to beat .

This coming year, r, the standard deduction has been increased to $15 , 000 for single filers and $30 , 000 for joint filers . These amounts will continue to adjust for future years (and we ' ll show them here in the guide for you!) .

section_heading: ## Special Deductions Available Whether You Itemize Or Not
text:
## Special Deductions Available Whether You Itemize Or Not

NEW! Are you 65 or older? Plan on this for 2025: You ' ll receive an additional new deduction of $6 , 000 per individual . To qualify , you must have a Social Security number and to receive the full deduction earn less than $75 , 000 if single and $150 , 000 for married filers . At that point you can still receive a phased-out deduction with reduces at a 6% rate until it phases out entirely at $175 , 000 for single taxpayers and $250 , 000 for married taxpayers . You receive this deduction EVEN IF YOU ITEMIZE. E. This deduction is scheduled to phase out in 2028 .

NEW! Are you paid in tips or work overtime? Plan on this for 2025: Deduct up to $25 , 000 in tip income or $12 , 500 in overtime . This deduction is scheduled to phase out in 2028 .

section_heading: ## Itemized Deductions: Cutting Your Taxable Income Down to Size
text:
## Itemized Deductions: Cutting Your Taxable Income Down to Size

While the standard deduction is tempting for many taxpayers (and a no-brainer for some , as we explained) , itemizing your deductions can save you big bucks if your qualifying expenses exceed the standard deduction . Here are the big players:

## 1 . Mortgage Interest Deduction

What It Covers: Interest paid on your mortgage for your primary or secondary residence .

## Key Rules:

- You can deduct interest on loans up to $750 , 000 ($375 , 000 if married filing separately) for mortgages taken out after December 15 , 2017 .
- For older loans , the limit is $1 million ($500 , 000 if married filing separately) .

Actionable Tip: If you 'r 're on the fence about buying a home, e, the mortgage interest deduction might help you decide . Just don 't 't let the tax savings be the only reason you buy — remember, r, Doug isn 't 't going to help with the yard work!

2. State and Local Taxes (SALT) Deduction

What It Covers: Property taxes , state income taxes , or sales taxes (you can deduct one or the other) .

## Key Rule:

- In 2025 this deduction cap, formerly $10 , 000 ($5 , 000 if married filing separately) , jumped to $40 , 000 .

## Who Benefits from this change most?

- Tax payers in New York , California , New Jersey, y, and Connecticut who earn six -figures are the most likely to find this helpful .
