## THE SB TAX CALENDAR

## February 1, 2026 – February 1, 2027

Taxes don ' t sneak up on you because they ' re complicated - they sneak up on you because the calendar is sneaky .

This standalone tax calendar is designed to live outside the main Tax Planning & Filing Guide AND in Mom ' s Monster Resource section . Think of it as your fridge‑worthy companion: a simple roadmap of the most important federal tax and tax‑planning deadlines you ' ll face over the next year .

Timeframe covered: February 1

, 2026 through February 1 , 2027

Who it ’s

’s for: Most individual taxpayers , investors , retirees , and small business owners

What it includes: Federal deadlines only (state rules vary - check locally)

If a date applies only to certain people (business owners , retirees , quarterly payers) , we ' ll tell you .

Let ' s walk the calendar .

## February 2026

## February 2, 2026

- Deadline for employers to file W‑2s and most 1099s with the IRS and Social Security Administration .
- If you ' re a worker or contractor and haven 't 't received your forms by now, w, it ' s time to follow up.

## March 2026

## March 16, 2026

- S‑Corporation (1120‑S) and Partnership (1065) tax returns due .
- Deadline to issue Schedule K ‑ 1s to owners and partners .
- Also the deadline to file an extension for these returns .

This date matters even if you personally file in April - K‑1 delays are one of the biggest reasons individual returns get pushed back .

## April 2026 (This Is the Big One)

## April 15, 2026 - Tax Day

## Deadline for:

- Individual federal tax returns (Form 1040)
- First quarter 2026 estimated tax payment (if you pay quarterly)
- C‑Corporation returns (Form 1120)
- Gift tax returns (Form 709)
- Filing Form 4868 for an extension (remember: extension to file , not to pay)

## Also important:

- Last day to contribute to a Traditional IRA or Roth IRA for the 2025 tax year

If you only remember one date on this calendar, r, make it this one .

## June 2026

## June 15, 2026

- Second quarter 2026 estimated tax payment due (self‑employed , investors , side hustlers -this is you) .

## September 2026

## September 15, 2026

- Third quarter 2026 estimated tax payment due
- Deadline for extended S ‑ Corp and Partnership returns (if you filed an extension in March)

This is also a smart month for a tax projection check‑in before year‑end decisions get rushed .

## October 2026

## October 15, 2026

- Deadline to file extended individual tax returns (Form 1040)
- Deadline for extended C ‑ Corporation returns

After this date , penalties and interest start to stack and not in the fun way .

## December 2026 (Quiet , but Powerful)

## December 31, 2026

- Deadline to complete Roth conversions that count for the 2026 tax year
- Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) must be taken by year‑end if you ' re already subject to them
- Last chance for most year‑end tax planning moves (loss harvesting, charitable strategies , income timing)

Nothing to file today - but plenty of consequences if you miss it .

## January 2027

## January 15, 2027

- Fourth quarter 2026 estimated tax payment due (individuals)

This payment closes the loop on the 2026 tax year .

## February 1, 2027

You ' ve officially entered a new tax season cycle .

## By now:

- You should be receiving W‑2s and 1099s again
- Your 2026 planning decisions are locked in
- Your only remaining lever is how you file not what you did

Time to flip back to the main Tax Planning & Filing Guide and get organized .

## Final Reminder

Deadlines don ' t care how busy you are , but your future tax bill absolutely does . Set reminders to flag these dates . I prefer to set dates early, y, so I have time if there are complications (and there are always complications) .

section_heading: ## Welcome To the SB/College Investor COLLEGE PLANNING GUIDE
text:
0 Copyright © 2026 Stacking Benjamins LLC

## Welcome To the SB/College Investor COLLEGE PLANNING GUIDE

Congrats on purchasing a guide that we know will save you LOTS of money and headaches! Because college planning truly is two different types of actions (one largely on saving for college and the other on what to do when you ' re on final approach or attending) , this guide is going to be useful at two different times: early on as you get the ball rolling, and later, r, when you ' re ready to dive into financial aid programs and college funding options .

We kick off this guide with a checklist on the next page for one reason: 99% of you don ' t want to read this entire guide , and (here ' s some good news) there ' s no reason to even try . Finish the check list immediately to lock in all of the important and " hot " stuff . Later, r, dive into individual sections as you ' re either in " learning mode " or working through a specific problem .

Each month we ' ll send you an email with updates . Again , no reason to go check them out right away if they don ' t apply to you . These emails are just to let you know that when the time comes , we ' ve updated everything to be current and you don ' t have to worry that this guide is out of date . .

So , welcome to our college planning guide . For those of you who don ' t know us , I ' m Joe Saul -Sehy . He ' s Robert Farrington . And over there is the amazing Kevin Bailey . (Kevin says " Hello! " ) . First , none of us are tax pros or financial advisors . We are all guys who ' ve worked in the financial industry for collectively over 75 years . We ' ve also received help on this guide from college admissions officers , financial aid pros , and plenty of former college students who ' ve gone through the process . Additionally, y, before we sent this out , we ' ve sent it to influencers in the college planning space . You ' ve heard many of them on our podcast and our YouTube channel .

That said , we need to begin with an important disclaimer: this guide will NOT provide specific tax or investing advice . You should consult professional help for your individual tax or investing questions .

section_heading: ## Welcome To the SB/College Investor COLLEGE PLANNING GUIDE
text:
Enough will the disclaimers . Let ' s get you rolling toward an effective college planning strategy!

Let 's 's begin!

Joe, e, Robert, t, & Kevin

## QUICK ACTION CHECKLIST:

## Stacking Your College Plan Like a Pro

Before diving into this guide , knock out these quick , high-impact actions . These moves will immediately set you up for success , save you money, y, and make the whole college process way less painful .

When college planning , timing is everything , and sooner equals better. r. Do these now, w, and you ' ll be ahead of most families who wait until the panic stage .

## 🟢 STAGE 1: Middle School & Earlier


## Foundation Mode: Savings + Money Mindset

At this stage, e, college still feels far away, y, which is exactly why this is the best time to start . You don 't 't need to know the perfect school, l, the perfect major, r, or the perfect career path yet . The goal is to build the savings engine, e, start casual conversations about money and careers, s, and make college feel like something your family talks about before it becomes an emergency. y.

The families who start here don 't 't have to sprint later. r. Small contributions, s, early exposure, e, and simple conversations can create a huge advantage by the time high school decisions start to matter

## Money Moves

- Check your state ’ s 529 plan options . Some states give tax breaks , some don ’ t .

- If your state doesn ’ t offer the best option , compare stronger out - of - state 529 plans . The College Investor has a tool that can help you find your state ’ s plan .

- Open a 529 plan if you haven ’ t already . Even if college is next year, r, it may still help you capture tax benefits .

- Check your state ’ s tax deduction rules . Some states offer deductions for 529 contributions .

- In a pinch , see if a Roth IRA could work as a backup college savings option . Yes , it ’ s for retirement , but in limited situations it can double as a college fund . We only like this in extreme cases , and we recommend speaking with a tax professional first .

- Avoid saving large amounts in the student ’ s name (UTMA/UGMA) unless you understand how it can affect financial aid .

- Don ’ t sacrifice your own retirement savings to fund college . Your student can borrow for school . You can ’ t borrow for retirement .

## Education & Exposure

## Parent Prep

- Start building a list of target schools . Focus on schools that offer the best mix of educational opportunity, y, career outcomes , and affordability .

- Begin looking up merit scholarships at those schools . Some are automatic if the student meets GPA , test score , or academic requirements .

- Research college student discounts and freebies early . Schools often share discounts on books , software , phone plans , gym memberships , and local services .

- Run the numbers on a “ what if ” college cost scenario . Use online calculators to estimate the real cost of attendance at schools you ’ re considering.

- Use each school ’ s net price calculator . These tools estimate tuition , room and board , transportation , books , fees , and other costs .

- Have a family money talk about college expectations . Who ’ s paying for what? What ’ s the backup plan? What ’ s off the table?

- Set up a budget using realistic college costs . Include hidden expenses like books , fees , travel , supplies , and move - in costs .

- Open a high-yield savings account for college expenses . Even if it ’ s only for dorm supplies , travel , or application fees , start building the cash bucket now .

Goal: Build the savings engine and normalize talking about money. y.

## 🟡 STAGE 2: Early High School

## Positioning Mode: Academic & Scholarship Setup

Freshman and sophomore years feel early, y, but this is when the scholarship profile starts taking shape .

## Academic Strategy

- Make a list of target schools and begin thinking about academic fit , career fit , and financial fit .

- Find out whether AP, P, IB , or dual enrollment credits may transfer to colleges your student might consider . Some schools accept them , some don ’ t . Know before you assume .

## Financial Prep

- Check your Student Aid Index estimate using a free calculator so you can see what colleges may think your family can afford .

- Learn the difference between need - based aid and merit - based aid . You may qualify for more than you think .

- See whether your state offers free college programs or tuition discounts . Some states cover tuition if students meet specific requirements .

- Find out whether an employer or relative can contribute . Some workplaces offer tuition benefits for children of employees , and family gifts can affect aid .

## Scholarship Prep

- Start a scholarship spreadsheet . Track deadlines , requirements , award amounts , and submission status .

- Look up merit scholarships at target schools . Some scholarships are automatic if your student meets certain requirements .

Goal: Build the academic and financial profile that unlocks merit money later. r.

## 🟠 STAGE 3: Junior Year


## Strategy Mode: Cost + Admissions Alignment

Junior year is when the family should stop talking about college in theory and start comparing real schools with real costs .

## Testing & Academics

- Confirm whether AP, P, IB , or dual enrollment credits transfer at each target school .

- Look up graduation rates and retention rates for every college on your list . A cheap school isn ’ t cheap if it takes six years to finish .

## Financial Fit Work

- Create a college list with a “ financial fit ” column . Rank schools by cost , aid , scholarships , graduation rate , career outcomes , and ROI , not just prestige .

- Run net price calculators for every school on the list .

- Compare in - state vs . out-of - state costs . Sometimes out - of - state schools offer scholarships that make them less expensive than an in-state option .

- Compare public vs . private schools . Private school scholarships can narrow the cost gap or eliminate the “ public is cheaper ” advantage altogether .

- Consider the two - year transfer strategy . Check which local community colleges have articulation agreements with universities .

- Research state tuition alliances or reciprocity programs that may offer lower out-of-state tuition .

## Scholarship Execution

- Keep building the scholarship spreadsheet with due dates , requirements , award amounts , and application links .

- Look for local and niche scholarships . These often have less competition than big national awards .

Goal: Align school choice with financial reality before applications go out .

## 🔴 STAGE 4: Senior Year

## Execution Mode: Deadlines + Dollars

Senior year is where organization can directly affect how much college costs .

## Financial Aid Prep

- Create StudentAid . gov accounts , formerly FSA IDs , for the student and any required parent contributor .

- Create these accounts early, y, even if you don ’ t think you ’ ll qualify for aid . You ’ ll be ready when deadlines hit .

- Check your Student Aid Index again before filing financial aid forms .

## Fall: The Critical Window

- File FAFSA as early as possible after it opens .

- Confirm whether any schools on your list require the CSS Profile .

- Track every school ’ s financial aid deadline separately . Don ’ t assume all deadlines are the same .

## Winter

- Continue applying for scholarships after college applications are submitted .

- Compare aid offers carefully as they arrive . Look at net price , not sticker price .

## Spring

- Compare packages side by side using a spreadsheet .

- Appeal aid offers if your financial situation changed or if another school offered more .

- Make the final decision based on affordability, y, graduation odds , career fit , and total debt .

## After Acceptance

- Find out whether the school offers tuition refund insurance . If illness or an emergency forces a withdrawal , this may help recover some costs .

Goal: Reduce borrowing before anyone signs anything .


## 🔵 STAGE 5: Starting College

## Optimization Mode: Don

’ t Blow It Now

Getting accepted is only half the battle . Now the job is to avoid unnecessary debt and sloppy spending .

## Money Setup

- Set up a real college budget . Tuition is only one piece .

- Open or use a high-yield savings account for non-tuition expenses , emergency costs , books , travel , and supplies .

- Look up work-study and part-time job options early . Some campus jobs fill quickly .

- Check credit history and score if the student is old enough . If borrowing is needed later, r, a clean credit history helps .

- Review official credit reports for free through AnnualCreditReport . com .

## Smart Spending

- Research college student discounts and freebies . Look beyond campus to banks , software companies , local businesses , phone plans , and transportation .

- Be careful with credit card offers tied to free shirts , free food , or campus swag. Just ask Joe how that can turn out .

- Find out which books are actually required before buying them .

Goal: Graduate with the least debt possible .

## 🟣 STAGE 6: Graduation & First Job

## Launch Mode: Protect the Investment

This stage is less about getting into college and more about making sure the degree actually improves the student 's 's financial life .

## Financial Foundation

- Revisit the total amount borrowed and compare it to expected first-year salary .

- Build a starter emergency fund before aggressively attacking student loans .

- Start retirement contributions as soon as possible , especially if there ’ s an employer match .

- Avoid lifestyle inflation during the first year after graduation .

Goal: Turn the degree into financial momentum .

section_heading: ## 🚨 LATE START: No Savings , Junior or Senior Year
text:
## 🚨 LATE START: No Savings , Junior or Senior Year

## Triage Mode: Cut Cost First , Catch Up Second

If college is close and savings are thin, n, don 't 't panic . The strategy shifts from "s "save more " to "b "borrow less . "

## Immediate Actions

- Open a 529 anyway if your state offers a tax deduction and the timing still makes sense .

- Run net price calculators immediately for every school under consideration .

- Prioritize FAFSA timing. File as early as possible .

- Focus on schools where your student ’ s academic profile may earn merit aid .

- Compare in-state , out-of - state , public , private , and community college transfer options .

- Confirm whether AP, P, IB , or dual enrollment credits transfer before choosing a school .

- Look for free college programs , tuition discounts , and state - based aid .

- Have an honest family conversation about what you can and cannot pay .

Goal: Reduce the cost of attendance before trying to catch up on savings .
