section_heading: ## Why college costs are outrageous (and what you can do about it)
text:
## Why college costs are outrageous (and what you can do about it)

If you ' ve ever looked at a college ' s price tag and thought , that must include a beachfront resort and daily lobster dinners, you ' re not alone . Over the past few decades , tuition has skyrocketed faster than your uncle ' s crypto investments in 2021 (before he stopped talking about them) .

So why is college so expensive? There are SO many reasons …

- Admin bloat: Universities now have more vice presidents than a bad corporate office sitcom .
- Fancy dorms & gyms: Because nothing says higher education like a lazy river and rock climbing wall .
- Financial aid shenanigans: Schools charge more so they can " discount " tuition later . It ' s like Kohl ' s cash , but for student loans .
- Demand & loans: Colleges charge what people are willing (or forced) to pay . Federal loans make sure demand never dries up.
- Inflation and salary increases: Good professors cost money and students (and parents) often express that they want smaller class sizes .
- Declining federal and state funding for higher education
- More students pursuing STEM: Higher cost programs offered by schools are popular . Liberal arts curriculum offerings are often less expensive (you need a professor/instructor , projectors , and students . For computer science , engineering , health , and other STEM areas you often need labs , computers , high-speed processing capability , and more . Plus instructors in these fields often can command higher salaries .
- Beautiful old buildings look incredible , but are a bear to maintain . This costs ever-increasing amounts of money .

So what does this have to do with you? Controlling your college costs means thinking differently about this experience . Time to put on our Stacker cap so we get out of college alive (and solvent)!

## Try these ideas:

- Avoid prestige inflation: Fancy names don ' t always mean better outcomes . Research what grads actually earn .
- Look at ROI: Compare degrees and potential salaries . Engineering? Solid . Puppetry studies? Maybe not .
- Consider community college: A cheaper way to knock out core credits before transferring.
- Find schools that give merit aid: Some private schools cost less than public schools once discounts kick in .

Want to learn more? Listen to the show we did on how to pay for college:

Why college costs are outrageous (and what you can do about it)

section_heading: ## Should you even go? (And other heretical thoughts)
text:
## Should you even go? (And other heretical thoughts)

It ' s heresy to question the sacred journey to a four-year degree , but let ' s be real: college isn ' t for everyone . Some of the best -paying jobs today don ' t require it (hello , trade schools , apprenticeships , and self -taught coders) .

## When college makes sense:

- You want to be a doctor, r, lawyer, r, engineer, r, or something that requires a degree .
- You love learning, and the ROI checks out … or you ' re financially independent and the ROI doesn ' t matter .
- Your employer is paying for it (lucky you) .

## When you should hit pause:

- You have no idea what you want to do but feel pressured to go .
- You ' re considering majoring in something vague like " general studies . "
- You ' re about to take on six figures of debt without a plan to pay it off .

Want more? Listen to our show from FinCon 2017: Should you even go?

## STACKER ACTION PLAN:

- Explore alternatives — trade schools , apprenticeships , boot camps , or straight-up working for a year .
- Try a gap year (but a productive one) — work , save money, y, and test -drive a few careers .
- Find out if your dream job requires a degree — many high-paying jobs don ' t!

section_heading: ## Your high school playbook: laying the foundation early
text:
## Your high school playbook: laying the foundation early

You already know that high school isn ' t just about surviving awkward prom photos and figuring out locker combinations . It ' s your chance to earn free college credits , set yourself up for scholarships , and make future -you really, y, really grateful . So , what should you be focused on now? Luckily, y, the list isn ' t long:

section_heading: ## How to win at high school (for college planning):
text:
## How to win at high school (for college planning):

- Take advanced classes—AP, P, IB , and dual enrollment can save thousands on college credits .
- Get good grades (but don ' t obsess) — GPA matters , but so does the whole package .
- Do strategic extracurriculars — be involved , but don ' t join 12 clubs just for the resume .
- Prep for standardized tests (if required) — a higher SAT/ACT score = more merit aid .
- Start college & scholarship applications early — senior -year -you will thank freshman -year -you .

Want more? Check out our Funding College Through Micro Scholarships Show:

High School Playbook

## STACKER ACTION PLAN:

- Check if your state offers free college credits in high school — many do!
- Start a scholarship list now — many awards are for freshmen and sophomores , not just seniors .
- Make connections with teachers & counselors—they ' ll write your recommendation letters later .

section_heading: ## The family money huddle: talking college costs without tears
text:
## The family money huddle: talking college costs without tears

Nothing ruins a Thanksgiving dinner quite like realizing nobody in the family has any idea how to pay for college . It ' s time for the family money huddle around college — where everyone gets real about expectations .

section_heading: ## The college money questions you need to ask:
text:
## The college money questions you need to ask:

- Parents: How much can (or will) we contribute?
- Student: What are your dream schools , and do they make financial sense?
- Loans: Who ' s borrowing what , and how will repayment work?

section_heading: ## How to make this a productive (and tear-free) chat:
text:
## How to make this a productive (and tear-free) chat:

- Start early: the earlier you talk , the more options you have .
- Be honest: if parents can ' t contribute , don ' t pretend . Look for alternatives .
- Focus on solutions: scholarships , savings , part -time jobs , and smart school choices .

## STACKER ACTION PLAN:

- Schedule the college money talk this month — set expectations and avoid last-minute panic .
- Get everyone on the same page about loans — who ' s borrowing? How much is too much?
- Start FAFSA & financial aid research now—the earlier you apply, y, the better .

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: Your college plan starts now
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: Your college plan starts now

The key to college planning is starting before it feels urgent . The sooner you act , the more options (and money) you ' ll have .


## Take action today:

- Research the cost of your dream schools .
- Check your state ' s college savings plans .
- Set up the family money huddle ™ .

section_heading: ## CHAPTER 2: How To Stack College Savings
text:
## CHAPTER 2: How To Stack College Savings

## Investing without selling a kidney

If you ' ve ever heard the phrase , " saving for college is like running a marathon , " you know that ' s a terrible analogy — because in a marathon , at least you know when it ' s over . College costs keep rising like your neighborhood HOA fees , and unless you have a secret vault of gold bars hidden behind the water heater, r, you ' re going to need a strategy .

Let ' s talk about the best ways to stack your college savings before you resort to selling plasma or entering a high-stakes poker tournament .

section_heading: ## 529 plans, Roth IRAs, and that coffee can in the backyard
text:
## 529 plans, Roth IRAs, and that coffee can in the backyard

## Where to put your college savings (besides under the mattress)

Saving for college is not as simple as throwing money into a checking account and hoping for the best . (If it were , this chapter would be a single sentence . ) Instead , there are specialized , tax-advantaged accounts designed to make your dollars work harder — so that you don ' t have to .

## The 529 plan: the heavyweight champ

- Tax-free growth & withdrawals: Like a Roth IRA , but for education .
- State tax benefits: Many states give you a tax deduction for contributions .
- High contribution limits: You can stash way more here than in a Roth IRA .
- New bonus: You can now roll over unused 529 money to a Roth IRA — which means you don ' t have to worry about over-saving.


Stacker Pro Tip: Even if your kid is already in high school, l, contributing to a 529 can still get you state tax benefits. Some states let you deduct contributions and take the money out soon after. r. (Read the fine print first!)

section_heading: ## Roth IRAs: the sneaky college savings hack
text:
## Roth IRAs: the sneaky college savings hack

- Tax-free growth: Contributions can be withdrawn tax-free anytime .
- Flexibility: If junior gets a scholarship (or joins the circus) , the money can stay for your retirement .
- No impact on FAFSA: Since it ' s considered your retirement money, y, it doesn ' t count against financial aid as much as a 529 .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you qualify, y, a Roth IRA is a great backup savings vehicle — use it for retirement, college, e, or that midlife crisis road trip later. r. For this reason, n, we do NOT like it as a first choice for college planning . It works so well in other areas that we prefer to save this money for other opportunities .

section_heading: ## The backyard coffee can (and other bad ideas)
text:
## The backyard coffee can (and other bad ideas)

- Regular savings accounts: Low interest means you ' re losing money to inflation .
- Cash under the mattress Might work , but only if you don ' t have a particularly enterprising toddler .
- Whole life insurance for college: Agents love to sell it , but it ' s a bad deal 99% of the time

section_heading: ## Coverdell ESAs and other fancy-sounding accounts
text:
## Coverdell ESAs and other fancy-sounding accounts

The Coverdell education savings account (ESA) is like a 529 ' s underappreciated cousin—similar, r, but with some quirks .

- Tax-free growth & withdrawals: Just like a 529 .
- More investment options: Unlike 529s , you can invest in individual stocks , not just funds , and you ' re not limited to your state ' s choice of investment provider
- Low contribution limit: You can only put in $2 , 000 per year, r, per child .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you want more control over investments (and don 't 't mind the lower contribution limits), a Coverdell ESA can complement a 529 . This money may also be used for KK12 education expenses.

section_heading: ## UTMAs & UGMAs: the "oops, I gave my kid too much power" accounts
text:
## UTMAs & UGMAs: the "oops, I gave my kid too much power" accounts

These custodial accounts let you save money for your child , but …

- They count against financial aid more than a 529 .
- Your kid gets full control at 18 or 21 (depending on the state) .
- They can spend it on anything. (Even a jet ski . Do you want your kid buying a jet ski?)


Stacker Pro Tip: Use UTMAs/UGMAs only if you 'r 're okay with your kid deciding how to spend it. Also, o, you 'd 'd better be sure you 'r 're not going to receive financial aid if you have money in these vehicles because the "t "tax " against financial aid considerations is very, y, very high .

section_heading: ## What if you started late? The fast-track plan to catch up
text:
## What if you started late? The fast-track plan to catch up

If your kid is already in high school and you just realized you have exactly $7 . 42 saved for college , don ' t panic . You still have options .

## How to play catch-up

- Use a 529 anyway: Some states let you contribute and get a tax deduction even if you withdraw the money soon after .
- Boost savings with windfalls: Tax refund? Bonus? Side hustle money? Dump it in .
- Get the student involved: Part -time work , scholarships , and community college can help.
- Prioritize FAFSA & merit aid: If you can ' t save much , reducing the bill is just as good .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you 'r 're late to the game, e, focus on tax breaks, free money (scholarships), and reducing costs (community college, e, CLEP tests, etc . ) .

section_heading: ## Grandparents, windfalls, and other unexpected helpers (or hurdles)
text:
## Grandparents, windfalls, and other unexpected helpers (or hurdles)

Sometimes , well -meaning relatives want to help with college , but how they help matters .

## Grandparent help: the right way

- Contribute to a parent-owned 529: Keeps it off the FAFSA radar .
- Wait until the last two years of college to give money: FAFSA looks at past income , so delaying payments can help financial aid eligibility .

The FAFSA trap: If a grandparent directly pays tuition or gives money, y, it can count as student income, e, which reduces financial aid eligibility a lot.

## Scholarship & unexpected windfall strategy

If your student scores big on scholarships or gets a surprise inheritance , use it wisely:

- Pay for education expenses first .
- Put extra money into a Roth IRA for their future (up to allowed limits) .
- Avoid stashing cash in the student ' s name (bad for FAFSA) .

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: Stack early, stack smart
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: Stack early, stack smart

Saving for college doesn ' t mean sacrificing your own financial future . The best strategy is to start early, y, use tax-advantaged accounts , and prioritize smart spending over blind saving.

## Take action today:

- Open (or review) a 529 plan and check for state tax benefits .
- See if a Roth IRA makes sense for college savings .
- Start a " catch -up " plan if you ' re getting a late start .

Coming Up Next: Navigating the college admissions circus (without losing your mind) .

## CHAPTER 3: The College Admissions Circus

## Jumping Through Hoops Without Tripping

Welcome to the big top—where admissions officers act as ringmasters , students juggle extracurriculars , parents walk the financial tightrope , and standardized tests breathe fire into your stress levels . If you ' ve ever felt like college applications are a weird mix of strategy, y, endurance , and outright wizardry, y, you ' re not wrong.

But don ' t worry — this chapter will help you navigate the chaos , skip the unnecessary stress , and get into a great school without faking a rowing career or hiring a consultant who costs more than a year of tuition .

section_heading: ## Extracurriculars, Essays, and Other High-Stakes Juggling Acts
text:
## Extracurriculars, Essays, and Other High-Stakes Juggling Acts

Contrary to popular belief , you don ' t need to be a 4 . 0 student who also rescues puppies , codes apps , and plays violin at Carnegie Hall to get into a great college .

## The Extracurricular Myth

A lot of students (and parents) think they need to do all the things — sports , clubs , volunteer work , musical instruments , student government , interpretive dance , etc . But admissions officers don ' t care about a laundry list of activities . They care about what you actually did and if you stuck with it .


Stacker Pro Tip: Quality over quantity. y. Two years leading a robotics club beats four months of 12 different clubs.

section_heading: ## What Admissions Officers Actually Look For in Activities
text:
## What Admissions Officers Actually Look For in Activities

- Commitment and leadership: Long-term involvement with increasing responsibility .
- Impact: Did you start a project? Grow a club? Raise money? Solve a problem?
- Passion and focus: Doing something because you love it , not because it " looks good . "

Best Bet: Pick two or three solid activities you enjoy and go deep . Start a project, lead a team, m, or make a real impact.

section_heading: ## Essays: The "Sell Yourself Without Sounding Like a Robot" Section
text:
## Essays: The "Sell Yourself Without Sounding Like a Robot" Section

This is where students stress out the most , trying to sound unique , brilliant , and emotionally moving—all in 650 words or less .

## What Not to Do:

- Write a sob story designed to impress . (Admissions officers have seen them all . )
- List achievements instead of telling a story . (Your resume already does that . )
- Use a thesaurus to sound smarter . (No one naturally says , " My journey was replete with serendipitous revelations . " )

## What Works:

- A personal story: Something real , authentic , and specific .
- A lesson learned: Growth and self -reflection go a long way .
- A strong hook: Start with an attention-grabber that isn ' t , " Since I was a child … "


Stacker Pro Tip: Your essay doesn 't 't have to be dramatic—it just has to be authentic . The best essays sound like a conversation , not a TED talk .

section_heading: ## The SAT vs. ACT Smackdown: Which Test to Take (If Any)
text:
## The SAT vs. ACT Smackdown: Which Test to Take (If Any)

Ah , standardized test — the bane of every student ' s existence . But do you even need them anymore? And if so , which one should you take?

## The Test -Optional Revolution

Many colleges have gone test-optional (meaning you don ' t have to submit scores) , but before you ditch the SAT, T, check the fine print:

- Some schools still prefer scores for scholarships and merit aid .
- Certain competitive programs require them (engineering, pre-med , etc . ) .
- Test -optional doesn ' t mean test-blind—if your scores are great , they can help.


Stacker Pro Tip: If you 'r 're a strong test taker, r, submit your scores. If you bombed them, m, look at test-optional schools.

| Feature           | SAT                                           | ACT                                          |
|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Math focus        | More algebra-heavy                            | Includes more advanced math (trigonometry)   |
| Science section   | Nope                                          | Yep (but no actual science knowledge needed) |
| Time per question | More relaxed                                  | Faster-paced                                 |
| Best for …        | Strong math students and slower test - takers | Fast thinkers and strong English skills      |


Stacker Pro Tip: Take a practice test for both . Choose the one that feels easier—and focus on that. Check which test is accepted at your target school(s) .

section_heading: ## Do You Even Need an Admissions Coach? (Hint: Probably Not)
text:
## Do You Even Need an Admissions Coach? (Hint: Probably Not)

Let ' s be real: college admissions coaching is a $2 billion industry, y, mostly designed to make rich parents feel better about their kids ' chances .

## When You Don ' t Need a Coach

- If your student is applying to solid schools with reasonable acceptance rates .
- If they ' re organized and can follow application instructions .
- If they don ' t have $10 , 000 lying around to pay for one .

section_heading: ## When a Coach Might Be Helpful
text:
## When a Coach Might Be Helpful

- If you ' re aiming for Ivy League or super-competitive programs and need an edge .
- If your student struggles with writing essays or organizing applications .
- If you just need a one-time strategy session (some counselors charge hourly) .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you want help without the price tag, g, check your high school 's 's guidance office, e, free online resources, or college application workshops.

section_heading: ## How to Impress Without Selling Your Soul (or Faking a Rowing Career)
text:
## How to Impress Without Selling Your Soul (or Faking a Rowing Career)

You don ' t need to be an Olympic-level athlete , a tech genius , or the founder of a nonprofit that saves endangered squirrels to impress admissions officers . You just need a real , thoughtful , well -rounded application that shows effort and potential .

## College Application Green Flags (What Works)

- A cohesive story: Your extracurriculars , essay, y, and letters of recommendation all support a theme (like leadership, creativity, y, or innovation) .
- Strong academics , even if not perfect: Admissions officers look for consistent effort , not just straight A' A' s .
- A clear reason why you want to be there , specifically. y. — Colleges love to know why you want to be there .

section_heading: ## College Application Red Flags (What Hurts You)
text:
## College Application Red Flags (What Hurts You)

- Generic essays: If your " Why this college " essay works for multiple schools , you ' re doing it wrong.
- Overly polished applications: Admissions officers can spot when a parent (or consultant or artificial intelligence) wrote everything.
- Fake or overinflated activities: Schools check . Don ' t say you ' re fluent in Mandarin if you can ' t order dumplings .


Stacker Pro Tip: Be real, l, be strategic, c, and don 't 't try to game the system . Colleges want humans, not perfectly engineered applications.

## FINAL THOUGHT: You've Got This

Admissions can feel overwhelming, but it ' s not about being perfect — it ' s about being the best version of yourself on paper . Stay organized , play to your strengths , and focus on what actually matters (not what social media makes you think you need) .

## Take action today:

- Narrow down your top-choice schools .
- Start a rough draft of your main essay .
- Take a practice SAT and ACT to see which suits you best .

Coming Up Next: The Free Money Treasure Hunt—How to Get Scholarships and Financial Aid Without Losing Your Mind .

## CHAPTER 4: Financial Aid 101

## The free money treasure hunt

College costs a fortune , but here ' s the good news: there ' s free money out there (if you know where to look) . Unfortunately, y, most students (and parents) approach financial aid the way Indiana Jones approached ancient ruins: blindly hoping for treasure while dodging bureaucratic booby traps .

This chapter is your treasure map, minus the snakes (we , like Indy, y, hate snakes) . We ' ll show you how to fill out the FAFSA without losing your mind , decode the difference between need -based and merit -based aid , and uncover hidden scholarships that don ' t require you to be a straight-A student or an Olympic athlete . Let ' s start stacking some free cash .

FAFSA: How to fill it out without losing your mind (or your patience)


Ah , the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) . . . a government form so infamous it might as well have its own horror movie . But here ' s the deal: if you don ' t fill this out , you ' re leaving free money on the table . There ' s simply no getting around it .

section_heading: ## When should I fill out the FAFSA?
text:
## When should I fill out the FAFSA?

As you ' ll see , financial aid is first come , first served . This means that both institutions and the government will evaluate your paperwork once they receive it based on the merits of your own situation . When the money is gone , it ' s gone . The first day you can send in your paperwork this year is October 1 , 2026 .

## Why the FAFSA matters

- Qualifies you for grants (a . k . a . free money you don ' t have to pay back) .
- Gets you access to federal student loans (which have better terms than private loans) .
- Colleges use it to determine need-based aid .
- Some scholarships require it , even for merit aid .

section_heading: ## Why you NEED to fill out the FAFSA even if you think you don ' t qualify
text:
## Why you NEED to fill out the FAFSA even if you think you don ' t qualify

- If a school costs more than $65 , 000 , you MAY still qualify for aid , even if your family has middle class or upper class income .
- The FAFSA will be required for MOST other scholarships (these programs don ' t want to give you money until they know that the government denied you) .
- Even if you are only going for merit-based scholarships , the institution may still require the FAFSA before awarding you any money .
- The FAFSA may improve your chances of being accepted into a school . Studies have shown (and enrollment offices know this) that people who don ' t fill out a FAFSA are less likely to attend a school .
- The FAFSA is required to ask for low interest loans (and you ' re automatically qualified for many of these loans once you ' ve filled it out) .
- The FAFSA is required before parents can apply for a federal parent PLUS loan .

Here is a video on how to fill out the FAFSA form , step by step from our friends at The College Investor:

section_heading: ## How to fill out the FAFSA without wanting to scream
text:
## How to fill out the FAFSA without wanting to scream

- Create an FSA ID early. y. Both the student and the parent need one . Do this before starting the form .
- Gather your documents first . The FAFSA uses prior-prior year income , so for 2026 you ' ll need your 2024 tax returns , W -2s , and bank statements .
- File as early as possible . The FAFSA opens 10/1 , and some aid is first -come , first -served .
- Double -check your info . Even tiny mistakes can delay your application .


Stacker Pro Tip: If your family 's 's financial situation changed drastically from what's on last year 's 's tax return , call the school 's 's financial aid office . They can adjust your aid package based on new circumstances.

section_heading: ## Are there other avenues besides the FAFSA I should explore?
text:
## Are there other avenues besides the FAFSA I should explore?

Many states have their own financial aid applications that you should fill out immediately AFTER filling out the FAFSA form . Here is a current list:

- Arkansas: Requires an additional scholarship application .
- California: Offers the California Dream Act Application (CADAA) for undocumented and nonresident students who meet specific eligibility requirements .
- Delaware: Requires students to complete a state-level application to be considered for certain programs .
- Iowa: Has a separate state application process for students to transfer their FAFSA information into .
- Minnesota: Students who used to transfer FAFSA information directly may now need to complete additional forms for Minnesota state aid .
- Mississippi: Features a separate application for state aid , which is required in addition to the FAFSA .
- Nevada: Has a distinct state application process for financial aid .
- New Jersey: Requires students to complete a separate application to be considered for state financial aid programs .
- New York: Offers the New York State Tuition Assistance Program (TAP) and has an " Alternate Eligibility Path " for certain students .
- Oregon: The state has its own promise programs for financial aid .
- Pennsylvania: Uses a separate application for students to be considered for state financial aid programs .
- Vermont: Requires a separate state application for financial aid programs .
- Washington: Offers its own pathway for residents to access state financial aid .
- West Virginia: Has a distinct state application process for financial aid .

Here ' s a great resource for individual state and alternate financial aid programs:

https://www . nasfaa . \_ financial \_ aid \_

org/state programs Stacker Pro Tip: If your family 's 's financial situation changed drastically from what's on your tax return , call the school 's 's financial aid office . (Remember, r, you 'r 're using priorr-p -prior year income for financial aid, d, so in 2026 this would be 2024 income) . They can adjust your aid package based on new circumstances.

section_heading: ## Need-Based vs. Merit-Based Aid: The Fine Print That Actually Matters
text:
## Need-Based vs. Merit-Based Aid: The Fine Print That Actually Matters

Not all financial aid is created equal . Some is based on financial need , while other aid is given just for being awesome at school , sports , or random trivia (yes , there are scholarships for Star Trek fans) .

section_heading: ## Need -based aid: The " you actually need help " money
text:
## Need -based aid: The " you actually need help " money

- Pell grants . Federal grant for low-income students (free money!) .
- Work -study . Part -time campus jobs that help with expenses .
- Institutional need -based aid . Some colleges give extra grants based on FAFSA results .
- State grants . Many states offer tuition assistance for in-state students .


Stacker Pro Tip: Even if you think you won 't 't qualify for need-based aid, d, fill out the FAFSA anyway. y. Middle -class families often get aid, d, too .

section_heading: ## Merit -based aid: The " you ' re impressive " money
text:
## Merit -based aid: The " you ' re impressive " money

- Academic scholarships . Based on GPA and test scores .
- Athletic scholarships . For those who can throw, w, run , or swim like a champ.
- Talent -based scholarships . Music , art , dance , and even esports scholarships exist .
- Private scholarships . From companies , nonprofits , and weirdly specific contests .


Stacker Pro Tip: Some private colleges give automatic merit aid to good students. A "$ "$60, 0, 000 " tuition bill might actually be $30, 0, 000 after discounts.

section_heading: ## CSS Profile and other forms that sound made up but aren't
text:
## CSS Profile and other forms that sound made up but aren't

If you thought FAFSA was the only form you had to deal with , welcome to the CSS Profile . This is the financial aid form used by many private colleges to decide how much extra money they ' ll give you .

section_heading: ## What you need to know about the CSS Profile
text:
## What you need to know about the CSS Profile

- Used by 200+ colleges (mostly private schools) .
- More detailed than FAFSA (it asks about home equity, y, family businesses , etc . ) .
- Costs money to file ($25 for the first school , $16 for each additional) .
- Deadlines vary — some are earlier than FAFSA deadlines .


Stacker Pro Tip: If your student is applying to both FAFSA and CSS Profile schools, check deadlines early. y. Some schools won 't 't give aid if you miss the deadline (even by a day) .

section_heading: ## Scholarships: The art of getting paid to go to school
text:
## Scholarships: The art of getting paid to go to school

Scholarships are free money, y, and the best part? There are scholarships for everything. Left -handed? There ' s a scholarship. Love ducks? Yep, there ' s one for that , too .

## Where to find scholarships

- Your student 's 's school . Many high schools have local scholarships available .
- College websites . Schools list their own merit -based and need -based awards .


Stacker Pro Tip: While private school tuition and fees appear on a "r "retail " level as much higher than those of public schools, many offer far more scholarship opportunities. They don 't 't just hand these out to anyone, e, though! You 'l 'll need to ask questions, apply, y, and diligently follow up to find (in many cases) lots of available money!

- Scholarship search sites such as Fastweb, b, Scholarships360, 0, and Cappex
- Unusual and niche scholarships . There are scholarships for tall people , knitters , and even duct tape prom outfits .

section_heading: ## How to win more scholarships (without losing your mind)
text:
## How to win more scholarships (without losing your mind)

- Start early. y. Many scholarships are available to juniors and even freshmen .
- Apply for smaller scholarships . Fewer applicants = better odds .
- Create and keep a scholarship spreadsheet . Track deadlines , essay requirements , and award amounts .
- Recycle essays . Many scholarships ask similar questions . Use and tweak previous essays instead of writing from scratch every time .


Stacker Pro Tip: Apply to at least one or two scholarships per week . Even small ones add up fast.

section_heading: ## Work-study, side hustles, and other money-making on-campus gigs
text:
## Work-study, side hustles, and other money-making on-campus gigs

College is expensive , but that doesn ' t mean your only option is to eat cereal three meals a day . There are smart ways to make money while keeping your grades up. After all , what ' s the college experience without pizza and beer a well-balanced 3-square meals a day?

section_heading: ## Work -study: The campus job with perks
text:
## Work -study: The campus job with perks

- Earnings don ' t count against financial aid as much as regular income .
- Flexible schedules—because they know you have class .
- Jobs are often on -campus or nearby .


Stacker Pro Tip: Work-study jobs go fast. Apply as soon as possible to get the best options.

## Side hustles for college students

- Freelancing . Write , edit , design , or code from your dorm .
- Reselling . Channel your inner Gary Vee and flip thrift store finds on Amazon , eBay, y, or Depop.
- Tutoring . Help younger students (or your classmates) and get paid .
- Gig economy jobs . Uber Eats , Instacart , and pet-sitting can bring in extra cash .


Stacker Pro Tip: Avoid jobs that pay you in "e "experience " (a . k . a . free labor) . If a company wants your time, e, make sure they pay you in dollars, not exposure .

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: Free money is out there. Go get it!
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: Free money is out there. Go get it!

If you ' re willing to put in a little work , you can slash your college bill significantly . Grants , scholarships , work -study, y, and smart financial moves can turn a terrifying tuition bill into something actually manageable .

## Take action today:

- File the FAFSA ASAP. P.
- Search for at least three scholarships and apply . Make this a weekly habit .
- Check if your target schools require the CSS Profile , the SAT or ACT. T.

Want to learn more about how to tame the costs of higher education? Check out our interview with Brad Baldridge:

BIG College Planning Misconceptions (with Brad Baldridge) The Stacking Benjamins Show


Stacker Pro Tip: Work with your student's high school guidance counselor from early on during his/her high school years to make it known that you 'r 're interested in stacking scholarship opportunities. The guidance counselor will be able to guide you and give you a more complete list of available scholarship opportunities.

Coming Up Next: The student loan playbook — borrowing Benjamins without borrowing trouble .

## CHAPTER 5: The Student Loan Playbook

## Borrowing Without Borrowing Trouble

College is one of the few things in life where people gladly sign up for six figures of debt without a second thought (right up there with buying a house and financing a car they can ' t afford) . But here ' s the thing: while student loans can be a helpful tool , they can also turn into a lifelong financial anchor if you don ' t approach them wisely. y.

This chapter will help you borrow smart , avoid the worst mistakes , and make sure student loans don ' t turn your future into a monthly payment nightmare .

section_heading: ## How much is too much? The real math on student loans
text:
## How much is too much? The real math on student loans

If your college bill is looking like a mortgage payment and you ' re thinking, Well, l, I 'l 'll just pay it off later when I 'm 'm rich!, !, slow down . Not all student loan debt is " good debt . "

section_heading: ## The golden rule of student loans
text:
## The golden rule of student loans

- Borrow no more than your expected first-year salary . Translation: If your degree is likely to land you a $50 , 000 job , don ' t borrow more than $50 , 000 in total loans .


Stacker Pro Tip: Check salary projections for your major at sites like Payscale or the Bureau of Labor Statistics before committing to a big loan . (See more resources in Mom 's 's Monster Resource Guide at the rear of this document) .

## How loan payments actually work

Let ' s say you borrow $50 , 000 at a 6% interest rate with a 10 -year repayment plan . Your monthly payment? About $555 per month .

Now, w, if you borrow $100 , 000+ , you could be looking at payments over $1 , 000 per month—which is more than most rent payments .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Thinking, I 'l 'll figure out repayment later. r. Future -you will not appreciate that strategy .

section_heading: ## Federal vs. Private Loans: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly
text:
## Federal vs. Private Loans: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly

## Federal student loans: The safer bet

- Fixed interest rates (set by the government , not the bank) .
- Income -driven repayment plans (so payments adjust to your salary) .
- Forgiveness options (for teachers , public service workers , and some other careers) .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you must borrow, w, always max out federal loans before touching private loans.

section_heading: ## Private student loans: The high-risk option
text:
## Private student loans: The high-risk option

- Higher interest rates (varies based on credit score) .
- No income -based repayment (you owe the full amount , no matter what) .
- Co -signers are often required (which means mom and dad are on the hook , too)
- You may be required to begin repayments immediately, y, NOT after you graduate .


Stacker Pro Tip: Try to find scholarships and grants FIRST, T, then supplement with loans. Private loans should be your absolute last resort.

section_heading: ## Loan repayment strategies (that don't require winning the lottery)
text:
## Loan repayment strategies (that don't require winning the lottery)

Once you borrow, w, the real game begins: paying it off as fast as possible .

## Best ways to crush your student loans

- Pay more than the minimum . Even an extra $50 per month can shave years off your loan .
- Refinance (but only if it makes sense) . If you have high-interest private loans , refinancing to a lower rate can help. Read the fine print carefully before refinancing! Some loans have awful fees or locked -in "c "capitalization " features that will not work in your favor. r.
- Use windfalls smartly. y. Tax refunds , bonuses , and side hustle money can knock out chunks of your loan .
- Look for employer repayment benefits . Some companies will help pay off your student loans (seriously!) . This is becoming a bigger benefit now because of favorable provisions in the government ' s latest SECURE 2 . 0 legislation .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Deferring payments unless absolutely necessary . Interest keeps growing during deferment , making the loan even bigger .


Stacker Pro Tip: Use a loan calculator to see how much you 'l 'll save by making extra payments (See our favorite calculator options in Mom 's 's Monster Resource List at the rear of this guide) .

section_heading: ## Should Parents Take Out Loans? (Spoiler: Only If You Hate Retirement)
text:
## Should Parents Take Out Loans? (Spoiler: Only If You Hate Retirement)

Many parents feel guilty about not paying for their kid ' s college , so they take out parent PLUS loans or borrow against their home . This is usually a bad idea .

## Why parents should think twice:

- Parent PLUS loans have high interest rates .
- Retirement savings can ' t be replaced — but student loans can .
- Co -signing means you are legally responsible if your kid can ' t pay .


Stacker Pro Tip: It's better for students to borrow in their own name than for parents to wreck their retirement.

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: Borrow Smart or Don't Borrow at All
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: Borrow Smart or Don't Borrow at All

If you follow one rule from this chapter, r, make it this: borrow as little as humanly possible . Every dollar you borrow now is a dollar plus interest you ' ll have to repay later .

## Take action today:

- Run the numbers on what your degree is worth .
- Compare federal vs . private loan options .
- Make a plan for repaying your loans before you borrow .

Coming up next: The cheap college degree — hacking your way to an affordable education .

## CHAPTER 6: The Cheap College Degree

## Hacking Your Way to an Affordable Education

Let ' s get one thing straight: a great education doesn ' t have to come with a six -figure price tag. Somewhere along the way, y, we started acting like the only way to get a good degree was to pay for a fancy dorm , gourmet dining hall food , kegs , and a campus climbing wall .

Newsflash: employers don ' t care about how many waterfalls your student union has . They care about your skills and experience … not how much debt you racked up getting them .

So , let ' s talk about how to get a degree for less , hack your way to an affordable education , and keep more Benjamins in your stack .

section_heading: ## Community college first: The smartest (and cheapest) route
text:
## Community college first: The smartest (and cheapest) route

Want to slash your college costs in half (or more)? Start at community college .

## Why this move saves big money

- Costs a fraction of a four-year school . Tuition is often $5 , 000 per year or less vs . up to $20 , 000+ at a university .
- Credits transfer. r. Many community colleges have agreements with universities .
- No dorm costs . Live at home and avoid the overpriced " shared shoebox " dorm room .

## How to do it right

- Check transfer agreements before enrolling . Some community colleges have guaranteed transfer programs to big-name universities .
- Make sure credits transfer. r. Not all classes move seamlessly — talk to advisors .
- Take the required gen-ed classes here . English , math , history? Get them done for cheap.


Stacker Pro Tip: Some states offer free community college tuition . Look up what's available before you pay anything .

section_heading: ## In-State vs. Out-of-State: Is the fancy school worth it?
text:
## In-State vs. Out-of-State: Is the fancy school worth it?

Some students dream of going out-of-state for college . Is living hours away from mom and dad really worth an extra $10 , 000 to $20 , 000 more per year? (No judgement!)

## Compare these two scenarios

- In -state public university: tuition = $10 , 000 per year
- Out -of -state public university: tuition = $30 , 000 per year

That ' s an extra $80 , 000 over four years (before factoring in travel costs) . Is the " better experience " really worth the price of a luxury car down payment on your first house?

## When going out-of-state makes sense

- The school offers out -of -state tuition waivers (some do for good students) .
- Your dream major isn ' t offered in your home state (Be very certain about this before signing up) .
- The out -of -state school is giving you tons of merit aid .


Stacker Pro Tip: Some schools participate in regional tuition exchange programs, where students from certain states get reduced tuition at out-of-state schools. Look into programs like WUE, E, MSEP, P, and SREB .

section_heading: ## The art of transferring without losing credits (or your sanity)
text:
## The art of transferring without losing credits (or your sanity)

Transferring is a brilliant way to cut costs … if you do it correctly . But mess it up, and you could waste time and money retaking classes .

## How to transfer like a pro

- Know the transfer policies . Some schools only accept certain credits or require a minimum GPA .
- Use articulation agreements . These are pre-approved credit transfers between schools .
- Meet with an advisor. r. Do not assume credits will transfer—get confirmation . (You know what Mom says about what happens when you ass-u-me) .


Stacker Pro Tip: Some universities offer special scholarships just for transfer students. Don 't 't forget to check!

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: Your degree is an investment—spend wisely
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: Your degree is an investment—spend wisely

College isn ' t about where you go;it ' s about what you do while you ' re there . Employers don ' t care if you went to Harvard or State U … they care about your skills , experience , and ability to get the job done .

## Take action today:

- Check your state ' s free/low -cost college programs .
- Make sure your AP, P, IB , and dual enrollment credits will transfer .
- Compare in-state vs . out-of -state costs before applying.

Coming up next: Campus life and money — avoiding the broke college student trope .

## CHAPTER 7: Campus Life and Money

## Avoiding the Broke College Student Trope

We ' ve all heard the stereotype: college students survive on instant ramen , hoard free pizza , and mysteriously " forget " their wallets at group dinners . But here ' s the truth: being in college doesn ' t mean you have to be broke .

With the right money moves , you can avoid overdraft fees , dodge student loan debt traps , and still have enough left over for an actual meal that doesn ' t come in a Styrofoam cup. Let ' s talk about how to master money while living that campus life .

section_heading: ## How to Budget When You've Never Had to Budget Before
text:
## How to Budget When You've Never Had to Budget Before

For many students , college is the first time they ' re in charge of their own finances . The problem? Budgeting isn ' t usually taught in high school . (But hey, y, at least you know how to dissect a frog, right?)

section_heading: ## The simple Stacker college budget formula
text:
## The simple Stacker college budget formula

- 50% needs . Rent , tuition , books , food (real food , not just snacks) .
- 30% wants . Concerts , road trips , takeout , and all the Target runs .
- 20% savings/debt repayment . Future -you will thank you .


Stacker Pro Tip: Some universities offer special scholarships just for transfer students. Don 't 't forget to check!

Biggest mistake to avoid: Ignoring your bank balance until your debit card gets declined in front of your friends . Check your balance weekly .

Another big mistake to avoid (this directly from our college student intern Debahnie , who says: " Don ' t buy books before your first class meeting. Sometimes books listed on your syllabus are not required , some are provided by the professor, r, and sometimes you can find free PDF copies on the internet . Additionally, y, check other places to buy/rent them outside of your school ' s bookstore like Amazon or Chegg, and your school ' s library! "

section_heading: ## Credit Cards on Campus: Perk or Pitfall?
text:
## Credit Cards on Campus: Perk or Pitfall?

Credit cards can help build your credit score , but they can also send you straight into debt if you don ' t use them wisely .

## The smart way to use a credit card in college

- Get a student credit card . Lower limits , easier approval .
- Only charge what you can pay off in full . No exceptions .
- Set up auto-pay. y. Avoid late fees and build credit effortlessly .


Stacker Pro Tip: Use a credit card only for small, l, recurring expenses (like Spotify or Netflix) and pay it off in full every month .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Carrying a balance to " build credit . " That ' s a myth — and an expensive one . Interest on a $500 balance can turn into $800+ fast .

section_heading: ## Free Food, Free Fun: How to Enjoy College Without Spending a Dime
text:
## Free Food, Free Fun: How to Enjoy College Without Spending a Dime

Your campus is overflowing with free stuff — you just have to know where to look .

## Where to find freebies

- Campus events . Student groups love bribing people with free pizza .
- The library. y. Free access to books , movies , software , and even textbooks .
- Career fairs . Even if you ' re not job hunting, you can score free food , t-shirts , and weird stress balls .


Stacker Pro Tip: Check Reddit, Facebook groups, local businesses, and your student union for events giving out free food and swag .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Paying full price for anything on- (and sometimes off-) campus . There ' s usually a student discount , used option , or completely free alternative .

section_heading: ## Part-Time Jobs That Actually Work With Your Schedule
text:
## Part-Time Jobs That Actually Work With Your Schedule

A good college job won ' t tank your GPA , ruin your social life , or require waking up at 5 a . m .


## Best jobs for college students

- Remote/online work . Freelancing, tutoring, or virtual assistant gigs .
- On-campus jobs . Work -study, y, library assistant , or dorm RA (free housing!) .
- Gig work . Uber Eats , Instacart , pet -sitting, or reselling thrift store finds .


Stacker Pro Tip: On-campus jobs are gold because they usually work around your class schedule . Apply early — they go fast!

Biggest mistake to avoid: Taking a job that pays well but kills your grades . If your job hurts your academics , it ' s not worth it .

section_heading: ## Avoiding Scams, Pyramid Schemes, and Other College Money Traps
text:
## Avoiding Scams, Pyramid Schemes, and Other College Money Traps

College students = prime targets for shady money schemes . Here ' s how to spot (and avoid) them .

## Scams to watch out for

- Fake job offers . If a job requires " paying a fee " to get started , run .
- Pyramid schemes & MLMs . If you have to buy inventory upfront , it ' s a scam .
- Too-good-to-be-true credit cards . Zero interest for six months … and then 29% APR .


Stacker Pro Tip: If a job or business requires you to pay them to start, it's a scam .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Signing a lease , loan , or contract without reading the fine print . Always check for hidden fees and shady terms .

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: College Is Expensive—But You Don't Have to Be Broke
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: College Is Expensive—But You Don't Have to Be Broke

Smart students don ' t just survive college financially; they thrive by using every resource available .


## Take action today:

- Set up a simple college budget .
- Find one free event on campus this week .
- Apply for an on-campus or remote job.

Coming up next: The graduation exit strategy — From college to financial adulthood .

## CHAPTER 8: The graduation exit strategy

## From college to financial adulthood

You made it! Four (or maybe five … or six … no judgment) years of hard work , cramming, and surviving on dorm food have led to this moment . Graduation! Now, w, it ' s time for real life—complete with taxes , retirement contributions , paychecks , rent , grocery lists , bills , and the crushing realization that no one is going to make you chicken nuggets at 2 a . m . anymore .

This chapter is your on-ramp to financial adulthood . We ' ll cover everything from building credit to negotiating your first salary so you can step into post-college life with confidence—and avoid moving back into Mom ' s basement indefinitely (#Hypocracy #LiveFromJoe 's 'sMom 's 'sBasement) . Hey, y, we made it clear that this is a judgment-free zone .

section_heading: ## Building credit before you graduate (without going broke)
text:
## Building credit before you graduate (without going broke)

Don ' t do what Joe did and sign up for the first credit card offer and immediately take your friends out for food … and then be " unexpectedly " stuck paying a maxed-out credit card balance . Lesson learned , Mom! Your credit score is your financial reputation , and like any good reputation , it ' s easier to build before you desperately need it .

## The college graduate credit checklist

- Have at least one credit account . A student credit card , a small car loan , or even an old utility bill can help.
- Pay everything on time . On -time payments = 35% of your credit score . This is the most important factor .
- Keep credit utilization low . Don ' t max out your card . Use less than 30% of your credit limit .


Stacker Pro Tip: If you don 't 't have credit yet, become an authorized user on a parent's credit card . Their good history helps you instantly. y. Note: this does not give you free rein to spend their money without permission .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Ignoring your credit score until you need a loan or apartment . Check your score now (for free) at Credit Karma or Experian . You can also check with your bank to see if they offer this free service .

section_heading: ## Salary Negotiation 101: How to make more from day one
text:
## Salary Negotiation 101: How to make more from day one

Your first job offer sets the tone for your salary baseline and growth . If you accept whatever they offer, r, you ' re leaving money on the table .

## How to negotiate like a pro (even if it feels awkward)

- Know your worth . Use Glassdoor , Payscale , or Indeed to see what others are making.
- Let them make the first offer. r. But if they ask , give a range , not a single number .
- Ask for more . They expect negotiations . If they offer $55k , ask for $60k .


Stacker Pro Tip: Even if they won 't 't budge on salary, y, ask for other perks, like a signing bonus, remote work flexibility, y, or extra vacation days.

Biggest mistake to avoid: Accepting the first offer without asking if there ' s flexibility . The worst they can say is no .

section_heading: ## Employer student loan repayment perks (yes, these exist!)
text:
## Employer student loan repayment perks (yes, these exist!)

Some companies actually help pay off student loans . If you ' re job hunting and have an outstanding student loan balance , look for this perk — it ' s free money .

section_heading: ## How to find companies that offer student loan repayment
text:
## How to find companies that offer student loan repayment

- Check job descriptions . Some list it as a benefit .
- Ask HR before accepting an offer. r. Sometimes it ' s offered but not advertised .
- Look for industries with loan assistance . Healthcare , tech , and government jobs often have programs .


Stacker Pro Tip: Even if your job doesn 't 't offer this, check state and federal loan forgiveness programs—especially if you work in public service, e, non-profit, or education .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Not checking for student loan repayment perks before accepting a job. A company offering $200/month toward your loans = $2 , 400 per year in free money .


Stacker Pro Tip: Interview with multiple prospective employers and use the knowledge that you have other offers to your advantage .

section_heading: ## The "What Now?" Guide: Budgeting, investing, and other adulting tasks
text:
## The "What Now?" Guide: Budgeting, investing, and other adulting tasks

Now that real paychecks are coming in , it ' s time to create a grown -up budget that doesn ' t involve "g "guessing until my debit card gets declined . "

## The fresh grad budget formula

- 50% needs . Rent , utilities , student loans , groceries .
- 30% wants . Streaming services , fun , travel .
- 20% saving & investing . Future -you deserves some love , too .

section_heading: ## Where to put your money first
text:
## Where to put your money first

- Start an emergency fund . Aim for $1 , 000 first , then 3 -6 months of expenses .
- Invest in a 401(k) ASAP. P. If your employer matches , contribute at least enough to get the free money . Be aware of possible vesting schedules .
- Open a Roth IRA . Even small contributions now grow massively over time . Make sure to invest the money and not just keep it in a glorified savings account .


Stacker Pro Tip: Set up automatic transfers for savings and investing so you don 't 't have to think about it.

Biggest mistake to avoid: Spending all of your first paycheck on a new car, r, fancy apartment , or going crazy celebrating " finally being an adult. "

section_heading: ## Why you still need Mom 's 's basement (financially speaking, of course)
text:
## Why you still need Mom 's 's basement (financially speaking, of course)

Even if you ' re moving out , you should keep some of mom ' s financial wisdom close by .

## Financial lessons worth keeping

- Live below your means . Just because you can afford something doesn ' t mean you should buy it .
- Debt is easy to get into , hard to get out of . Be cautious with credit cards and loans .
- Automate your finances . The easiest way to build wealth is to save before you spend .


Stacker Pro Tip: Keep one financial habit from college — whether it's cooking at home, e, using student discounts, or tracking spending . Those small habits add up to big savings.

section_heading: ## FINAL THOUGHT: You're ready for financial adulthood
text:
## FINAL THOUGHT: You're ready for financial adulthood

Your time in college is over, r, but your financial journey is just beginning. Mastering money early means less stress , more freedom , and no need to crash on Mom ' s couch indefinitely .

## Take action today:

- Check your credit score and make a plan to build it .
- Negotiate your first salary — don ' t just accept the first offer .
- Start investing (even a little) so future-you has a nice retirement .

Coming up next: The college plan wrap-up — how to stay financially smart for life .

section_heading: ## FINAL CHAPTER: Putting It All Together
text:
## FINAL CHAPTER: Putting It All Together

## Your Roadmap to a Smart (and Affordable) College Plan

Congratulations , Stacker—you ' ve just powered through a complete , step-by-step strategy for tackling college costs without going broke . You ' re now miles ahead of the families who are still staring at tuition bills in shock , wondering if selling plasma is a viable strategy .

But now comes the big question: What do you actually do next?

This final chapter is all about bringing it all together — turning everything you ' ve learned into a clear, r, simple plan that makes college affordable , stress-free , and totally doable . Let ' s put the finishing touches on your Stacking Benjamins College Plan and send you (or your student) off with confidence .

section_heading: ## STEP 1 Lock Down the College Game Plan
text:
## STEP 1 Lock Down the College Game Plan

## Start early, y, but don ' t panic if you ' re late

No matter where you are in the process , you still have options . The earlier you start , the more you can save and prepare , but even if college is right around the corner, r, there are still plenty of cost-cutting hacks you can use .

## Key actions to take:

- Have the family money talk . Be honest about what ' s affordable and set expectations .
- Make a smart college list . Balance " dream schools " with financially smart choices .
- Research community college & transfer programs . Two years at a low-cost school can cut your bill in half or more .
- Check for free college options . Some states , programs , and employers cover tuition .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Choosing a college just for the name without thinking about cost , graduation rates , or job prospects .

## STEP 2 Stack Those College Savings


## Use tax-advantaged accounts & free money

Your future college costs don ' t have to come from your paycheck . Between 529 plans , Roth IRAs , scholarships , grants , and employer benefits , there are tons of ways to reduce what you have to pay out of pocket .

## Key actions to take:

- Open a 529 plan ASAP (even if you ' re late) . Tax -free growth = free money .
- Max out merit aid & scholarships . Free money is always better than loans .
- Look for employer tuition benefits . Some jobs will help pay for college .
- If you started late , focus on tax breaks & catch -up savings . Every little bit helps!

Biggest mistake to avoid: Assuming it ' s too late to save . Even a little saved now reduces future debt .

section_heading: ## STEP 3 Hunt Down the Free Money First
text:
## STEP 3 Hunt Down the Free Money First

## Financial aid , grants & scholarships

Before you even think about loans , exhaust all your free money options first .

## Key actions to take:

- File the FAFSA ASAP. P. Some aid is first -come , first -served .
- Check with your state to see if there are supplemental forms that are either required or that might increase your ability to secure more aid .
- Check if your school requires the CSS profile . Some colleges have extra aid forms .
- Search for local & niche scholarships . The smaller the scholarship , the fewer people apply .
- Look into work -study or part-time gigs . If you have to work , let it be in a way that helps with tuition .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Assuming you won ' t qualify for aid . Many middle-class families still get grants and scholarships .

section_heading: ## STEP 4 Borrow Smart (If You Have to Borrow at All)
text:
## STEP 4 Borrow Smart (If You Have to Borrow at All)


## Use student loans wisely — not recklessly

If you must borrow, w, do it strategically . Student loans can be useful , but they should be your last resort—not the first option .

## Key actions to take:

- Follow the " first -year salary rule . " Never borrow more than what you expect to make in your first year after graduation .
- Max out federal loans before considering private loans . Federal loans have better protections and lower interest rates .
- Make interest -only payments while in school . This prevents interest from ballooning your loan amount .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Borrowing with no plan for repayment . Future -you will not appreciate being shackled to debt .

section_heading: ## STEP 5 Hack Your Way to a Cheaper Degree
text:
## STEP 5 Hack Your Way to a Cheaper Degree

The best degrees aren ' t always the most expensive ones

A fancy degree doesn ' t guarantee success — but a strategic , affordable degree lets you graduate with money in your pocket .

## Key actions to take:

- Consider community college or AP/dual enrollment credits . Knock out classes for a fraction of the price .
- Compare in-state vs . out-of -state costs . Out -of -state tuition can be 3x higher .
- Use employer-sponsored degrees . Some companies pay 100% of tuition .
- Test out of classes where possible . CLEP and DSST exams let you earn credits fast .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Paying way more than necessary for the same degree you could get for half the price .

section_heading: ## STEP 6 Graduate Like a Financial Pro
text:
## STEP 6 Graduate Like a Financial Pro


## The real education starts after college

Once you ' re out of college , you need a solid financial strategy. y.

## Key actions to take:

- Negotiate your first salary. y. Even a small raise now = big money later .
- Check for employer loan repayment perks . Some companies help pay off student debt .
- Start saving & investing immediately. y. Even $50/month in a Roth IRA is a win .
- Live below your means & avoid " lifestyle creep . " Just because you have a paycheck doesn ' t mean you need a luxury apartment .

Biggest mistake to avoid: Ignoring student loan repayment strategies until the first bill arrives .

## FINAL THOUGHT: You've Got This!

College isn ' t just about getting a degree — it ' s about building a financially strong future . With the right strategy, y, you can graduate with confidence , a solid financial foundation , and none of the crushing debt that holds so many people back .

## Final action steps:

- Choose at least three money-saving strategies from this book and put them into action NOW .
- Set up a financial plan for saving, borrowing (only if necessary) , and repaying wisely .
- Most importantly — remember that a degree is just a tool . How you use it (and how you manage your money) will determine your financial success .

Stackers , you ' ve got this! Now go forth , save big , and stack those Benjamins like a pro .

## Next Steps & Bonus Resources

Want more college planning hacks , savings strategies , and smart money moves? Dive into Mom 's 's Monster Resource List , packed with:

- Our recommended college savings calculators
- Scholarship search tools
- FAFSA tips & tricks
- Stacking Benjamins ' best episodes on college & student loans

## Mom ' s Monster Resource List

## Sites to Research Career Salaries:

- Payscale . com – Easy-to-read salary data and comparisons
- Bureau of Labor Statistics – Reliable government data on career earnings and job outlook

## Interview Advice & Networking:

- Glassdoor . com – Company reviews , interview questions , and salary info
- LinkedIn . com – Networking and job search tools

## Loan Calculators

- Bankrate loan calculator – Helps estimate monthly loan payments

## Budgeting Apps

- YNAB (You Need A Budget) – Popular zero-based budgeting tool
- Monarch Money – Great for families
- TillerMoney – Spreadsheet-based tracking
- Your bank ' s budgeting tool
- Qube Money – Digital envelope system

## Our recommended college saving calculators:

- Saving For College website calculator: https://www . savingforcollege . com/calculators/college-savings-calculator
- Fidelity Investments College Savings Calculator:
- https://www . fidelity . com/misc/college-savings/college \_ savings . html

## Scholarship Search Tools:

- Scholarships . com (they ask some pretty invasive questions and your phone number , address , AND if they can market you . However , they came back with some great scholarship offers and deadlines . )
- FastWeb . com Maybe the granddaddy of them all . Comprehensive . Personalized . Love that you can set up notification preferences .

section_heading: ## Our Best Episodes on College Planning:
text:
## Our Best Episodes on College Planning:

- BIG College Planning Misconceptions (with Brad Baldridge)
- Lessons From a College Planning Wizard (With Jodi Okun)
- How To Surround Yourself With Good People (plus big money savings on college w/ Pam Andrews)

## The SB College Planning Calendar

## A Year-at-a-Glance Guide for Families Paying for College

## What This Is (and Why It Matters)

College planning rarely falls apart because families do nothing. It falls apart because they do things at the wrong time .

This calendar is a standalone companion to the SB Planning for College Guide . It pulls the most important college , financial aid , and planning milestones into one place so you can see what matters when , without rereading the entire guide .

This is not a list of every possible deadline for every student . Colleges set their own dates . States have their own aid rules . But these are the moments that consistently create the biggest financial wins -or the most expensive mistakes - for families who miss them .

Use this as a planning tool , not a panic button .

## Ninth Grade (Freshman Year)

## All Year

- Focus on strong academic habits . Grades from this year forward matter .
- Begin tracking extracurriculars , volunteer work , and activities .
- Start conversations about what college is for not where it is yet .

Why this matters: Scholarships and admissions look for consistency, y, not last -minute heroics .

## Tenth Grade (Sophomore Year)

## Fall – Spring

- Continue GPA focus . This is not a throwaway year .
- Explore career interests and possible majors .
- Take the PSAT if offered (practice , not pressure) .

## Spring – Summer

- Visit colleges casually if travel allows . No pressure , just exposure .
- Begin learning how financial aid actually works (FAFSA , CSS Profile , merit vs . need -based aid) .

## Eleventh Grade (Junior Year)

This is where timelines start to matter .

## Fall

- Take the PSAT/NMSQT (October) . This is the qualifier for National Merit Scholarships .
- Begin building a college list based on academics , fit , and affordability .
- Start scholarship searches .

## Winter

- Register for SAT or ACT exams .
- Compare colleges using net price , not sticker price .
- Get familiar with each school ' s financial aid methodology .

## Spring

- Take SAT/ACT exams .
- Ask teachers for future letters of recommendation before summer .
- Start outlining college essays while schoolwork is still fresh .

## Summer

- Draft personal statements and essays .
- Narrow college list .
- Visit campuses if possible .

## Twelfth Grade (Senior Year)

This is the most deadline -heavy year .

## Fall

- October 1 -FAFSA typically opens (confirm each year) .
- Submit FAFSA as early as possible to maximize aid eligibility .
- Complete CSS Profile if required by schools .
- Submit Early Action or Early Decision applications if applicable .

Reminder: Early does not mean rushed . Accuracy beats speed .

## Winter

- Regular Decision applications are usually due between January 1 and February 1 .
- Confirm all test scores , transcripts , and recommendations were received .


## Spring

- Review acceptance letters and financial aid offers side by side .
- Appeal financial aid offers if warranted . This is more common than families think .
- Decide where to enroll .

## May

- May 1 -National College Decision Day for most schools .
- Submit enrollment deposit .
- Decline other offers formally .

## After Acceptance (Often Overlooked)

## Late Spring – Summer Before College

- Complete loan counseling if borrowing federal student loans .
- Set up payment plans with the school bursar .
- Review health insurance requirements .
- Revisit the family budget and cash flow .

## Ongoing

- Refile FAFSA each year the student is enrolled . Aid is not one -and -done .
- Track scholarships with renewal requirements .

## Final Thought

The biggest mistake families make with college planning is waiting for a single moment to get everything right .

There is no single moment .

There is only a series of well-timed decisions .


## Take action today:

- Use this calendar to stay ahead , ask better questions , and avoid expensive surprises . That is how college planning works best - calmly, y, deliberately, y, and on your terms .

section_heading: ## What You'll Get with This Guide!
text:
## What You'll Get with This Guide!

Want the keys to a treasure chest filled with money? You ' re reading it . Your company ' s benefits package is a treasure chest that most people never know how to unlock .

But , because you ' re using this guide , you can expect two things to happen:

This guide–this key–isn ' t just a shiny object . It ' s an exciting tool IF you take advantage of it . We ' ve compiled hours of our best lessons from all the individual podcast segments , 201 newsletters , and videos that we offer. r. We ' ve combed the web for the best resources available . The time we ' ve spent curating this Benefits Guide alone saves you the money you ' ve invested , but true wealth begins piling up when you find that your benefits fit like the bed in Goldilocks and the three bears: not too much or too little , your benefits are juuuust right. Let ' s get started .

## How to Use This Guide

Begin with the quick checklist . This will give you a head start on picking better benefits immediately . If you have time today to do nothing else with this package , secure your investment in this guide by capitalizing on our knowledge . In 15 minutes , you ' re better off. f.

Then , to understand the most commonly found benefits , read this handbook . You need to know what each benefit actually means , so we ' ve quickly (and—dare we say—entertainingly) defined the " must-knows . "

Finally, y, the heart of this guide is the Planning Checklist included in this bundle . One by one , we ' ve given you the questions to ask , resources to explore , and decisions you ' ll need to make . We ' ve saved you time by linking to the best resources available , so you aren ' t wasting valuable energy on fruitless Google searches .

Better yet , we ' re also consistently updating the resources . Your investment includes a lifetime of downloads , so feel free to grab the most up-to-date version next month . We want this to be your go-to for the rest of your career. r. Well , enough preamble . It ' s time to get moving . Need something along the way? Email us: joe@stackingbenjamins . com .

Let 's 's get going on that treasure chest!

## COMPLETE THESE STEPS FIRST!

Ready to stack some quick Benjamins? Let ' s grab you the best return on investment right away . Want to learn about better HR choices? Sure , we ' ll cover all of that deeper in the guide . But right upfront , let ' s cut to saving you money quickly and giving you better benefit options .

Grab a pen and check these big eleven " misses " off of your list . You '

re about to do all of these correctly!

- Check your beneficiaries . On Stacking Benjamins , we share MANY stories about people who have the wrong beneficiaries on their retirement accounts and insurance . One man unintentionally left over a MILLION DOLLARS to an ex - girlfriend . Needless to say, y, she really doesn ’ t care what his brothers (the intended beneficiaries) think about that . She ’ s keeping the cash .

section_heading: ## Check your beneficiaries, like, right now .
text:
## Check your beneficiaries, like, right now .

These are popular accounts with beneficiaries:

- Life insurance (through work)

- Life insurance (outside of work)

- Checking and savings accounts

- IRAs , Roth IRAs

- 401k plans , 403b plans , and 457 plans


- Grab the match . Does your employer match your 401k? If so , even if you have other priorities , contribute up to the match , if at all possible . This is free money that ’ s added to your account and means less work for you in the future .

- True Up Your Matching . While we ’ re on the topic of matching , are you adding in the maximum possible to your retirement plan? Even if you think you are … there might be room for improvement . Confirm that your employer is matching based on your entire annual pay, y, not just based on the check amount . If the employer is matching each contribution (and not just annual payments into the plan) , level out your contributions so they last all year. r. If you fill up your retirement savings early, y, you may be missing out on free matching money . (See “ Retirement Plans ” section for details) .

- Disability insurance . Have it? Great . Don ’ t have it? Sign up . This is the number one most overlooked and most “ missed ” insurance available . Use our disability section inside to tweak it later. r.

- Accidental death and dismemberment insurance . Have it? Do you work at a desk? Cancel it! Sure , it ’ s cheap , but as we ’ ll cover, r, these policies are cheap for a reason . This only pays if you lose an eye or an appendage . If you ’ re at a desk , what ’ s the chance of that happening?

- Health Savings Account - If you have this benefit , move money that you aren ’ t going to need this year into investments . Most people leave excess savings sitting in cash and miss out on a ton of compounding .

section_heading: ## Check your beneficiaries, like, right now .
text:
- Flexible Savings Account - First , you can ’ t have this account AND an HSA … so pick one . Of the two , we like the HSA better. r. Second , are you running costs like eyewear and feminine products through your FSA? These could be purchased pre-tax , an average of a 20% discount for most people .

- Dependent Care Account - Do you require childcare? While you can write this off on your taxes , it ’ s FAR more beneficial (and easier) to use this at-work benefit instead . Sign up for this through your FSA . This could be well over $2 , 000 of tax savings alone!

- Legal Expenses - Have you finished your estate plan? If so , cut the legal expense benefit , unless you have some upcoming or ongoing legal issues . If not , use the legal expenses credit to cut the cost of an attorney to complete your will/trust . THEN cancel the benefit .

- Check your direct deposit . Is this really where it should be going? You can move some money directly into a savings account instead of into a checking account if you wish , assuming your company allows more than one deposit point .

- How big of a tax refund did you receive last year? Let ’ s get more of that in your paycheck , now that interest rates are higher again . Request a new W-4 and adjust your withholding to match your current life situation .

- Have your benefits guide open? Head to the worksheet in Mom ’ s Monster Resource to quickly fill out your 30-minute benefits guide worksheet .

## BAM! You Did It .

Now, w, let 's 's prep to dive in . It 's 's time to truly win by understanding ALL of your benefits .

1. Grab pens , paper, r, a to -do list or a calendar to schedule out all of the things you ' ll need to investigate .
2. Print out the benefits you know you are signed up for already AND all that are available .
3. Do you have deductions for benefits already? Keep a copy of your paycheck with you for this .
4. If you ' re married or in a committed relationship , grab your spouse/partner ' s benefit package . Let ' s dovetail both packages so you have the best of both worlds .

## Order of Operations

You may have more needs than money available , so what benefits are most important? Attack your benefit options in this order. r.


Employee benefits are allowances a company uses as additional compensation , aka " hidden Benjamins . " There ' s insurance , savings plans , and more . Many employees consider these perks at best , gravy, y, and at worst , irrelevant . Because of that , they underutilize their benefits package and miss out on HUGE opportunities .


Take a listen to this interview we did with Amanda Lannert , CEO of Jellyvision , to gain a basic understanding of employee benefits before diving in .

## Types of Benefits

|----------------------------------------------|----------|
| HSA/FSA                                      | 14 - 18  |
| Dental Benefits                              | 19 - 20  |
| Disability Insurance                         | 21–23    |
| Life Insurance                               | 24 – 26  |
| Retirement Plans                             | 27 – 29  |
| Childcare                                    | 30       |
| Adoption Assistance                          | 31       |

| Health Related Expense Reimbursement         | 36       |
