| The SB Workplace Benefits & HR Calendar      | 55 - 57  |

## What's Inside the Guide?

## Health Insurance

## What It Is

Coverage for medical and surgical expenses . HMO and PPO are the most popular variations , but you may also see EPO or POS plans .

## Why You Should Care

Health expenses are … expensive! Health plans through an employer are typically less expensive than individual plans through the public marketplace . This is because they are often subsidized by your employer. r. Don ' t spend your own precious Benjamins when your employer wants to pay for you!

## Key Terms

Network Most insurances work with a network of linked healthcare providers . Like being in the " in -crowd , " or the " cool kids club , " your doctor is either " in -network " or " out -offnetwork . " You ' ll need to know which doctors are " in -network " with your company ' s insurance choice .

If your favorite physician isn ' t in your network , you ' ll have a tough choice to make: find a different physician that ' s in -network or pay a potentially huge premium to keep them .

Deductible This is the amount you ' ll pay before your insurance kicks in , generally measured on an annual basis . For example , if you have a $1 , 000 deductible , you ' ll pay the first thousand dollars of charges before your insurance picks up the remainder (or a portion) of the bill for your treatment .

Co-pay Different than a deductible , you ' ll pay this amount , but on an ongoing basis . For example: you may have a $25 co -pay for office visits whenever you seek out a physician . There are many reasons for this benefit , but mostly it ' s to ensure that you truly need treatment before scheduling time on a doctor ' s busy schedule .

section_heading: ## Key Considerations when choosing " best " health coverage
text:
## Key Considerations when choosing " best " health coverage

- How many health visits will you need? If you use the doctor frequently, y, more comprehensive options such as traditional care or a PPO may be your best bet .
- Don ' t use the doctor often? HMO plans and High Deductible Plans may be your best choice .
- Feel savvy and have an HSA available? If so , there are many nuances (see below) , but a High Deductible plan may be your best choice … but you may pay more upfront for care .

## How do different options work?

## An HMO is a "Health Maintenance Organization." "

These types of insurance coverages were originally created to lower costs by getting participants to focus on maintaining a healthy lifestyle . If you used this option for coverage , you ' d pay less money because 1) you were healthy; and 2) therefore , you used the medical system less frequently for complex and expensive treatments .

For this reason , HMOs cover pregnancy costs , well -check visits , and sometimes even offer memberships to gyms or other health-related maintenance seminars , clubs , or organizations!

## The downside of an HMO?

## EVERYTHING you do flows through your primary care doctor. r.

This is a strict rule . You simply cannot use care providers who are out of the network your insurance offers .

## A PPO is a "Preferred Provider Organization." "

This type of insurance is all about the " network " as well . However, r, rather than having the bottleneck that everything must flow through your single primary care doctor, r, so long as a provider is in the network, k, you can schedule appointments with them . You can also schedule coverage outside of your network , but that will come at a high cost .

For this reason , lots of people choose a PPO for their coverage needs . So long as you have a large enough network , you have the flexibility to find the care you need and schedule it without jumping through hoops .

The downside of a PPO? If it ' s a great network and your deductibles are low, w, you ' re going to pay MUCH more than you would for HMO coverage . Also , going out-of-network is always going to be very costly .

section_heading: ## What Happens To My Coverages When I Leave a Company?
text:
## What Happens To My Coverages When I Leave a Company?

Health plans don ' t follow you around unless you work on it and are prepared to pay through the nose . When you leave your employer, r, coverage will lapse upon termination . Unlike the Karate Kid ' s rival dojo , Cobra Kai , COBRA insurance is available for between 18 and 36 months , which allows you to keep your insurance plan , but often for an additional cost .

COBRA No , we ' re not referring to the giant snake . COBRA is your health insurance lifeline between periods of employer-provided coverage . Companies are required to offer COBRA insurance when you leave , so that you aren ' t stuck with only the ACA plans to choose from .

Since its enactment in 1986 , back when Joe may or may not still have had hair, r, COBRA has been a critical piece to the health insurance coverage picture for employees . You pay an arranged rate to your former employer ' s insurance provider, r, and you get to stay on the company ' s insurance plan as if you were still employed . Given the out -offpocket costs of medical care , COBRA may be a must -have when you leave a job!

Here 's 's how it works: After separating from the employment through which you had your health insurance coverage , you can stay on COBRA for up to 18 months .

When the qualifying event is the end of employment or reduction of the employee ' s hours , and the employee becomes entitled to Medicare less than 18 months before the qualifying event , COBRA coverage for the employee ' s spouse and dependents can last until 36 months after the date the employee becomes entitled to Medicare . For example , if a covered employee becomes entitled to Medicare 8 months before the date his/her employment ends (termination of employment is the COBRA qualifying event) , COBRA coverage for his/her spouse and children would last 28 months (36 months minus 8 months) .

Can I use FSA or HSA funds to pay for COBRA premiums? You can use money in your HSA to pay for COBRA premiums , but NOT money in your FSA .

section_heading: ## What Happens To My Coverages When I Leave a Company?
text: What 's 's the difference in care between an HMO and a PPO? Since the Affordable Care Act , where coverage levels were largely standardized , there is no appreciable difference in care between these two plans. The key difference is that you can schedule out-of-network providers without having to go outside of your insurance coverage plan .

## High Deductible (HDHPs) or "Major Medical" Insurance

This subtype of insurance plans ONLY covers the most significant issues you may have with your health . The good news? These plans are either PPOs or HMOs , so while they may look different , there is only one big change: these are sometimes referred to as "c "catastrophic " insurance coverages because they only kick in if you have a major health issue, e, like needing surgery . These plans ensure that you won ' t use the coverage unless it ' s an emergency by moving the deductible to a monster amount of money, y, sometimes $10 , 000 or more . High-deductible health plans (HDHPs) begin with deductibles as low as $1 , 500 for single coverage and $3 , 000 for a family plan .

Frequently Asked Questions about HDHP Coverage: What about well-checks and physicals … you know, w, " health maintenance " visits? Most high deductible plans will cover a limited number of these visits , so you can continue to have professionals help you monitor how things are going " under the hood " without worrying about payment . There may be a small co-pay you ' ll have to front for this visit .

This type of insurance is very popular for budget-minded people because it ' s so much less money than the other types . However, r, this can also present some significant problems . Some budget geeks don ' t do well -check visits because they either misunderstand that this will cost money or they believe that it may lead to visits that are not covered . Also , some people will avoid warning signs that their health isn ' t great because they ' re afraid to pay the money it may take to visit the physician .

For this reason , if you 'r 're just starting out, we aren 't 't fans of HDHPs.

However, r, if you have a plan and are open to using your coverage , there is a portion of this insurance coverage that can help you win big monetarily in the end:

These plans are coupled with a Health Savings Account (HSA) .

We ' ll cover a Health Savings Account in detail next , along with its cousin , the Flexible Savings Account .

## Traditional Coverage

We won ' t spend much time on this because it ' s simple . Your policy has a deductible (the amount you ' ll pay before the insurance kicks in) . At that point , with very few exceptions , your insurance policy covers either everything or a large majority of the cost .

The upside of traditional coverage is also the downside . You probably can ' t afford it unless your company is heavily subsidizing your insurance costs .

section_heading: ## Situational Examples — Which Plan Fits Your Adventure?
text:
## Situational Examples — Which Plan Fits Your Adventure?

## HMO — Budget-Friendly & Structured

- You ' re starting a new job and want to keep costs low while you build your emergency fund . You don ' t mind working with a primary care doctor who coordinates all your care .
- You have a predictable health routine . Annual check -ups , the occasional sinus infection — nothing that sends you trekking to out-of-network specialists .
- You like staying in one " neighborhood " of providers . Think of it as joining a club — once you ' re in , you stick with their doctors .
- You ' d rather spend your extra cash on plane tickets than premiums .

## PPO — Ultimate Flexibility

- You ' ve got a baby on the way and want to choose your own OB , even if they ' re out of network . Peace of mind is worth the higher premium .
- You travel often for work or play . You want coverage that follows you whether you ' re in Denver, r, Dublin , or Doug ' s favorite roadside diner in Des Moines .
- You see specialists regularly . And you don ' t want to hassle with referrals — you just want to call and book .
- You ' re willing to pay more each month for the freedom to see the doctor you want , when you want .

section_heading: ## HDHP — Low Premiums & HSA Power Moves
text:
## HDHP — Low Premiums & HSA Power Moves

- You ' re healthy, y, in your 20s or 30s , and rarely see the doctor. r. You ' d rather pocket the savings and grow your HSA for future adventures .
- You ' re a long-term planner. r. You like the triple tax advantage of an HSA and think of it as your " stealth retirement account . "
- You want to be covered for big , unexpected expenses but keep your monthly costs low .
- You ' re disciplined with savings . You ' ll actually put the difference into your HSA instead of letting it vanish into the " oops , where ' d that go? " budget category .

section_heading: ## How Do You Choose the "Correct" Option?
text:
## How Do You Choose the "Correct" Option?

Largely, y, you ' ll review the strengths and weaknesses above and fit your situation into the correct box . However, r, lately we ' ve found that MOST people are reporting that between the amount of money companies are discounting HDHP plans and subsidizing HSA accounts , it ' s difficult to find a situation where a PPO beats a HDHP. P. Compare deductibles , what ' s covered , the amount of money reimbursed/subsidized by your company and you ' ll find your best choice .

## HSA/FSA

## What It Is

Accounts to supplement health insurance and help pay for medical expenses not covered by insurance .

Additionally, y, an HSA , if used correctly, y, can also be used as a long-term savings vehicle .

## Why You Should Care

Both FSA and HSA accounts use pre-tax dollars to pay for expenses .

However, much like the red pill and blue pill in The Matrix, you can only choose one of these options if both are available.

## Key Considerations (HSA)

## PROS

- Contributions are pre-tax AND funds can be invested and earnings in the account grow tax -free . WAIT, T, WHAT!? Yes, you read that correctly. y.
- Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax -free .
- Non -qualified withdrawals are subject to a 10% penalty and income tax . After age 65 , withdrawals can be made for any purpose without penalty (but are subject to income tax) .
- Employees can take the account with them when they leave their job .

## CONS

- Requires a high deductible health insurance plan and minimum deductible limits to be eligible . Also , cannot be eligible for Medicare or listed as a dependent on another person ' s tax return .
- Recordkeeping can be tedious if you choose to pay for medical expenses out -offpocket and reimburse yourself from your HSA at a later time .
- HSAs often need to be rolled over to secure better investment options if you want to save the money for long-term expenses .

## Key Considerations (FSA)

## PROS

- Funds are withdrawn pre-tax from paycheck directly .
- No high deductible health insurance plan requirement .
- The full pledged amount is available for immediate use , even if contributions are planned to be made throughout the year. r.

## CONS

- Use it or lose it . Generally, y, funds must be used in the year they are contributed . There are a few exceptions and grace periods , so check with your HR department for specifics
- Funds are lost if you are fired or leave a job
- Changes to pledged contribution amounts are only allowed during open enrollment .

## HSA Deep Dive

## How Do I Use My HSA Effectively?

Use this HSA Decision -Making Flowchart


## Have You Decided to Use Your HSA for Long-Term Needs?

If you want to save your HSA money as additional retirement funds or for later-in-life needs , you ' ll want to invest this money (especially if your goal is more than 10 years away) .

- Ask your HSA provider if there are investment options available inside of your plan
- No investment options? You aren ' t stuck . HSAs can be rolled over to another HSA of your choice that offers investment opportunities .
- Check out our podcast episode with Morningstar ' s Leo Achinson (just after the 5 minute mark): Morningstar HSA discussion on Stacking Benjamins
- For a current list , Morningstar rated their top HSA choices here: Morningstar ' s Top HSAs 2025 (most current)

section_heading: ## FAQ on HSA Contribution Limits, Exceptions, and Weirdness
text: ## FAQ on HSA Contribution Limits, Exceptions, and Weirdness

section_heading: ## FAQ on HSA Contribution Limits, Exceptions, and Weirdness
text:
- How much can I contribute? In 2026 , individuals can contribute up to $4 , 400 and joint filers up to $8 , 750 . Individuals over age 50 can also contribute up to $1 , 000 more . These can come from any source .
- "A "Any source , " … what does that mean? Directly, y, it usually means your employer may contribute as well if they choose , but between all sources , you can ' t go over the above limits .
- Are there any other weird rules on contribution limits? Just to keep it interesting , the IRS has two muddy rules . First , if you weren ' t eligible for an HSA the entire year, r, you may only contribute the same percentage toward the limit that coincides with the amount of time you were eligible . So if you began on July 1 of the year, r, you ' d cut your contribution limit in half for that year. r. BUT to make it weirder, r, if you were HSA eligible on December first of a given year AND you maintain eligibility the entire following year, r, you can forget everything above and make the maximum contribution for that year. r.
- How high does the deductible on my health insurance need to be for my plan to qualify for HSA pairing? Your plan documents should advertise that they ' re eligible for HSA pairing . However, r, for individuals , the lowest deductible in 2026 is $3 , 400 . For a family, y, $6 , 150 .
- What ' s the maximum I ' d be expected to pay out of pocket in 2026? For individuals , $10 , 600 . For families , $21 , 200 .
- Are there any expenses that are covered but that aren ' t subject to the deductible? Yes! Child and adult immunizations , routine prenatal care , and screening services for conditions such as cancer and heart disease are covered even before you reach your deductible .
- If my spouse doesn ' t have an HSA -compliant plan but I do , can I still contribute the maximum for joint filers? Yes , but only if the insurance contract covers the family .
- If my spouse has an FSA and I have an HSA , can we use both? No , you cannot use both IF your spouse ' s plan also covers YOU . If your spouse is enrolled in an FSA for both of you , that usually disqualifies YOU from using your HSA , even if they never take a dime of benefit from it .
- My spouse is on Medicare and is ineligible to open an HSA-compliant plan . Can I still contribute the family maximum or only the individual maximum? The only thing that matters is your contract . So , if your spouse is ineligible to open an HSA but you have a family plan which is HSA compliant , you can save up to the join filer maximum .
- Do you have a longer list of who wouldn ' t be eligible to open an HSA? Sure! This is not exhaustive , so check with your situation , but you aren ' t eligible if you ' re on Medicare , TRICARE, TRICARE for Life , or if you ' re a dependent on someone else ' s tax return . Also , if you have applied for or are receiving Social Security benefits , which automatically entitle you to Part A , you cannot continue to contribute to an HSA .

section_heading: ## FAQ on HSA Contribution Limits, Exceptions, and Weirdness
text:
- Can I have other health insurance other than my HSA? Generally, y, no . However, r, there are some exceptions . If you have a specific medical condition or illness , you may receive coverage for this . You may also secure a fixed amount for hospitalization (per day, y, month , or other period) . You may also purchase insurance for liabilities related to worker ' s compensation laws . Of course , your other benefits , like disability, y, dental , telehealth , long-term care , and vision coverage , coexist nicely with your HSA .
- Can I use my HSA to pay expenses for my spouse ' s or dependents ' expenses? Yes , assuming the expenses qualify, y, and even if you only have an insurance plan that only covers you .
- My spouse is HSA eligible . Can THEY contribute to my account? Yes , they can contribute and then claim a deduction for the amount they saved on their tax return . However, r, if they do , while they ' ll avoid federal and any state taxes by deducting them , the payroll tax still applies on money that YOU made (money that did NOT go into the HSA) . To find the biggest tax break , it ' s a better strategy to shift your budget so YOU contribute as much as possible through payroll deductions and your spouse then covers more of the family ' s other bills to pick up the slack .
- Let ' s say my spouse has medical coverage that is NOT part of an HSA-compliant plan , and I have an HSA -approved medical plan . What could disqualify me from an HSA? Great question . If you ' re covered in ANY WAY by their insurance policy (they have a family plan which includes you) , you cannot open an HSA .
- I no longer have an HSA-compliant health plan . What can I do with my HSA? You can still keep it , maintain it , and use it for medical expenses in the future that qualify! You cannot contribute more money, y, however. r.

section_heading: ## FAQ on HSA Contribution Limits, Exceptions, and Weirdness
text: Do you use Telehealth services? Here ' s what ' s new this year: Your High Deductible Health Plans (HDHPs) can now provide first-dollar telehealth services without jeopardizing your HSA eligibility! This is now " permanent , " (in " because … well , it ' s the government saying the word " permanent " . . . ) retroactively effective to plan years beginning after December 31 , 2024 .

section_heading: ## Dental Benefits: More Than Just a Nice Smile
text:
## Dental Benefits: More Than Just a Nice Smile

## What It Is

Insurance for everything from routine dental care to major dental procedures .

## Why You Should Care

Your dental coverage isn ' t just about cleanings and cavity fillings—it can save you thousands on major procedures and prevent bigger health issues down the road . Poor oral health has been linked to everything from heart disease to diabetes , so skipping dental insurance to save a few bucks could cost you more in the long run . But not all dental plans are created equal , and knowing what to look for can help you make a smarter choice .

## Key Considerations

- What ' s Covered? Most plans cover preventive care (cleanings , exams , X -rays) at 100% , but coverage for fillings , crowns , and major procedures varies . Some plans also offer orthodontic coverage , but often with limits .
- Network Matters – Sticking to in-network dentists saves you the most money, y, while going out -offnetwork may mean higher costs or reduced reimbursements .
- Annual Maximums – Unlike health insurance , dental plans often cap how much they ' ll pay per year (typically $1 , 000 – $2 , 000) . If you need extensive dental work , you could hit that limit fast .
- Waiting Periods – Some plans require waiting periods for major procedures . If you need a crown or root canal soon , check the fine print before choosing a plan .
- Deductibles & Copays – These can apply even for basic care , so check if your plan requires out -offpocket payments before coverage kicks in .
- Flexibility & FSAs – If your plan isn ' t great , you can supplement dental costs with an HSA or FSA to pay for care with pre-tax dollars .

## Comparing Coverages:

Here ' s what we like to especially focus on when comparing different dental plans , if you ' re offered two:

- Cleanings . Try to find a plan that covers two cleanings a year. r. Some experts recommend 3-4 cleanings and exams a year to help keep up with basic oral health .
- Fluoride treatments . While fluoride is currently a hot political issue , you ' ll find that including fluoride treatments at recommended levels isn ' t at all controversial . Especially if you have kids under 14 years old , try to find a plan that will include fluoride treatments at least once per year. r. This helps stop the growth of mouth bacterias and prevents cavities .
- X -rays . Dive into the details on how often and to what level x -rays are included in your coverage . These can not only detect cavities , but they can also show the need for braces or advanced procedures .

## Bottom Line

Dental insurance can be a great deal—if you choose the right plan . Prioritize preventive care , check for hidden costs , and make sure it aligns with your needs . A little research now can save you a lot of money (and toothaches) later. r.

## Disability Insurance

## What It Is

Provides income if you are no longer able to work due to a disability. y. Short -term and long-term disability coverage may be available through your employer. r. Disability insurance typically covers a portion of your monthly income .

## Why You Should Care

More than 25% of 20 -year -olds today will become disabled before age 67 . If you are the sole earner for your family, y, not having disability coverage can leave you with no income , which is no bueno .

## Key Definitions

Own Occupation This simple phrase changes a disability policy ' s scope . A policy that does NOT have this key component may require that you work a DIFFERENT job other than the one you are skilled or trained in .

Let ' s say you ' re an accountant and your work is valued at a high level . If you can still clean the floors at another company, y, the disability insurance can force you to work another type of job , rather than pay benefits . Not cool .

## Types of Disability Coverage

## Accidental Death and Dismemberment

This popular type of insurance only pays a benefit if you lose an appendage or an eye (or in some policies , other parts of the body) . This type of insurance is cheap for one reason: it's rarely used . Especially if you ' re an office worker, r, we recommend canceling this type of coverage . Work in a warehouse around machinery? It could be very valuable insurance .

## Short-Term Disability Coverage

This often only pays for a few weeks , months , or sometimes up to a year. r. This provides money so that you have income coming in while you aren ' t working .

## Long-Term Disability Coverage

This type of insurance typically won ' t begin paying benefits for a long time (sometimes over a year after you ' ve suffered a disability that prevents you from working) . However, r, this coverage is designed to bring in money for an extended period , often until age 65 .

## Social Security Disability (SSDI)

You won ' t find this on your benefits sheet . Some people may ask , " Well, l, isn 't 't social security disability available? I 'l 'll just use that. " Social Security pays benefits to people who can ' t work because they have a medical condition that ' s expected to last at least one year or result in death . Federal law requires this very strict definition of disability. y.

In addition to meeting our definition of disability, y, individuals must have worked long enough—and recently enough—under Social Security to qualify for SSDI benefits . While some programs give money to people with partial disability or short-term disability, y, Social Security does not .

SSDI is funded through payroll taxes . Recipients have worked for years and have contributed to the Social Security trust fund in the form of Social Security taxes— received under either the Federal Insurance Contributions Act for employees or the SelffEmployment Contributions Act for the self-employed . These taxes translate into Social Security " credits . " Qualified dependents of a disabled worker may also receive benefits even though they may not have worked .

## About SSDI "Credits"

In order to qualify for SSDI , you must have accumulated at least 40 " credits " over your working lifetime . As of 2026 , you earn one " credit " for each $1 , 890 you earn in covered employment (or self-employment) , up to a maximum of 4 credits per year ($7 , 560 or more in earnings) . Note: each year, r, the value of each "c "credit" is subject to change .

## Key Considerations

Definitions matter with disability insurance . The difference between " own occupation " and " any occupation " could determine your benefit and could be the difference between having a worthwhile policy and a horrible one .

Accidental death and dismemberment insurance is valuable for only a small subset of the population , a . k . a . NOT most people with office jobs , so only add this insurance if you 'r 're vulnerable to losing an appendage, e, eye, e, or other covered part of your body. y.

Short -term disability is interesting . You need it until you have an emergency fund in place . For example , if your short-term disability coverage lasts six months , when you have six months of expenses in the bank , you can drop this insurance .

Long-term disability coverage is important , and you should have some if you aren ' t already financially independent . However, r, if this is a workplace benefit , you need to be careful and ask an important question:

section_heading: ## "Is this policy a pre-tax or after-tax benefit?"
text:
## "Is this policy a pre-tax or after-tax benefit?"

It ' s a pre -tax benefit if your company covers the premium cost . It ' s after -tax if you ' re covering the cost . If, f, for some reason , you ' re given the choice , always choose " after tax . "

Why is this important? If the policy includes an " after -tax " benefit , that means you won ' t have any tax withheld from your payments , and you also won ' t be responsible for paying tax on the benefit later. r. It ' s tax -free . That means the amount of coverage you sign up for is exactly the same as the amount available to spend . However, r, if the policy is " pre -tax , " you ' ll need to purchase enough to account for the fact that this benefit will be taxed .

## Life Insurance

## What It Is

Buckle up , things are about to get a little morbid , but with good reason . Life Insurance provides a financial benefit to your beneficiary upon your death . Hopefully, y, it was peaceful and you were 110 years old … but we all know that sometimes this happens far too soon . We need to be ready. y.

## Why You Should Care

Life insurance can help a spouse or other beneficiary pay for childcare , mortgage , or any general living expenses when you pass away .

## Key Definitions

Underwriting Most basic life insurance through work does not include underwriting , which means that they ' ll ask you a series of medical questions and possibly ask you to comply with a physical (technician or doctor provided by them) . Because of the underwriting waiver, r, you may be able to score some life insurance through your company even if you have significant health issues .

Face Amount The amount of life insurance your beneficiaries will receive when you pass away .

Premium While in other areas of your life , this means a better experience (like premium seats or premium services) , the premium in the world of life insurance is simply the amount that you pay into the policy . This may go completely toward keeping the policy in force , but may also go toward cash value or fees for riders and other policy charges .

Cash Value Only rarely found in workplace life insurance policies , this money accumulates inside the policy . It generally garners a return and can be used by the policy owner to either stop paying premiums later or to spend on outside activities .

Capital Needs Analysis An analysis that financial advisors will use to determine how much life insurance a client needs , based on the capital they ' ll need to pay off debt , provide emergency funds , and achieve goals for remaining beneficiaries .

Human Life Value The sum of money a person would have been expected to earn over their lifetime , given reasonable expectations . This amount is often reduced to a present value for lawsuit settlements and to determine the insurance needed to continue payouts to remaining family members , resembling the insured ' s income stream .

Suicide Exclusion Speaking of morbid , let ' s go there . Some people purchase life insurance because they plan on taking their own lives . One of the most common exclusions for life insurance payouts is because an insured person passed away at their own hand within the first two years of the policy (this is usually the exclusion period for suicide clauses) .

section_heading: ## Key Considerations - Taxes and " How Much?"
text:
## Key Considerations - Taxes and " How Much?"

Let ' s get the little tax one out of the way:

## Premiums paid by the employer above $50,000 of coverage are taxable to the employee .

So , if your employer plans to stuff an insurance policy with money on your behalf, f, that ' s great … but the IRS will still take a cut .

The BIGGER (by far) consideration about life insurance for most of us is , "H "How much do you need? " This is a more complicated question .

Some publications recommend using a rule of thumb based on your age and dependents . While that may land you in the ballpark , it ' s easy enough to do this the right way .

There are generally two numbers you need to know: your " Capital Need " and your " Human Life Value . "

Calculating the capital need isn ' t simple , but because it ' s thorough , you ' ll feel comfortable with the amount of life insurance you ' re purchasing .

1. List all debts .
2. List any shortage in your emergency fund .
3. List the amount of money you need today (present value) that , at a reasonable return , would meet your goals (retirement for survivors , education expenses for children , etc . ) .

Avoid this a common mistake when thinking about "h "how much you ' ll need: " Even a well -calculated capital needs analysis can fall short if you don ' t account for inflation . A payout that feels like " plenty " today may not stretch as far in 10 – 20 years .

- 👉Pro tip: Use a conservative estimate (3–4% annual inflation) when running your calculations or when choosing how long to keep a policy .

This is your " Capital Need " over and above the amount you currently have . Minus any insurances you already have in force , this is the amount of life insurance you ' ll need , minimum . The other half of the equation you ' ll need is your " Human Life Value . " How much money would you earn for the rest of your life if you lived fully?

My favorite place to find human life value quickly is lifehappens . org , a non-profit site run by the insurance industry to educate about insurance and how it really works . Here ' s their calculator. r.

section_heading: ## Key Considerations - Taxes and " How Much?"
text: Both numbers have problems: a Capital Needs analysis is a number that blows in the wind . It literally changes daily! Who knows what this number will be five years from now? However, r, the number is small , and if you continue to accumulate assets , it will become smaller daily . Human Life Value is problematic because for most of us , what you earn isn 't 't the same as what you spend . Buying life insurance purely to replace income isn ' t as efficient as beginning with your goals and working backward to ensure they are secure , even if you pass away .

section_heading: ## So, which number do you use?
text:
## So, which number do you use?

We treated them like a field goal when I was a financial planner. r. Do you want to be extra safe and aren ' t concerned that it will cost you a lot of money? Move toward human life value . Need lots of money for goals , and you don ' t want to " risk it " with insurance , but your goal is to barely have enough? Skew toward the capital needs analysis number. r.

## Know What You Need

What will the financial strain be for your family if you ' re no longer bringing income in? Think: mortgage/rent, medical/insurance, e, childcare, e, food, d, transportation , education expenses, etc . Life is fleeting, g, but some bills can go on forever!

section_heading: ## Bottom Line - Re-evaluate this number frequently
text:
## Bottom Line - Re-evaluate this number frequently

Not all coverage needs to last forever. r. If your biggest concern is getting the kids through college or paying off the mortgage , these numbers will decrease over time if you ' re also saving toward these goals . Also , every year you live is a full year ' s worth of death benefit you no longer need .

You also may want to supplement any policy through work with insurance outside , in case you lose your job or decide it ' s time to move on . In this case , if you ' re just trying to take care of dependents , a 20 -year term might be enough . If your family will rely on your income indefinitely (or you want to leave a legacy) , a permanent policy could make sense .


👉Ask yourself: " When will my dependents be financially independent? " .

## Retirement Plans

## What It Is

Savings plan for retirement . This could be a 401(k) , 403(b) , 457(b) , SIMPLE IRA or other various accounts .

## Why You Should Care

These are tax -deferred accounts that allow employees to save and invest for retirement now .

## Key Definitions

Matching Contributions This is the amount of money that the company will contribute to your account if you participate (and sometimes even if you do not!) . If you must participate to receive the matching contribution , it will usually be expressed in terms of a percentage: " We match $ . 50 of every dollar you contribute, e, up to 6 percent of your salary. y. "

If a match is offered , take this free money by investing at least the amount that gives you the match! It ' s free cash . Take it .

Roth This contribution option means your contribution has no special status as it ' s contributed . You will use " after -tax dollars " just like you ' d use at the grocery store or gas pump . These dollars , however, r, will be tax -sheltered the entire time they stay in the account and they also exit the plan tax -free as long as you follow the rules for your plan .

Pre-Tax This contribution option gives you a tax break upfront because none of the taxes are applied to your paycheck before you contribute . This could mean a 30% or higher tax savings off the top , allowing you to save more money into the plan!

So , why is " pre -tax " not our first choice? Great question , person we made up for this question .

After-Tax This option gives you ZERO tax benefits and , in many cases , creates an accounting MESS when you retire . Do NOT choose this option unless you ' re working with a professional who advises you to do so .

In a future iteration of this guide , we ' ll share one advanced application where after-tax works … it ' s called a " mega back door Roth IRA . " However, r, this is outside of the scope of choosing great benefit options , so currently isn ' t in the guide .

## Key Considerations

All these plans are slightly different , but basically, y, employees cannot access the account without penalty until age 55 or 59½, depending on the type of withdrawal . Consult your CPA for specifics to your situation .

If it ' s offered , and , if at all possible , try to use the Roth option . This may not be the right advice for everyone , but our bias is (and rightly so) toward choosing the Roth . On our podcast , tax expert , Ed Slott , echoed our position . If you choose the Roth option, n, every dollar of that money will be yours later. r. If you choose " pre -tax , " you ' re splitting the money with your Uncle Sam in Washington . (As Ed jokes, s, "H "He isn 't 't even really your uncle! ") ")

Do you place the maximum allowed investment in your retirement plan? If so , AND if you receive a match , it ' s critical that you understand how the match is applied by your employer. r. Do they add the match based on your total investment over the year OR do they match on an ongoing basis?


What ' s the big deal? Here ' s an example:

Stacking Benjamins matches Doug 's 's paycheck, k, adding fifty cents for every dollar he invests up to six percent of his total pay. y. Each paycheck, k, Stacking Benjamins places their portion into the retirement plan ONLY when Doug adds his portion . Because Doug shovels the maximum amount allowable into his 401k by September, r, that also caps the amount that his employer adds . Because of this, s, Doug misses out on free money. y.

The question to ask/what you need to know: The phrase you need to know is " true up . " Does the company ensure that , no matter how quickly the account is funded , they place their portion based on the total amount and not just on the paycheck amount?

If the company doesn ' t true up your retirement matching , you should level out your contributions so that you ' re adding money to the fund all year long (and getting the entire " free money " from your company) .

## Childcare

## What It Is

Somebody 's 's gotta watch the kids , and it 's 's not you , because you ' re out making Benjamins! Some companies provide financial assistance for childcare expenses in the form of a direct payment or a reimbursement for expenses . Some extra-super-fancy employers offer on-site daycare .

## Why You Should Care

Childcare is expensive (as H-E-double-hockeysticks!) and can turn your life literally upside down if not carefully planned for. r. Remember the chaos inflicted on working parents by the pandemic? Having all or a portion of childcare paid for means more money in your pocket . Studies have also shown that employee turnover and absence are reduced when childcare is paid for. r.

## Key Considerations

Some employers may only offer a partial reimbursement for off-site care .

Legally, y, the most an employer may allow employees as an FSA dependent care is $7 , 500 (this is up from $5 , 000 pre -OBBB) . This means strategically-minded Stackers may place aside in an FSA up to this amount and then receive childcare tax free up to this amount . If you have dependents and are NOT taking advantagoe of this benefit , you ' re losing out on potentially LOTS of money:

Hypothetical person: 25% tax bracket , 4 . 5% state tax . By saving into the FSA you ' ll avoid $2 , 250 of potential taxes!

Check with your HR team for specifics .

## Adoption Assistance

## What it is

Financial assistance related to direct and indirect expenses incurred for adopting a child .

## Why You Should Care

Adoption can cost upwards of $70 , 000 in the U . S . ! Holy pile of money, y, Batman! Financial and non -financial (time off to travel for adoption if needed) assistance is a significant benefit for families looking to adopt .

## Key Considerations

Some companies may require an employee to be with the company for a certain amount of time before benefits are available or may require to be under the company health insurance plan to be eligible .

## Investing (Employee Stock Purchase)

## What It Is

Some benefit plans allow you to add money to investments outside of your retirement plan .

## Why You Should Care

Many companies incentivize you to invest in programs they operate by either giving you matching gifts or preferential treatment .

## Key Definitions

Employee Stock Purchase (ESPP) This is a special program that allows you to set aside money directly from your paycheck to purchase shares of your publicly traded company, y, often at a discount .

Capital Gains This refers to the tax you ' ll pay when you sell your shares . You may incur a capital loss if your shares drop in value .

## Key Considerations

ESPP plans allow you to share in your employer ' s stock ownership , buying shares through payroll withdrawals or some other means . This is often a great way to accumulate dollars , but with a few caveats .

First , it ' s never a great idea to load up on one company ' s stock . Even worse , for this particular company, y, your paycheck depends on this same company . Imagine if something horrible happens . Your paycheck AND your net worth could be gone in one swoop! So , ESPP plans are a great way to accumulate dollars , but not by loading up on the shares . You ' ll need a plan to diversify .

An additional benefit of these plans is that you often receive a discount on shares purchased . This makes shares even more attractive . Imagine if you receive a ten percent discount on your stock: all the company needs to do is find a way not to lose money, y, and you ' ve achieved the same return that the stock market has averaged over long periods , all without any stock market risk .

This brings up a cautionary point . You need to be honest about how your company is faring . Follow online what outsiders think . Is there a chance that your company could go bankrupt? If so , you ' ll be out the value of every dollar you place into this account . Avoiding this risk is the main reason we recommend using index funds and diversified investments in your portfolio . There is a significant risk in owning a single company, y, and you don ' t want to be left with nothing .

You should learn ahead of time if you ' ll have any restrictions on when or how you can sell your shares . If you ' re a company insider (this is an official term and your employer will let you know if you are one) , there may be blackout dates when you cannot sell . This may materially affect your plan .

Finally, y, you will pay tax on the shares you own when you sell . For this reason , we like waiting until your shares enjoy long-term capital gains treatment . This means (according to current rules) , you must hold your shares for one year before selling .

Based on the above , here is a simple , conservative ESPP strategy . Modify this to fit your situation .

- 1 . Purchase shares through payroll deduction . Buy as many as possible based on your financial plan and your employer ' s health .
- 2 . Hold shares for one year. r.
- 3 . Each year, r, sell the shares you purchased over one year ago and diversify these funds based on your goals .

In this way, y, you ' re avoiding short-term capital gains taxes and you ' re staying diversified while still always holding some of your employer ' s stock

## Eye Care and Vision Coverage

## What It Is:

Employer-provided benefits that cover routine eye exams , prescription glasses or contacts , and sometimes discounts on corrective surgeries like LASIK— K— for employees and , in some cases , their families .

## Why You Should Care:

The cost of a comprehensive eye exam averages $200 , while a pair of prescription glasses can set you back $300 or more . For families , these costs multiply quickly, y, especially with children needing updated prescriptions as they grow . If you or a family member needs contacts , expect an additional $150 to $1 , 500 annually . Vision problems can also impact overall productivity and well-being .

## Key Considerations:

- Vision benefits typically cover one eye exam per year, r, a set dollar amount for frames or lenses , and a portion of the cost for contacts .
- Family coverage may include spouses and dependents , but the extent of coverage varies by plan .
- Some vision plans may subsidize vision therapy for conditions like lazy eye (amblyopia) or convergence insufficiency, y, but coverage is limited .
- For medical eye conditions such as glaucoma , cataracts , or macular degeneration , treatment is usually covered under your health insurance plan rather than vision care benefits .
- Many vision plans operate on a network basis—out-of-network providers may require higher out -offpocket expenses .
- LASIK or other corrective procedures are often covered at a discounted rate or partially reimbursed , but not always fully covered .
- Some companies (generally smaller, r, but not always) include vision coverage , but it may be better to just pay cash . Find a local provider (preferably locally-owned) and find out the difference between out -offpocket against this coverage option .

## Paid Time Off (PTO)

## What It Is

Paid time away from the office for employees (i . e . Slams laptop shut until … " )

## Why You Should Care

Work -life balance is important , and we all need time away from the office (and our electronic leashes , er, r, phones and computers) to recharge .

## Key Considerations

Some companies gradually increase PTO time with each year of employment . Others have adopted an unlimited PTO policy, y, giving employees more flexibility when it comes to time off. f.


## Health Related Expense Reimbursement

## What It Is

Reimbursement or direct payment for fitness-related expenses . This can include getting " up in the gym just working on your fitness " with memberships , equipment , or classes .

## Why You Should Care

Selffcare is important and the cost of fancy gym memberships with saunas , pools and massages can add up! Use this benefit to keep up with your overall health .

## Key Considerations

Most employers set a maximum reimbursement limit , and you may have to pay upfront and be reimbursed at a later time .


## Student Loan Repayment Assistance

## What It Is

Employer payments toward employee 's 's student loans .

## Why You Should Care

The average student loan balance is nearing $40 , 000 , with interest rates anywhere from 3% to 12% . That ' s a whole lotta dough .

## Key Considerations

Student loan payments from employers are excluded in the employee ' s taxable income up to $5 , 250 . The new OBBB legislation in 2025 made this exemption " permanent . " .

## Tuition Reimbursement

## What It Is

Payment for an employee 's 's higher education tuition . This could be a certification or a higher education degree .

## Why You Should Care

Learn ya something! This is a great way for employees to continue their education while remaining an employee at the company .

## Key Considerations

Some employers require the employee to remain at the company for a certain time after receiving their degree or limit assistance to degrees in the employee ' s current line of work . Most often , this applies to tuition only and does not include books or other fees

section_heading: ## Mental Health and Wellness Benefits (More Than Just a Day Off)
text:
## Mental Health and Wellness Benefits (More Than Just a Day Off)

## What It Is

Support for your emotional and psychological well-being , ranging from therapy and counseling to stress-reducing apps and wellness stipends . Post -pandemic , many companies expanded mental health offerings—so if it ' s been a while since you checked your benefits , now ' s a good time .

## Why You Should Care

Burnout , anxiety, y, and stress don ' t punch a clock—and neither should your support system . Just like dental or vision coverage , mental health benefits can prevent small issues from becoming big (and expensive) ones . From free therapy sessions to meditation apps , these perks aren ' t just fluff—they ' re foundational to your long-term health and productivity . And let ' s be real: access to care can be a game -changer in both your personal life and career. r.

## Key Considerations

## What 's 's Offered?

Mental health benefits can include:

- EAPs (Employee Assistance Programs): Short-term counseling , usually free and confidential
- Therapy Coverage: Insurance may cover a number of sessions per year (some fully, y, some with copays)
- Teletherapy Access: Options like Talkspace or BetterHelp might be included
- Wellness Apps: Look for subscriptions to Calm , Headspace , or other mindfulness tools
- Crisis Support: Some plans offer 24/7 hotlines or chat support
- Wellness Allowances: Stipends or reimbursements for gym memberships , massages , or wellness classes

## Navigating Mental Health Benefits

Here ' s a simple flow for accessing care (print this , screenshot it , or tattoo it on your forearm—whatever helps):


## Bottom Line

Mental health support is no longer a nice-to-have—it ' s a core benefit . Whether you ' re dealing with daily stress or deeper challenges , your benefits might offer more than you realize . Ask the right questions , use what ' s available , and don ' t leave peace of mind on the table .

## Legal/Estate Planning Assistance

## What It Is

Legal or estate planning assistance with in-house or contracted attorneys .

## Why You Should Care

We don ' t care how many times you ' ve watched Legally Blonde , sometimes you need to call in a professional . (What , like it ' s hard?!) This benefit is an easy and convenient way to get legal help if needed . This can include estate planning or general legal advice .

## Key Considerations

Typically, y, you must pay monthly or annually for this benefit .

## Professional Development & Conferences

## What It Is

Some employers cover costs for professional development , continuing education , or conferences . This can include tuition toward certifications , training courses , or even travel and lodging for industry events .

## Why You Should Care

Sharpening your skills means bigger opportunities (and bigger paychecks) down the road . Why pay out -offpocket for the conference you actually want to attend when your employer might foot the bill? Bonus: stacking these benefits with tuition reimbursement or student loan repayment can supercharge your career growth without draining your wallet .

## Key Considerations

- Budget your continuing education funds toward certifications or licenses that matter most in your field .
- Stack benefits: some companies allow you to combine tuition reimbursement with professional development stipends for a double win .
- Many companies cap what they ' ll pay or require you to stay a certain period afterward — always check the fine print .
- Don ' t overlook " soft " benefits: networking , mentorship , and recognition at conferences can be just as valuable as the knowledge .

## Mom's Monster Resource List

## Choosing Benefits (in general)

Stop Losing Money On Your Company Benefits Podcast Episode The Ultimate Guide to Workplace Benefits (with Amanda Lannert) Podcast Episode

## Choosing Insurances (in general)

Our Primer on Making Better Insurance Decisions (SB1504) Podcast Episode Insurance 101: The Different Types , and Who Needs What Podcast Episode

## Health Insurance and HSA/FSA

Listen to these podcast episodes to familiarize yourself with the various options:

SB1532 " Let ' s Save a Bundle on Taxes " During our discussion with Hannah Cole we discuss how understanding your HSA and FSA can pay huge dividends . Podcast Episode

SB1118 " Slick Deals on Black Friday " During a break from our discussion on Black Friday deals , we chat with Shawna Haufman from the FSA Store about optimizing your FSA . Podcast Episode

Leadership Lessons from Walt Disney In the middle of this episode we take a break to chat with Leo Atcheson , a researcher for Morningstar, r, comparing the different available HSA accounts . Podcast Episode

- Compare premiums , deductible and out - of- f- pocket max for each plan

- Would you benefit from a high deductible plan and HSA?

## Life Insurance

- Get educated on how life insurance works . Listen to these podcast episodes to start:

Life Insurance Starter Kit (Our Top 5) How To Avoid Getting Scammed on Life Insurance

- Ask your employer what policies are available .

- Determine how much you need . Check out this life insurance calculator from LifeHappens and this one from Bankrate .

- Determine your beneficiary . This person will receive the death benefit when you pass away . Who relies on your income? Already named a beneficiary? Make sure you still want that person listed .

- Do you work in dangerous conditions or with heavy machinery? Accidental Death & Dismemberment (AD&D) coverage may be right for you . Work in front of a computer all day? It ' s generally not necessary and you can cut this benefit , saving the money for more important options .

## Adoption Assistance

Resource: Dave Thomas Foundation - Workplace Adoption Assistance Toolkit

## Paid Time Off (PTO)

- Find out if your vacation and sick time are accrued separately or lumped together. r.

- Find out if your unused PTO rolls over to the next calendar year. r.

- Ask if you are awarded the full PTO time at the beginning of the year or if you accrue it over time .

- Plan a meeting with everyone involved to set up your PTO calendar. r. This way everyone is clear and on board with the yearly plan .

## Legal/Estate Planning Assistance

What ' s the Purpose of a Will? Why Would I Need a Trust? (Your Estate Questions Answered with Attorney Tim Semro) SB1559 Podcast Episode

## Student Loan Repayment and Tuition Assistance

- Find out if your employer offers student loan repayments for employees— and what the conditions are .

- Clarify the maximum payment that the employer will make .

- Understand what types of payments the employer makes - direct or discretionary .

## Retirement Plan

- Clarify what type(s) of employer-sponsored retirement plans are available and how to enroll .

- Understand if there is an employer match , how much , and what the vesting schedule is .

- Understand your investment options , who is the plan custodian , and what the all - in fees are .

- Ask at what age you are able to take distributions from the plan . Do you have to be separated from service?

- Listen to these podcast episodes to learn more about retirement plan options:

- https://www . stackingbenjamins . com /top-401k-mistakes-1211/
- https://www . stackingbenjamins . com /the -roth -401k -cinderella -offyour -financial -plan -and -your -letters/
- Is a Simple 401(k) Mistake Costing You Millions

## Health Care Options

Listen to expert Scott Heiser discuss how to lower the cost of your healthcare on this podcast episode:

https://www . stackingbenjamins . com /health -and -finances -with -scott -heiser/

Watch Scott Heiser answer your healthcare questions on this YouTube video:

Scott Heiser Joins Us! (youtube . com)

HSA/FSA Planning

Listen to Joe discuss HSAs with Leo Atchinson from Morningstar. r. com on this podcast episode:

Leadership Lessons from Walt Disney (with Pat Williams) » The Stacking Benjamins Show

Read Morningstar ' s most current " best of " HSA list:

The Best HSA Accounts and Providers Guide | Morningstar

## Eye Care and Vision Coverage

Watch Delray Gust share insider tips on how to maximize your optical insurance on this YouTube video:

How to Maximize Your Optical Insurance with Insider Tips | LIVE with Delray Gust

## Looking For More Help?

Still not feeling confident in your benefit options?

For simple clarifications , write to us with your questions .

Email us at joe@stackingbenjamins . com .

If you ' d like to walk through it with Joe , you can buy an hour of his time .

Visit www . stackingbenjamins . com /getjoestake for more information .

If you need a financial plan and an advisor , set up a planning meeting with OG .

section_heading: ## Mom's Benefits Planning Checklist For You
text:
## Mom's Benefits Planning Checklist For You

"You can't remember everything, honey. Here 's 's the important stuff. "

Some of you will want to start here and see what you understand . Then , you can refer BACK to the guide and fill in the gaps in your knowledge . Others will read the guide and then use it when asking questions of your HR guide or HR management team .

Here is a comprehensive checklist of EVERYTHING we reviewed in the guide and what to ask your employer:

## Health Insurance

- What health plans do you offer?

- Is there a high-deductible health insurance option?

- What are the premiums (the amount you will pay each month) for each plan?

- What physicians are “ in - network ” ?

- Will my spouse and dependents be covered?hat health plans do you offer?

- Do you offer vision and dental insurance? What does it cover? Can my spouse and dependents get coverage , too?

## HSA/FSA

- Do you offer an FSA?

- Do you offer a matching contribution?

- Do you offer an HSA?

- Are there investment options available?

- Do you match HSA contributions?

## Dental Insurance

- What exactly is covered with my policy?

- Which dentists are in my network?

- How much will it cost me vs . paying out of pocket?

- What is my deductible?

- Are there any exclusions, waiting periods, or procedures which are not covered?

- What ’ s my deductible and/or copay?

## Disability Insurance

- Do you offer short-term and/or long-term disability?

- What percentage of my pay does it cover?

- How much will it cost me?

- How long is the benefit period?

- What is the insurance plan ' s definition of disability?

- Are the disability benefits taxed as income when paid?

## Life Insurance

- What is the minimum and maximum insurance benefit you provide?

- Do you provide coverage for my spouse?

- Do you offer coverage for my children?

- Is the policy portable (can you take the policy with you when you leave the job)?

## Retirement Plans

- What type of retirement account do you offer?

- Do you offer a company match? If so , what is the match? SHOW ME THE MONEY!

- Is there a Roth option?

- What are my investment choices?

- Is there a vesting schedule?

- Where is the account held?

- Is there a brokerage window within the plan?

- What are the all - in plan fees? (Maybe add this)

- Are new hires automatically enrolled? If so , at what percentage of salary?

- In - service withdrawal/distribution availability?

- Are loans available on my plan balance?

## Adoption Assistance

- Does this benefit include paid time off to travel if you need to go out of the area to pick up your precious bundle of joy?


## Paid Time Off (PTO)

- Can I (stop , drop and) roll over my PTO time year after year?

- How many vacation and sick days are awarded each year?

- Are sick and vacation time separate?

- When I leave this job , do I get paid out for unused vacation and sick time?

- Is this unlimited paid time off REALLY unlimited?

- If I have an emergency or would like a sabbatical , is there an opportunity for UNPAID leave?

- Health Related Expense Reimbursement

- What is the annual limit for reimbursement?

- What expenses does this include?

- Student Loan Repayment Assistance

- How much do you pay toward student loans?

- What is the maximum you will pay?

## Tuition Reimbursement

- What degrees/programs will you reimburse?

- Do I have to finish the entire degree before reimbursement or will I be reimbursed after each semester?

- Do you require me to stay at the company for a set amount of time after receiving the degree?

## Legal/Estate Planning Assistance

- What legal or estate planning services do you offer?

- What is the cost for it?


## Your 30-Minute Benefits Decision Worksheet

(Because benefits decisions should take less time than watching an episode of your favorite show . )

Grab your benefits packet, a pen, and maybe a coffee strong enough to wake Mom upstairs. Work through these steps in order and you'll secure the most valuable benefits first.

## Step 1 — Secure the Free Money

## Retirement Match

Employer Match Formula:

My Salary:

Percentage I Need to Contribute to Get Full Match:

My Contribution Per Year:

Employer Contribution (Free Money):

- I am contributing enough to receive the full employer match

Employer HSA Contribution (if available)

Employer Annual HSA Contribution:

Do I qualify for the HSA plan?

- Yes

- No


My Planned HSA Contribution:

- I am capturing the employer HSA contribution .

## Step 2 — Protect My Income

## Long-Term Disability Insurance

Coverage replaces \_\_\_\_\_\_% of income.

Monthly cost:

I have long-term disability coverage.

- Yes

- No

## Life Insurance

Do people depend on my income?

- Yes

- No

Current life insurance coverage through work:

Estimated life insurance needed (if applicable):

- I reviewed my beneficiaries .

## Step 3 — Choose My Health Plan

## List the plans your employer offers .

Which plan fits my situation best?

- PPO (higher premium , more flexibility)

- HDHP + HSA (lower premium , higher deductible)

Why I chose this plan:

## Step 4 — Capture the Tax Advantages

Health Savings Account (HSA)

Maximum allowed contribution:

My planned contribution:

Am I investing the HSA funds?

- Yes

- No

- Later


## Flexible Spending Account (FSA)

Estimated medical expenses this year:

Planned FSA contribution:

- Medical FSA

- Dependent Care FSA

- Later

Estimated tax savings:

## Step 5 — Evaluate the Extra Benefits

Check any perks your employer offers that you may want to use.

- Tuition reimbursement

- Student loan repayment

- Wellness reimbursement

- Mental health services

- Legal assistance

- Professional development

- Childcare support

- Other:

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Benefits I plan to use this year:

## Final Check (Don't Skip This)

Before you close the benefits portal, confirm the following:

- I ’ m getting the full retirement match

- I captured any employer HSA contribution

- I have income protection (disability / life insurance if needed)

- I chose the health plan that fits my situation

- I used available tax - advantaged accounts

- I updated my beneficiaries

## Basement Wisdom

Most employees spend more time choosing a Netflix show than they do choosing their benefits .

But if you completed this worksheet , you just made decisions that could affect hundreds of thousands of dollars over your career. r.

Not bad for half an hour in the basement .

Now go stack some Benjamins. s.

section_heading: ## The SB Workplace Benefits & HR Calendar
text:
## The SB Workplace Benefits & HR Calendar

## A Year-at-a-Glance Guide for Employers Who Want Fewer Surprises

## What This Is (and Why You Want It)

Most workplace benefits problems do not start with bad intentions . They start with missed timing .

Open enrollment windows close . Notices go out late . A form that should have been filed quietly turns into a letter from a government agency that ruins your week .

This calendar is a standalone companion to the SB Choosing Workplace Benefits Guide . It pulls the most important benefits , compliance , and people-management moments into one simple , predictable rhythm so you can plan ahead instead of reacting .

This is not legal advice and it is not an exhaustive compliance list . It is a practical , real -world cadence used by good employers who want to take care of their people without turning HR into a second full -time job .

Use it as a reminder system , not a rulebook .

## January

## Start the year clean

- Review employee classifications (full-time vs part-time , exempt vs non -exempt) .
- Confirm benefit elections are correctly reflected in payroll .
- Distribute W -2s to employees and 1095-Cs if applicable .

Why this matters: Errors early in the year tend to get repeated 26 times .

## February

## Compliance catch-up month

- Confirm all prior-year payroll and benefits reporting is complete .
- Review health plan eligibility rules and waiting periods .
- Begin gathering information for Form 5500 if you sponsor a retirement plan .

This is also a good month for an HR policy refresh before habits set in .

## March

## Planning before busy season

- Review retirement plan contributions and employer match calculations .
- Check ACA affordability and minimum value thresholds if applicable .
- Start thinking about benefit changes you may want later this year. r.

Nothing urgent yet . That is the point .

## April

## First -quarter check-in

- Audit payroll deductions for benefits accuracy .
- Review PTO balances and usage patterns .
- Make sure new hires from Q1 completed enrollment correctly .

Quiet fixes now prevent loud problems later. r.

## May

## Mid -year people planning

- Review employee turnover and hiring plans .
- Evaluate whether benefits still match your workforce size and needs .
- Begin informal conversations with brokers or advisors if changes are coming .

This is strategy season , not decision season .

## June

## Mid -year compliance check

- Review summary plan descriptions (SPDs) for accuracy .
- Confirm COBRA processes are working as intended .
- Prepare to file Form 5500 (due in July) .

If you have a problem , you still have time to fix it .

## July

## Key filing deadline

- Form 5500 due for calendar -year retirement plans .
- Review retirement plan participation and contribution limits .

Missing this deadline creates penalties that add up quickly .

## August

## Open enrollment preparation begins

- Finalize benefit options for the coming year. r.
- Review renewal proposals from carriers .
- Decide what is changing and what is staying the same .

Clarity now makes open enrollment calmer later. r.

## September

## Communication month

- Prepare open enrollment materials .
- Train managers on how to answer basic benefits questions .
- Schedule enrollment meetings or webinars .

Employees handle change better when they see it coming .

## October

## Open enrollment (for most plans)

- Conduct open enrollment meetings .
- Collect employee elections and waivers .
- Confirm dependent documentation if required .

Accuracy matters more than speed .

## November

## Confirmation and cleanup

- Review enrollment results for errors or missing elections .
- Finalize payroll changes for the new plan year. r.
- Distribute required notices (Summary of Benefits and Coverage , etc . ) .

This is where most benefits mistakes happen . Slow down .

## December

## Year-end wrap-up

- Confirm all benefit changes are ready for January payroll .
- Review compliance notices and record retention .
- Document what worked and what did not for next year. r.

A short review now saves long meetings later. r.

## Final Thought

Good workplace benefits are not about having the most options or the fanciest plans .

They are about timing , communication , and follow -through .

Use this calendar to create a steady rhythm for your business , your people , and your peace of mind . When HR runs on a calendar instead of adrenaline , everybody wins .

## SAUNSL STACKED STACKED

Your Super-Serious Guide to Modern Money Management

JOE

SAUL-SEHY Creator and Cohost of Stacking Benjamins

EMILY GUY BIRKEN

Author of The 5 Years Before You Retire

KEI

SKE


##

##

## Introduction

## Wherein Joe Explains Why Us and Why This Book

In this time of health and financial uncertainty, there are two things I wish I'd known earlier:

1. Start with the basics.
2. Back  when  I  struggled  with  money,  I  was  always  searching  for shortcuts, easy fixes, and ways to cover up mistakes I'd made along the way-and I've made more than my fair share. A better path (and the only one I know now that works, after too much stumbling around) is to focus on building your house one brick at a time, just like the pig in that timeless tale.
2. Laughter is more important than ever.
4. If I hadn't had the ability to laugh through my mistakes, I wouldn't be here  to  roll  out  the  red  carpet  for  you  now.  If  you're  going  to  grow, mistakes quickly and work past them. The ability to shake your head, stand back up, laugh a little, and keep moving will allow you to stay
5. mistakes  are  going  to  happen.  Your  goal  should  be  to  make  those positive and focused on the prize. With these two universal truths in mind, it's become clear to me that there's a big need for a financial book that can make you laugh as you read. Sure, countless financial books already hit store shelves year after year.  (My  co-writer,  Emily,  the  brains  of  this  operation  and  a  former bookseller, wants me to clarify that I understand that financial books, like all other books, are placed lovingly on store shelves  by

professional  English  majors  who  understand  the  importance  of  the printed  word  and  they  do  not  go  about  throwing  them  willy-nilly  at shelves like they're flying salmon at Pike Place Fish Market. She also points out that the number of financial books released each year is, in fact, countable, and that my description of them as 'countless' shows laziness in my math.) My point, already nearly derailed, is that there are lots of financial books  out  there.  But  in  good  times  or  bad,  something  important  is missing  from  the  genre.  I  know  about  this  gap  because  nearly  every author of the 'top' titles each year asks to come talk about it on my podcast. In the course of creating and cohosting the Stacking Benjamins podcast,  I  construct  three  shows  per  week,  forty-seven  weeks  of  the year. With that many shows to record, I ask a number of experts to sidle on up to the microphone. The majority of the world's top personal finance guests have ducked their  head  and  climbed  down  the  stairs  to  my  mom's  basement  to discuss  their  latest  project  (you'll  hear  from  Mom  later  because  she owns  our  podcasting  'headquarters'  and  has  plenty  of  wisdom  to share).  In  our  corner  of  the  world,  finance  and  investing  are  serious sports. The prevailing attitude among many in my cohort seems to be 'Money  only  works  if  you  take  it  seriously.  There  are  important retirement  accounts  to  fill,  and  overly  serious  bank  managers  with somber  mustaches  and  knowing  gazes  awaiting  your  next  savings deposit. There's no room for giddiness in wealth building!' Unfortunately,  treating  money  as  only  slightly  less  fun  than  a colonoscopy limits the reach of our collective voices. We  are pigeonholed  with  people  promising  wealth  without  work  and  riches beyond measure-and often for a much stiffer (and hidden) price than a free podcast. Enter Stacked . The truth is, the sport of Benjamin stacking isn't played the way you may think. As it turns out, being a big shot and being wealthy are two totally different games. We're going to teach you the real game we had to  stumble  into-and  happily  for  all  of  us,  the  real  game  is  more unicorns and rainbows and less spreadsheets and deprivation.

- Emily

So if you've ever wondered, 'Could I stack some Benjamins?' then you've come to the right book. Whether you're intimidated by money terms or can quote every line from The Big Short ,  you're  sure  to  find something helpful in this handbook. But  don't  just  take  my  word  for  it.  Emily  Guy  Birken  has  helped countless readers build their own stacks into intimidating towers, and she's agreed to help. If you've listened to the podcast, you'll know that we promise to teach you nothing. But I don't want to tarnish Emily's reputation, so it's high time to do something useful for once and make this book a fresh start: something you can actually learn from. We have no clue how far along the journey you've come, so we're going to begin, as any good journey should, at the beginning. Our goal here  is  to  stack  that  first  Benjamin-which,  frankly,  any  supervillain will tell you is the hardest part of world domination. The first section of this  book  is  titled,  well,  Stacking  Your  First  Benjamin,  and  these chapters  focus  on  the  strategies  my  friends  and  I  used  to  build  our financial foundation. We'll help you set up a budget and figure out how to save for the things you care about most. You'll learn how to increase your  income  and  nix  any  money  drains  that  keep  your  dreams  from coming true. We'll also demystify the weirdness that is debt and credit so it's easier to reach your goal of a college education, early retirement, or maybe that volcano lair you've had your eye on. The second section is called Building a Stack of Benjamins, and now that we've filled the backpack with the basics, we'll embark on the great journey of investing. Why invest? Because if you don't, you're going to need  to  save  nearly  dollar-for-dollar  the  same  amount  you'll  want  to spend in later years. If you're living a $50,000 lifestyle and planning for a twenty-year-long retirement, you're looking at saving a million bucks. Sure, you might be able to do that, but what if we taught you how to let interest do the work for you? Let's all jump on the lazy train. Less work for you and more work for your money. That's a big key to success. From there, we'll teach you how to protect your money and potential income  in  the  Holding  On  to  Benjamins  section.  That  means  we're going to focus on something we money nerds call 'risk management,' which you might know by its more dreaded name of 'insurance.' You may need insurance, but starting  off  with  such  a  loaded  word  is  like walking up to the high school bully and asking for a wedgie. Talking about insurance may be a necessary mental wedgie, but the interesting part comes from discussing how it can skew the odds in our favor. In short, we use insurance to mitigate the risks of life, kind of like realworld cheat codes. That way, a broken dishwasher can't derail our plan to conquer the world. (Muahaha!) Finally,  we'll  wrap  up  this  whole  package  with  the  sexy  stuff everybody wants to talk about, in a section we call Stacking Benjamins on Top of Your Other Benjamins. We'll wind through tall-but-true tales about advanced-level investing, mitigating the effects of those shuffling hordes  known  as  inflation  and  taxes,  and  hiring  a  financial  advisor. We'll share examples from some of the best investors we know. We're confident  you'll  be  such  a  money  nerd  by  then  that  closing  the  book will leave you with a tear in your eye. You're welcome.
Now, go join us in Chapter 1 already. A richer, funnier, and infinitely Benjaminier life awaits you.

## -Joe (and Emily!)

## PART 1 STACKING YOUR FIRST BENJAMIN

The world is full of obvious things which nobody by any chance ever observes. -SHERLOCK HOLMES


## 1

## One Magical Idea to Rule Them All

## TOOLS YOU'LL NEED:

- Paper
- Pen
- Some degree of imagination (Don't worry. It's not so much 'Walt Disney' as 'that cloud looks like Uncle Tony.')

Virtually every financial book starts with the same tired move: asking you to write out your goals. While there's nothing wrong with this strategy for financial  planning,  seeing  it  over  and  over  starts  to  feel  like  hearing  the same old pickup lines: 'Hey,  baby,  are  those  some  well-thought-out  financial  goals  in  your pocket, or are you just happy to see me?' 'Is it hot in here, or is it just your thirty-year financial plan?' 'I  like  your  money  goals.  They'd  look  great  in  a  pile  on  my  bedroom floor.' Here's the problem with goal setting: Many people mess up their plans from  the  beginning.  They  ask  the  wrong  questions,  and  that  makes  the whole process far more difficult than it needs to be. Back when Joe was a financial planner, it felt like everyone wanted to know  the  answers  to  the  following  completely  irrelevant  questions,  long before they got into the important stuff:

1. How am I doing compared to everyone I work with/live near/compete with in my fantasy baseball league/watch on

## television/etc.?

If you're not doing better than a random sampling of people who don't know they are your nemeses, then how can you win? But who cares? #Spoiler:  when  you  die,  we're  fairly  certain  there  aren't  a  bunch  of tickets you take over to the Skee-Ball prize counter to cash in after you beat everyone else at the 'I accumulated money' game. Okay,  we  can't  guarantee  that  this  doesn't  happen.  (Truthfully,  we haven't yet been to heaven, but we have been to Vermont, and people who live there never tire of telling you how close to heaven it is-and more importantly, there's no place to claim your 'I made more money than Bob from accounting' prize anywhere in Vermont.)

- What  we  can  tell  you  is  that  there  are  significant  benefits  to accumulating  'enough'  and  then  pausing  the  accumulation  game  to spend money on the things that make you happy today . People without goals  either  spend  everything  or  save  far  too  much,  depending  on which  game  they're  inclined  to  play.  People  with  goals  are  able  to balance having fun today-which brings joy to your life right nowwith making sure they'll have enough left for fun tomorrow.
2. How do I find the 'best' investments?
- The real issue is that it's impossible to define the word 'best.' What's 'best'  changes  as  you  pursue  your  goals.
